一.origin: A single horizontal stroke representing the number one and the idea of unity or a baseline. Among the simplest Chinese graphs, it is a pure ideograph rather than a picture of an object.
一.history: Stable from oracle-bone through regular script: one horizontal line with only minor stroke-style changes. The modern form is essentially unchanged from early bronze and seal styles.

parts.note: early form suggested a cut or cross-mark

七.origin: Originally a simple mark, often linked to the idea of cutting or a crossed stroke, later fixed as the numeral seven. Exact pictorial intent is debated; the modern sense is purely numerical.
七.history: Oracle and bronze forms look more like a cross-cut; seal and clerical scripts straighten into the familiar 七 shape used in regular and simplified writing.

parts.note: traditional form; pictograph of a scorpion, loaned for 'myriad'

万.origin: Traditional 萬 depicted a scorpion and was borrowed phonetically for ‘ten thousand / myriad.’ Simplified 万 keeps the numeral sense without the insect pictograph.
万.history: 萬 appears in oracle and bronze as a scorpion-like figure; seal and clerical refine it. PRC simplification replaced 萬 with the much shorter 万 (also an older variant).

丈.meaning: unit of length; elder / husband (in compounds)
丈.origin: Depicts a hand holding a measuring rod, encoding a traditional length of about ten Chinese feet. Extended to respectful terms for adult men (e.g. 丈夫).
丈.history: Bronze and seal forms show a hand-plus-staff composition; clerical and regular scripts compress it into 丈. Unchanged in simplified Chinese.

三.origin: Three horizontal strokes marking the number three, parallel to 一 and 二. A transparent counting ideograph.
三.history: Oracle through regular script keep three stacked lines; only stroke thickness and spacing vary by script style.

上.origin: An indicator stroke placed above a horizontal baseline, meaning ‘above’ or ‘up.’ Paired conceptually with 下.
上.history: Early forms are a short mark over a longer line; seal and clerical add the vertical stem of modern 上. Stable in regular and simplified script.

下.origin: The counterpart of 上: a mark placed under a horizontal line to mean ‘below’ or ‘down.’
下.history: Oracle/bronze use a short stroke under a line; later scripts develop the modern T-like 下. Unchanged by simplification.

parts.note: originally a flower calyx; borrowed for negation

不.origin: Early forms likely pictured a flower calyx or similar plant part. The graph was borrowed for the negation ‘not,’ which became its dominant meaning.
不.history: Oracle and bronze show a forked plant-like shape; seal and clerical flatten into 不. Form and negating function are stable in modern script.

与.origin: Traditional 與 shows hands joining or giving, encoding association and bestowal. Simplified 与 preserves ‘and / with / give.’
与.history: 與 is complex in seal and clerical scripts; PRC simplification yields 与. Sense of accompaniment and giving continues from classical usage.

parts.note: traditional; spindle/reel related graph loaned or extended to ‘concentrate’

专.origin: Traditional 專 is linked to a spinning implement; meaning shifted to concentrated, exclusive activity. Simplified 专 keeps ‘specialized / focused.’
专.history: 專 appears in seal/clerical with a more pictorial upper part; simplified 专 truncates strokes while keeping the core silhouette.

parts.note: often linked to an ancestral tablet or stacked offering stand

且.origin: Early graphs resemble a ritual stand or ancestral tablet. Grammatical uses (‘moreover,’ ‘temporarily’) are later extensions from a concrete ritual object.
且.history: Oracle and bronze forms are more pictorial; seal regularizes the stacked outline into 且, retained through regular and simplified script.

parts.note: tens; three tens ≈ thirty years / a generation

世.origin: Traditionally analyzed as three ‘ten’ marks (about thirty years), encoding one generation, then extended to ‘age,’ ‘world,’ and ‘era.’
世.history: Bronze/seal forms already show the linked verticals of 世; clerical and regular scripts stabilize the modern shape. Unchanged in simplified Chinese.

parts.note: traditional; wooden rack / tablet stand, extended to ‘work, estate’

业.origin: Traditional 業 pictured a notched wooden board or rack and came to mean vocation, estate, or karma. Simplified 业 keeps occupational and industrial senses.
业.history: 業 is ornate in seal script; clerical simplifies somewhat; PRC form 业 is a drastic abbreviation used in mainland orthography.

composition.formula: 木 + 日 → 東 → 东

东.origin: Traditional 東 shows the sun (日) behind a tree (木), suggesting sunrise in the east. Simplified 东 abstracts that compound.
东.history: Oracle/bronze already combine tree and sun motifs; seal and regular keep 東. Simplified Chinese replaces it with 东.

composition.formula: 糸 + 糸 → 絲 → 丝

丝.origin: Traditional 絲 doubles the silk radical 糸, a pictograph of coiled threads, meaning silk or fine filament. Simplified 丝 retains that sense.
丝.history: 糸/絲 forms track silk pictographs from bronze onward; simplified 丝 reduces stroke count while echoing two thread-like strokes.

parts.note: top stroke; sometimes linked to ‘cast off’

丢.origin: Built on 去 (‘leave / go’) with an added top stroke, conveying casting something away or losing it. A relatively late popular form for ‘discard / misplace.’
丢.history: Rare or absent in earliest corpora; becomes common in later vernacular writing. Same form in simplified and traditional contexts for this sense.

两.origin: Traditional 兩 depicts paired objects or a dual measure, hence ‘two / both,’ and also the weight unit tael. Simplified 两 keeps those meanings.
两.history: Bronze and seal show symmetric dual structure; clerical yields 兩; simplified Chinese shortens to 两.

严.origin: Traditional 嚴 is a complex graph associated with sternness and austerity (also a surname). Simplified 严 preserves ‘strict / severe.’
严.history: Seal/clerical 嚴 is multi-component; PRC simplification collapses it to 严. Semantic range of severity remains.

parts.note: older bamboo-related variant also used as classifier

个.origin: Classifier sense comes mainly from 個 (person + phonetic 固) and older 箇. Simplified 个 is a drastically shortened popular form for the general measure word.
个.history: 個/箇 dominate classical and traditional texts; vernacular and PRC orthography standardize 个 for everyday classifier use.

丫.origin: A simple pictograph of a forked stick or branch. Extended to forked hairstyles and colloquial terms for girls (丫头).
丫.history: Appears as a plain Y-shaped graph in later scripts; form is essentially identical in traditional and simplified writing.

中.origin: Typically explained as a shaft or arrow through a central target, meaning ‘middle / hit the center.’ Extended to centrality and (in compounds) China.
中.history: Oracle forms already show a vertical through a circle/box; seal and clerical yield modern 中, unchanged by simplification.

串.origin: Pictures items threaded on a cord or skewer, hence ‘to string together,’ and by extension collusion or serial action.
串.history: Seal and clerical keep the double-round-on-a-stick look; regular 串 is stable in both traditional and simplified scripts.

parts.note: traditional; hand leading an elephant — ‘to do / act’

为.origin: Traditional 為 famously shows a hand with an elephant, encoding purposeful action (‘do / make’). Grammatical ‘for / as’ and reading wéi (‘become’) are later usages; simplified 为 continues them.
为.history: Oracle/bronze preserve the elephant motif more clearly; seal and regular keep 為; mainland simplified uses 为.

parts.note: lamp stand silhouette in later analysis

主.origin: Originally a lamp with a flame on top; the bright, central light metaphorically became ‘lord,’ ‘host,’ and ‘principal.’
主.history: Early forms emphasize the flame dot; seal/clerical converge on 主. Form unchanged in simplified Chinese.

丽.origin: Traditional 麗 relates to a deer (pair of antlers / elegant animal) and came to mean beauty and attachment. Simplified 丽 keeps ‘beautiful.’
丽.history: 麗 is elaborate in seal script; regular traditional retains deer components; PRC 丽 is a shortened form.

举.origin: Traditional 舉 shows hands lifting an object, hence ‘raise,’ then ‘propose,’ ‘elect,’ or ‘deed.’ Simplified 举 preserves those senses.
举.history: Seal/clerical 舉 is multi-hand complex; simplified 举 drops many strokes while keeping a raised-hands silhouette.

parts.note: early form often linked to a curved object or breath/connection

乃.origin: An ancient pictograph (exact object debated) used classically as a copula or connective (‘then / namely / be’). Also appears as phonetic in compounds like 仍.
乃.history: Oracle and bronze shapes are curved and compact; seal yields the modern 乃 outline still used today.

parts.note: person leaning on a staff / enduring posture (traditional explanation)

久.origin: Often explained as a person supported by a stick, suggesting lingering or long duration. Core meaning is temporal length, not spatial.
久.history: Early forms are more human-like; seal and clerical abstract into 久. Stable across traditional and simplified scripts.

么.meaning: interrogative / modal particle (in 什么, 怎么)
么.origin: Traditional 麼 is phono-semantic (麻 sound + 幺). In modern Mandarin the shortened 么 marks particles in 什么, 怎么, etc. (also read má/yāo in other uses of 幺/么).
么.history: 麼 dominates older texts; vernacular and PRC orthography favor 么 for the unstressed particle.

composition.formula: 羊 + 我 → 義 → 义

义.origin: Traditional 義 combines sheep (ritual propriety) with 我, yielding ‘righteousness / duty,’ later also ‘meaning.’ Simplified 义 keeps those senses.
义.history: 義 is clear in seal and regular traditional script; simplified 义 is a cursive-derived abbreviation.

之.origin: Originally a foot stepping forward (‘to go’). Classical Chinese then grammaticalized it as a possessive/linker (‘of / ’s’), now the dominant schoolbook sense.
之.history: Oracle forms resemble a foot; seal and clerical abstract to 之. Form unchanged in modern simplified orthography.

parts.note: traditional bird pictograph (crow), eye often unmarked vs 鳥

乌.origin: Traditional 烏 is a bird pictograph for the crow, extended to ‘black.’ Simplified 乌 continues both senses.
乌.history: Oracle/bronze bird forms differentiate 烏 from 鳥; seal/regular keep 烏; simplified Chinese uses 乌.

乎.origin: An ancient graph associated with exhaling or calling out, grammaticalized as a classical particle (questions, exclamations). Survives in set phrases and names.
乎.history: Bronze and seal already near modern 乎; clerical/regular refine strokes. Unchanged by simplification.

乐.origin: Traditional 樂 depicts a musical instrument on a stand, hence ‘music’ (yuè) and by extension ‘joy’ (lè). Simplified 乐 covers both readings.
乐.history: Oracle/bronze show instrument structure; seal/regular keep 樂; PRC simplification yields 乐.

乔.origin: Traditional 喬 conveys height or a tall appearance (also a surname). Simplified 乔 keeps ‘tall / lofty’ and name use.
乔.history: Seal and regular traditional use 喬; simplified Chinese shortens to 乔.

parts.note: bent shape; traditional link to a hooked arm or extreme number

九.origin: A curved numeral graph for nine; classical lore sometimes treats nine as an ‘extreme’ yang number. Pictorial origin is uncertain beyond the bent stroke.
九.history: Oracle forms are hook-like; seal and clerical stabilize 九. Unchanged in simplified script.

parts.note: origin debated (vessel / snake / other); later grammaticalized

也.origin: Ancient pictograph whose concrete referent is disputed. Classical Chinese used it as a sentence particle; modern Mandarin mainly keeps the adverb ‘also.’
也.history: Bronze/seal shapes already resemble 也; form is stable through regular and simplified writing.

parts.note: traditional; wings 羽 over sun/white — birds practicing flight

composition.formula: 羽 + 白 → 習 → 习

习.origin: Traditional 習 shows wings over a bright element, classically ‘birds practicing flight,’ hence practice, study, and habit. Simplified 习 truncates to a wing-like form.
习.history: 習 is standard in seal/regular traditional; mainland simplified uses 习.

parts.note: traditional; hand with brush over 曰

composition.formula: 聿 + 曰 → 書 → 书

书.origin: Traditional 書 depicts a hand holding a writing brush over speech/record, meaning ‘to write’ and then ‘book / document.’ Simplified 书 keeps those senses.
书.history: Bronze/seal emphasize the brush hand; regular traditional is 書; PRC orthography uses cursive-based 书.

乱.origin: Traditional 亂 shows tangled silk being handled, encoding disorder and the act of sorting chaos. Simplified 乱 retains ‘disorder / random.’
乱.history: Seal/clerical 亂 is intricate; simplified 乱 keeps the right-hand 乚-like hook and a reduced left.

parts.note: curved / rising element in traditional analysis
phonetic component in fuller analyses (gàn/qián lineage)

乾.origin: A classical graph for the trigram/hexagram Qián (heaven, creative) and, with reading gān, ‘dry.’ Structure is phono-semantic rather than a simple pictograph; senses split by pronunciation.
乾.history: Present in seal and clerical as 乾; in simplified Chinese, ‘dry’ often uses 干 while 乾 remains for the Yijing/name reading qián (and some gān contexts).

parts.note: origin debated; often infant without arms or a bound form — ‘finished’

了.origin: Early explanations vary (e.g. child with unfinished limbs). Lexically it means ‘finish’ (liǎo); Mandarin grammaticalized unstressed le as an aspect/change-of-state particle.
了.history: Seal forms already near 了; widespread particle use grows in vernacular writing. Form identical in traditional and simplified scripts.

予.origin: Depicts giving or transferring; classical Chinese also uses it as a first-person pronoun. Related in sense to 与/與 ‘give.’
予.history: Bronze/seal show a handing gesture abstracted into 予; stable in later scripts.

parts.note: another hand competing for an object

争.origin: Shows hands struggling over something, hence ‘compete / dispute.’ Traditional form 爭 is slightly more explicit; simplified 争 is standard in PRC.
争.history: Seal 爭 highlights dual hands; regular traditional keeps 爭; simplified Chinese uses 争.

事.origin: Linked to hands engaged in official or recorded activity; came to mean ‘affair,’ ‘duty,’ and ‘event.’ Exact early components are fused in the modern outline.
事.history: Oracle/bronze connect to scribal graphs; seal/clerical yield 事, unchanged by simplification.

二.origin: Two horizontal strokes marking the number two, parallel to 一 and 三.
二.history: Invariant from oracle bone through modern script aside from calligraphic style.

composition.formula: 于 (≈ 於 in many uses)

于.origin: An old graph used as a preposition ‘at / to / from / than.’ In many classical and modern contexts it overlaps with 於; simplified Chinese prefers 于 for that role.
于.history: Both 于 and 於 appear early; mainland orthography generally standardizes prepositional use as 于.

云.origin: Ancient 云 pictured swirling vapor and also meant ‘to say.’ Traditional 雲 adds 雨 for the weather sense; simplified Chinese returns to 云 for ‘cloud’ (and classical ‘say’).
云.history: Oracle cloud curls become 云; later 雲 dominates ‘cloud’ in traditional texts; PRC uses 云 for both common senses.

互.origin: An interlocking design suggesting reciprocity or crossing, hence ‘mutual / each other.’
互.history: Seal forms already show the crossed frame of 互; stable through regular and simplified script.

井.origin: A top-down view of a well’s wooden curb (# shape). Extended to other shaft-like structures and place names.
井.history: Oracle and bronze already use the hash-frame; modern 井 is nearly identical across script stages.

些.origin: Combines 此 (‘this’) with 二 to suggest a small plurality — ‘these few / some.’ A later vernacular quantifier.
些.history: Appears in later clerical/regular usage; form is the same in traditional and simplified Chinese.

交.origin: Pictures a person with legs crossed, encoding crossing and interchange; extended to social exchange and delivery.
交.history: Oracle/bronze human forms abstract to seal 交; modern shape is stable.

产.origin: Traditional 產 concerns childbirth and production, then property and industry. Simplified 产 keeps those meanings.
产.history: 產 is standard in seal/regular traditional; PRC simplification uses 产.

享.origin: Depicts a structure for ritual offerings, originally ‘to present / receive sacrifice,’ later ‘to enjoy’ benefits or hospitality.
享.history: Bronze and seal show a roofed ritual building; clerical/regular give modern 享. Unchanged by simplification.

京.origin: A tall building or artificial mound, hence a high place and by extension the capital. Anchors names like 北京.
京.history: Oracle/bronze emphasize height; seal and regular stabilize 京. Same in simplified Chinese.

parts.note: legs / person element in modern form

亮.origin: Associated with elevated brightness or a clear high place; meaning settled as ‘bright / luminous.’ Exact early analysis is somewhat fused in the modern graph.
亮.history: Later seal/clerical forms approach 亮; common in regular script and unchanged by simplification.

亲.origin: Traditional 親 adds 見 (‘see’) to a phonetic/semantic core, stressing close personal connection — kin and intimacy. Simplified 亲 drops 見.
亲.history: 親 is standard traditionally; PRC 亲 is the abbreviated everyday form.

parts.note: side view of a walking person

人.origin: A side-view stick figure of a human being — one of the core pictographs and the source of the 亻 radical.
人.history: Oracle forms are clearly anthropomorphic; seal leans into the modern chevron shape 人, stable ever since.

什.origin: Person radical plus 十: historically a file of ten men / assorted items (什锦). In 什么 the reading shén is a fused interrogative.
什.history: Phono-semantic structure clear from seal onward; form unchanged in simplified Chinese.

composition.formula: 亻 + 堇 → 僅 → 仅

仅.origin: Traditional 僅 is phono-semantic (person + 堇 sound) meaning ‘only / barely.’ Simplified 仅 replaces the phonetic with a short 又-like right side.
仅.history: 僅 in seal/regular traditional; PRC uses 仅 for the adverb.

parts.note: often analyzed as a mouth with a cover — ‘the present moment’

今.origin: An early graph for the present time (‘now / today’). Traditional explanations involve a covered mouth or assembling mark; function is temporal deixis.
今.history: Oracle through regular scripts keep a compact 今; unchanged by simplification.

介.origin: Often explained as a person in armor or with marks on both sides — ‘to lie between,’ then ‘introduce / mediate’ and classical ‘armor.’
介.history: Bronze/seal show the flanked-person motif; modern 介 is stable.

仍.origin: Person radical plus phonetic 乃, conveying continued human action — ‘still / yet / as before.’
仍.history: Clear phono-semantic compound in seal/regular script; identical in simplified Chinese.

parts.note: traditional; two people plus road elements
two people — one following the other

从.origin: Two people, one following the other — ‘follow / comply,’ then the preposition ‘from.’ Traditional 從 adds path components; simplified 从 restores the simple pair.
从.history: Oracle already uses paired figures; 從 becomes standard traditional; PRC prefers 从.

他.origin: Person radical with phonetic 也. Originally ‘other’; modern Mandarin specializes 他 as the masculine third-person pronoun (vs 她 / 它).
他.history: Established as phono-semantic by seal/clerical times; pronoun gender split is a modern orthographic convention.

仙.origin: A person associated with mountains — the Daoist immortal living apart from the ordinary world. Transparent associative compound.
仙.history: Appears in seal/regular as 人+山; form unchanged in simplified Chinese.

代.origin: Person plus phonetic 弋, meaning to take someone’s place, then ‘generation’ and ‘era’ as successive replacements.
代.history: Stable phono-semantic form from seal through modern script.

以.meaning: with; by means of; in order to

parts.note: hand using / carrying an object (traditional view)

以.origin: Early forms suggest a hand employing something, grammaticalized as ‘take / use,’ then the coverb ‘with / by means of / in order to.’
以.history: Oracle/bronze hand-using shapes abstract to seal 以; modern form is stable.

composition.formula: 亻 + 門 → 們 → 们

们.origin: Traditional 們 is phono-semantic (person + gate 門 sound), marking collective/plural for pronouns and some nouns. Simplified 们 replaces 門 with 门.
们.history: Plural 們 is relatively late vernacular; simplified Chinese systematically uses 们.

composition.formula: 亻 + 賈 → 價 → 价

价.origin: Traditional 價 (person + 賈 ‘merchant’ phonetic) means price or value. Simplified 价 uses 介 as a shorter right-hand stand-in.
价.history: 價 in traditional regular script; PRC standard is 价 for commercial ‘price.’

任.origin: Person radical with phonetic 壬: to entrust a person with a task, hence office, responsibility, and ‘let / no matter.’
任.history: Clear in seal/clerical; form unchanged by simplification.

份.origin: Person plus 分 (‘divide’), encoding a person’s allotted share or portion; also classifier for copies and months (月份).
份.history: Phono-semantic compound stable from seal/regular into modern simplified orthography.

企.origin: A person with emphasized foot (止), classically ‘stand on tiptoe / look forward,’ then ‘hope for,’ and in compounds 企业 ‘enterprise.’
企.history: Associative structure visible from seal onward; form unchanged in simplified Chinese.

parts.note: traditional; many people under a lens/eye or with 血 variants in history

众.origin: Idea of many people gathered. Traditional 眾 is more complex; simplified 众 plainly stacks three 人 for ‘crowd / the masses.’
众.history: Early graphs already emphasize plurality of people; PRC 众 is a transparent abbreviation of 眾.

composition.formula: 亻 + 憂 → 優 → 优

优.origin: Traditional 優 (person + 憂 sound) means abundant/excellent, also ‘entertainer.’ Simplified 优 shortens the phonetic side.
优.history: 優 in traditional regular script; mainland standard is 优.

parts.note: huǒ; also mess-mates sharing a fire

伙.origin: Person plus fire: originally messmates sharing a cooking fire, hence companion, crew, and colloquial ‘stuff / group’ (家伙).
伙.history: Later regular-script compound; same form in simplified Chinese (traditional sometimes distinguishes 夥 for ‘many/partner’).

parts.note: traditional; lid over combined elements — gathering

会.origin: Traditional 會 depicts a cover over gathered contents — assembly and meeting; Mandarin also uses huì for ‘know how to / will.’ Simplified 会 continues both.
会.history: 會 is elaborate in seal/regular traditional; PRC 会 is the everyday abbreviated form.

composition.formula: 亻 + 專 → 傳 → 传

传.origin: Traditional 傳 is person + 專 sound: to relay or hand down; reading zhuàn covers commentaries/biographies. Simplified 传 matches 专’s abbreviation.
传.history: 傳 standard traditionally; simplified pairs 传 with 专 in mainland orthography.

parts.note: traditional; 人 + 昜 phonetic (with wound associations)

composition.formula: 亻 + 昜 → 傷 → 伤

伤.origin: Traditional 傷 combines person with phonetic 昜, meaning bodily or emotional injury. Simplified 伤 reduces the right side in line with 阳-type abbreviations.
伤.history: 傷 in seal/regular traditional; PRC uses 伤 for ‘wound / hurt.’

伴.origin: 伴 is a phono-semantic compound: the person radical 亻 marks a human relationship, while 半 supplies the sound bàn. Together they denote someone who shares half of one's path — a companion or partner. The image is of a person standing with another as a matched pair.
伴.history: Used since early texts for traveling or living companions. Later extended to verbal accompany and compounds like 伙伴 (partner).

似.meaning: to resemble; similar to; as if
似.origin: 似 combines the person radical with 以 as phonetic. It originally suggested one person resembling another, then generalized to any likeness. The idea of seeming like grows from comparing human forms side by side.
似.history: Classical Chinese used 似 for resemblance and for the modal as if / seems. Remains common in modern 似乎 and 相似.

但.origin: 但 pairs 亻 with phonetic 旦 (dawn). Early senses included bare or only, as if a person alone at daybreak. From only it developed into the adversative conjunction but / however.
但.history: In classical prose 但 often meant only or merely; the contrastive but became dominant in later and modern Chinese.

位.meaning: seat; place; position; polite measure for people
位.origin: 位 is composed of the radical 亻 (rén), meaning person, and 立 (lì), meaning stand or position. The combination suggests a person standing in a specific place or position. From that concrete stance it came to mean rank, seat, and social place.
位.history: Used for court rank and seating order in early texts; later also a polite classifier for people (三位客人). Remains central for location and status.

住.meaning: to live; to reside; resultative complement of holding firm
住.origin: 住 joins person 亻 with phonetic 主. It suggests a person settled as if mastering a place — to stay or dwell. The sense of remaining fixed also yields the resultative use (hold on, stop successfully).
住.history: From stop / stay it became the ordinary verb for residing. As a complement (记住, 站住) it marks successful holding or stopping.

parts.note: simplified right side replacing traditional 豊

体.origin: Simplified from traditional 體, which had 骨 (bone) plus a phonetic related to ritual vessels — the body's framework. The simplified form keeps 亻 and uses 本, visually linking person with root/fundamental, while inheriting the meaning body, substance, form.
体.history: 體 was the classical form for physical body and abstract system/style; 体 is the modern simplified standard in mainland China.

何.origin: 何 combines 亻 with phonetic 可. Early graphs also related to carrying a load on the shoulder; that concrete sense faded as the character was borrowed and specialized for interrogatives what / how.
何.history: A core classical question word (何故, 如何). Still common in formal and set phrases, though everyday speech often prefers 什么.

parts.note: originally a pictograph of a thatched shelter or surplus dwelling

余.origin: Oracle-bone and bronze forms of 余 depict a simple shelter or roofed structure, linked to dwelling and abundance. It was used as a first-person pronoun in classical Chinese and also for surplus / leftover. Simplified 餘 (surplus) later merged partly with 余 in mainland usage.
余.history: In pre-Qin texts 余 often means I; later 餘 specialized surplus while 余 remained a surname and pronoun in literary style. Mainland simplification often writes both as 余.

parts.note: person; human figure of the Buddha

佛.origin: 佛 is 亻 plus phonetic 弗, used to write the Sanskrit-derived term Buddha via early transcriptions. The person radical fits a revered human or sage figure. Older readings also include fú in compounds meaning as if (仿佛).
佛.history: Entered Chinese with Buddhism; became the standard graph for Buddha and Buddhist culture. Dual readings fó (Buddha) and fú (resemble) persist.

作.meaning: to do; to make; to write; work
作.origin: 作 joins person with phonetic 乍. It denotes a person initiating action — to rise and do, to make or create. From physical labor it extended to composing texts and abstract acting as.
作.history: Extremely frequent from classical through modern Chinese for making, working, and literary composition (作文, 工作).

parts.note: ěr; simplified from 爾, related classical you

你.origin: 你 is a later vernacular pronoun: person radical plus 尔 (from 爾), which itself was a classical you. The compound solidified the everyday second-person singular. Related 妳 was a gendered variant in some regions.
你.history: Replaced much classical 爾/汝 in spoken Mandarin. Now the default informal and general you.

使.meaning: to make; to cause; to use; envoy

parts.note: person; agent who acts or is sent
lì; official — related form providing sound/sense of mission

使.origin: 使 depicts a person linked to 吏 (official/messenger). The core idea is sending someone on a mission or causing them to act. From envoy and employ came the causative make / let and use.
使.history: Classical uses cover embassies, employment, and causation. Modern Chinese keeps 使 for causing, using (使用), and missions (大使).

parts.note: originally a pictograph of a wheat plant; borrowed for come

來.origin: 來 originally pictured a stalk of wheat (related to 麦). Because its sound matched the verb come, it was borrowed as a loan for arriving. The wheat sense later specialized in other graphs, while 來 kept come. Traditional form of simplified 来.
來.history: One of the most basic motion verbs. Traditional 來 remains standard in Taiwan/Hong Kong; mainland uses 来.

parts.note: liè; arrange in a row — sound and idea of ordered cases

例.origin: 例 combines person with phonetic 列 (to arrange). Arranged human cases become precedents and examples. The sense of regular pattern follows from lining things up as models.
例.history: Used in law and ritual for precedents; now general for examples (例如) and instances (病例).

依.meaning: to rely on; according to; lean on

parts.note: yī; clothing — sound; also suggests covering/support

依.origin: 依 places a person beside 衣 (clothes/phonetic). Early imagery suggests clinging to clothing or staying close for support — hence rely on, depend on, and in accordance with.
依.history: Classical and modern uses include dependence (依靠) and following a standard (依照).

侣.origin: 侣 is person plus phonetic 吕. It denotes a paired human companion, overlapping with 伴 but often more literary or intimate (情侣). The doubled-mouth shape of 吕 historically suggested linked tubes or paired units.
侣.history: Literary word for partner; common in 伴侣 and 情侣. Simplified form matches traditional 侶 with 吕 for 呂.

侦.origin: Simplified from 偵: person radical plus phonetic 貞/贞. A person who inquires or scouts ahead yields investigate and spy/detect. The upright sense of 贞 (divination/upright) supplies sound more than meaning.
侦.history: Used for reconnaissance and criminal investigation (侦探, 侦查). Mainland simplified form is 侦.

侧.meaning: side; flank; to incline to the side
侧.origin: 侧 (trad. 側) is person plus phonetic 則/则. It pictures a person turned or placed to the side — flank, lateral position, or tilting. From posture it generalized to any side.
侧.history: Common for sides of objects and figurative side (侧面). Also appears in 侧身 and related posture words.

便.meaning: convenient; then; informal; (pián) cheap in 便宜

parts.note: gēng; provides approximate sound; also change

便.origin: 便 joins person with 更. The idea is a person for whom something is easier or more suitable — convenient, ready. Secondary reading pián appears in 便宜. As a conjunction it means then / in that case.
便.history: Wide classical and modern usage for convenience, excretion (方便), and discourse then. Dual pronunciations are lexicalized.

俗.origin: 俗 is person plus phonetic 谷. It refers to the ways of ordinary people — customs and common practice. From folk custom it also took on vulgar versus refined, and secular versus religious.
俗.history: Central in discussions of manners (风俗) and popular taste (通俗). Contrast with 雅 remains culturally important.

parts.note: child-like form; historically a child being carried/protected

保.origin: Early forms of 保 show a person holding or carrying a child, the quintessential protective act. The right side developed into a form resembling 呆. From shielding a child came protect, preserve, and guarantee.
保.history: Bronze-age protective sense expanded to political protect and modern insurance/security vocabulary (保护, 保证, 保险).

parts.note: speech; words that can be trusted

信.origin: 信 places a person beside speech 言: a person's word that can be relied on. Trust and belief are primary; written letter / message is a later extension of trustworthy communication sent afar.
信.history: Core ethical term in Confucian texts (诚信). Modern senses include belief, information, and correspondence (相信, 短信, 信封).

parts.note: liǎng; two — sound and meaning of duality

俩.origin: 俩 (trad. 倆) is person plus 兩/两 two. It is a colloquial measure-like word for two people or a pair, fusing the dual meaning of 两 with the person radical.
俩.history: Spoken Mandarin favorite (我们俩). Does not freely replace 两 before measure words for things.

俭.origin: 俭 is simplified 儉: person plus phonetic 僉/佥. It characterizes a person who restrains spending — thrift as a virtue. The phonetic once meant all together, but here mainly gives sound.
俭.history: Praised in moral texts as frugality (勤俭). Remains a positive value word in modern Chinese.

修.meaning: to repair; to fix; to cultivate; to build

parts.note: person; human action of mending or cultivating
yōu; distant/long; originally person with outstretched hands

修.origin: The character 修 combines the radical 亻 (rén, person) on the left, indicating a human action, and 攸 (yōu) on the right, which phonetically complements the meaning and originally depicted a person extending their hands, suggesting handling or mending. From physical repair it extended to self-cultivation and composing texts.
修.history: Spans crafts (修理), study (进修), and moral cultivation (修养). Also used in place names and personal names.

俯.meaning: to bow the head; to look down; to stoop
俯.origin: 俯 is person plus phonetic 府. It describes a person bending forward or looking downward. Contrast with 仰 (look up) forms a classic pair of postures.
俯.history: Literary and technical uses include 俯视 and 俯冲. Less common in casual speech than 低头.

倍.meaning: times; -fold; to double; times as much
倍.origin: 倍 combines 亻 with phonetic 咅. Early sense included turning one's back or multiplying — becoming double. It settled as the standard multiplier times / -fold.
倍.history: Essential in mathematics and comparison (两倍, 倍数). Also verbal to double in some contexts.

倒.meaning: to fall; to collapse; (dào) to pour; inverted; on the contrary
倒.origin: 倒 is person plus phonetic 到. A person going over yields fall / topple. Tone and sense split: dǎo for collapsing, dào for pouring, reversing, or discourse actually / on the contrary.
倒.history: High-frequency verb with many compounds (倒闭, 倒车). Learners must track the two readings.

候.meaning: to wait; season; time; climate; inquire after
候.origin: 候 adds a small stroke distinction to 侯: a person watching or waiting, as an attendant at a feudal court might. From waiting developed seasons (时候, 气候) and polite inquiry (问候).
候.history: Classical observational and seasonal senses persist; modern Chinese uses it heavily in 时候 and weather terms.

值.meaning: value; worth; to be on duty; to happen to

parts.note: zhí; straight — sound and idea of fair measure

值.origin: 值 pairs person with phonetic 直 (straight). Straight dealing suggests fair worth — value and price. Related senses include being on duty (值班) and happening to meet a time (正值).
值.history: Economic value (价值) dominates modern use; duty and coincidence senses remain productive.

parts.note: person; assumed identity or borrowed role
jiǎ; borrow — sound and related sense of borrowing

假.origin: 假 is person plus 叚 borrow. Borrowed appearance becomes false / sham; borrowed time from work becomes leave (假期, reading jià). Conditional if (假如) is a further abstract use.
假.history: Two readings jiǎ/jià are standard. Classical philosophy debated 假名; modern speech uses both fake and holiday senses daily.

停.meaning: to stop; to pause; to park
停.origin: 停 combines person with phonetic 亭 (pavilion). A pavilion is a place to halt on a journey, so the character means stop, pause, or park. The resting-place image supports the sense of cessation.
停.history: From human stopping to vehicles (停车) and services (停电). Extremely common modern verb.

健.origin: 健 is person plus phonetic 建 (build/establish). A firmly established person is strong and healthy. The building metaphor supports vigor and robust constitution.
健.history: Key health vocabulary (健康, 保健). Also describes vigorous action (健谈).

偶.origin: 偶 joins person with phonetic 禺. Early sense includes wooden figurines and paired mates — two matching human forms. From pairing came even number, and from chance encounters accidentally (偶然).
偶.history: Spans 偶像, 配偶, 偶数, and 偶然. Rich semantic family from one pairing root.

偷.meaning: to steal; stealthily; to slack off
偷.origin: 偷 is person plus phonetic 俞. It denotes a person taking things secretly — steal. Extended uses cover doing things stealthily and shirking (偷懒).
偷.history: Everyday verb for theft and sneaky actions. Moral and legal vocabulary built around 盗窃 overlaps with it.

傻.origin: 傻 is a later vernacular character: person radical plus a phonetic complex on the right. It labels a person as foolish, dull, or naively simple. Tone is often affectionate or teasing rather than purely clinical.
傻.history: Colloquial Mandarin staple (傻瓜, 傻笑). Relatively modern in popular writing compared with classical 愚.

像.meaning: likeness; image; to resemble; such as

parts.note: xiàng; elephant / image — sound and related sense of appearance

像.origin: 像 adds person to 象 (elephant; also image/appearance). A person's likeness or portrait is the core; from resemblance came look like and exemplifying such as. Closely related to 象 but specialized for images of people and likeness.
像.history: Used for statues, photos, and similarity (画像, 好像). Distinguishing 像/象 is a modern orthographic concern.

儒.origin: 儒 is person plus phonetic 需. It named soft-robed ritual specialists and scholars; later it became the label for Confucian literati and the Confucian school (儒家). The person radical marks a social role.
儒.history: Central cultural term from Han onward for the Confucian tradition (儒学, 儒生). Still identifies scholarly gentleness in compounds.

parts.note: simplified from 兒; legs of a child pictograph

儿.origin: Traditional 兒 pictured a child's head and legs — a young human. Simplified 儿 keeps the lower legs form. Meanings include child/son and the northern Mandarin diminutive suffix (花儿).
儿.history: Also a radical component in characters like 先 and 光. Erhua spelling uses 儿 extensively in mainland orthography.

允.meaning: to allow; to permit; fair; sincere

parts.note: upper mark; historically linked to assenting posture

允.origin: 允 shows a person with an affirming gesture or head element, conveying assent and reliability. From sincere agreement came permit / allow and fair and proper.
允.history: Classical sense of truthfulness (允执厥中) remains literary; modern 允许 is the main everyday verb.

元.meaning: first; primary; origin; dollar; Yuan dynasty

parts.note: person; the head of a person as foremost

元.origin: 元 depicts a person with emphasis on the head — the uppermost, primary part. Hence first, original, fundamental. Later senses include currency units and the Yuan dynasty name.
元.history: Philosophical origin (元气), calendrical first, and modern money (美元, 元) all stem from primacy of the head/beginning.

充.meaning: to fill; full; to serve as; sufficient

parts.note: upper related to growth/abundance forms historically

充.origin: Early 充 shows a person swollen or filled out, the body made ample. From physical fullness came fill up, sufficient, and act as / pose as (充数). Growth and abundance imagery underlies the graph.
充.history: Used for charging (充电), filling, and pretending. Remains a productive verb in modern Chinese.

parts.note: person; one who goes in front

先.origin: The character 先 combines elements historically read as a foot (止) above a person (儿). It depicted a person leading or going ahead, symbolizing being first or in front. From spatial precedence came temporal before and ancestral forebears.
先.history: Core sequencing word (先生, 先进, 祖先). The folk analysis with 土 is common but the foot-ahead analysis better matches paleography.

parts.note: person; one bearing or standing in light

光.origin: 光 shows fire or flames above a kneeling/standing person — light shining on or from a human figure. Brightness, glory, and smooth/bare (光头) extend from that luminous core.
光.history: Fundamental nature and moral metaphor (光明). Also colloquial only / nothing but (光吃饭).

克.meaning: to overcome; to restrain; able to; gram

parts.note: early form of a person with helmet/weapon subduing; also shouldered load

克.origin: Oracle forms of 克 suggest a helmeted figure or a person shouldering a burden — conquering or enduring. Meanings include overcome, restrain, and be able to. The metric gram is a modern phonetic loan (克).
克.history: Classical military and moral conquer oneself uses persist (克服, 克制). Scientific unit sense is recent.

入.meaning: to enter; to go into; income
入.origin: 入 is a pictograph of something pointed entering an opening — the act of going in. From physical entry came joining a group, recording income (收入), and many verbal compounds.
入.history: Basic antonym of 出. Unchanged in form from ancient to modern script in essence.

內.origin: Traditional 內 shows enter 入 inside an enclosure 冂 — that which is within. Meanings cover spatial inside, inner rooms, and domestic/internal affairs. Simplified mainland form is 内.
內.history: Paired with 外 across classical and modern Chinese. This traditional shape remains standard in Taiwan and many overseas texts.

parts.note: upper form related to covering/jade variants historically
jade/king component; pure jade as complete/perfect

全.origin: 全 is often explained as pure jade (王/玉 under a cover) — something complete and unflawed. From unbroken jade came whole, entire, complete. The upper strokes vary in analysis but the completeness sense is stable.
全.history: High-frequency quantifier and adjective (全部, 完全). Related 安全 preserves the intact sense.

parts.note: two strokes dividing apart; also numeral

八.origin: 八 shows two lines parting from each other — the idea of division/separation. It was used for the number eight and also as a radical meaning divide. Lucky associations of eight are later cultural overlays.
八.history: Basic numeral and radical. Appears in many characters as the divide component (分).

parts.note: private; contrasted with the open above

公.origin: 公 contrasts opening/dividing 八 with private 厶: what is made open is public and fair. Extended to male animals, feudal dukes, and later metric kilo- in compounds (公斤).
公.history: Cornerstone of political vocabulary (公共, 公共利益). Ancient title 公 and kinship 公公 coexist with modern public senses.

parts.note: numeral graph; early forms vary, stabilized as six

六.origin: 六 is the numeral six. Early graphs are debated (sometimes linked to a shed or a hand-related form), but the character stabilized early as the number. It also appears in calendrical and musical traditional sets of six.
六.history: Basic counting word. Cultural sets like 六合 keep the number prominent.

兰.meaning: orchid; elegant plant; used in names
兰.origin: Simplified from 蘭, a phono-semantic plant character (艹 + phonetic). Orchids symbolized elegance and integrity in literati culture. The simplified graph 兰 drastically abbreviates the complex traditional form while keeping the reading lán.
兰.history: Common in personal and place names; botanical and poetic orchid imagery remains. Mainland standard is 兰.

parts.note: upper element; shared object held up historically

共.origin: 共 depicts two hands offering or holding something together — joint action. From that came common, shared, and altogether. It is the root of many together compounds.
共.history: Basis of 共同, 共产党, 一共. Paleographic two-hands image is widely accepted.

关.meaning: to close; to shut; gate; concern; relation

parts.note: simplified lower form from traditional gate bars

关.origin: Traditional 關 shows a gate 門 with bars — a frontier pass that can be shut. Simplified 关 keeps the reading and core meanings: close, checkpoint, and by extension concern / relate to (关系).
关.history: From physical gates and mountain passes to abstract involvement. Extremely frequent modern verb and noun.

兴.meaning: to prosper; to rise; to start; (xìng) interest; mood

parts.note: lower dividing strokes in simplified form

兴.origin: Traditional 興 shows many hands lifting a shared object — raising up, starting, flourishing. Simplified 兴 inherits prosper/rise and the reading xìng for interest (高兴). Unity in lifting explains the flourish sense noted in folk explanations with 八 and 同.
兴.history: Dual readings xīng/xìng are standard. Political and everyday words (兴盛, 兴趣) both derive from rise / lift.

parts.note: originally a winnowing basket pictograph; loaned for pronoun

其.origin: The character 其 originally depicted a winnowing basket used for separating grain from chaff. Over time it was used phonetically and took on the abstract meaning of its or their. Components suggest a basket with a stand or hands beneath.
其.history: Core classical determiner/pronoun. The basket sense survives in 箕. Modern Chinese retains 其 in formal style more than speech.

具.meaning: tool; utensil; to possess; to provide

parts.note: upper derived from cowry/treasure forms in early graphs

具.origin: 具 shows two hands setting forth a valuable object (early forms involve cowries or ritual goods) — to prepare and present. From prepared implements came tool / utensil and to possess / be equipped with.
具.history: 工具 and 具备 are central modern uses. Classical ritual prepare offerings sense underlies the graph.

养.meaning: to raise; to support; to cultivate; to nourish

parts.note: simplified lower strokes from traditional 養

养.origin: Traditional 養 is phono-semantic with sheep/food elements — feeding livestock and dependents. Simplified 养 keeps the rearing sense: raise children, keep animals, nourish health, cultivate habits.
养.history: From agrarian caretaking to abstract cultivation (修养, 营养). Mainland form is 养.

parts.note: lower strokes abbreviating traditional animal form

兽.origin: Traditional 獸 depicts a hunting dog or wild creature with weapon/hunt elements — beasts of the chase. Simplified 兽 truncates the form but retains wild animal / beast, including figurative brutal people.
兽.history: Contrasts with 畜 (livestock). Compounds like 野兽 and 怪兽 are common.

内.origin: 内 is the simplified form of 內: enter/person inside an enclosure. It means inside, inner, and internal/domestic. Same etymology as traditional 內 in this batch.
内.history: Mainland standard graph. Pairs with 外 in all registers of modern Chinese.

册.meaning: volume; booklet; register; to appoint by imperial edict
册.origin: 册 pictures bamboo writing slips tied together with a cord — a bound document. From physical volumes came registers, booklets, and formal appointment documents (册封).
册.history: Fundamental to the history of Chinese books. Still used as a measure word for volumes and in 手册, 注册.

parts.note: related form; fishnet or repeated structure historically cited

再.origin: 再 conveys repetition — doing something a second time. Graphic analyses link it to layered or repeated structures. Semantically it marks again and, in modern Chinese, sequencing and then for future actions.
再.history: Distinguished from 又 in aspect and tense teaching. Extremely frequent adverb.

冒.meaning: to emit; to risk; to pretend; bold

parts.note: eye; peering out from under a cover

冒.origin: 冒 shows an eye under a covering/hat — emerging, sticking out, or braving exposure. From come forth came emit (冒烟), risk (冒险), and falsely assume (冒充).
冒.history: Also a surname. Compounds cover weather (感冒) via older medical attack senses.

写.meaning: to write; to compose; to describe
写.origin: Traditional 寫 involved a roof/shelter and a magpie-related phonetic, with senses of placing and transcribing. Simplified 写 keeps the writing sense as the primary modern meaning: write characters, compose, depict.
写.history: Core literacy verb. Mainland 写 vs traditional 寫 is a major simplified/traditional pair.

军.origin: Traditional 軍 shows a chariot under a cover/encirclement — the army organized around war chariots. Simplified 军 replaces 車 with 车 but keeps military force.
军.history: Central military vocabulary (军队, 将军). Reflects ancient chariot warfare in its structure.

冠.meaning: hat; crown; (guàn) to cap; to champion
冠.origin: 冠 shows covering the head (元) with a hand (寸) under a cover — putting on a ceremonial cap. Noun guān is the hat/crown; verb guàn is to cap or to take first place.
冠.history: Coming-of-age capping rites and champion metaphors (冠军) preserve the culture of the ceremonial hat.

parts.note: lower element in the standard form

冥.origin: 冥 depicts the sun covered over — darkness and obscurity. From physical gloom came the netherworld (冥府) and deep, hard-to-fathom matters.
冥.history: Literary and religious vocabulary (冥界). Less common in casual speech than 暗.

冬.origin: 冬 combines an ending/walking component with ice dots — the cold close of the year. It is the season when ice appears. Ancient forms emphasize finality of the agricultural cycle.
冬.history: One of the four seasons; stable meaning from early texts to today. Also a surname.

冰.origin: 冰 places the ice radical beside water — frozen water. The two-dot 冫 itself is an abbreviated ice sign; together they make the full modern character for ice and freezing.
冰.history: Literal ice and metaphorical coldness. Radical 冫 heads many cold-related characters in this batch.

决.meaning: to decide; to determine; definitely; to burst (dike)

parts.note: guài; decide/part — sound and cutting sense

决.origin: Traditional 決 shows water bursting a dike (氵/冫 + 夬). From a decisive breach came decide / determine and emphatic definitely. Simplified 决 uses 冫.
决.history: Decision vocabulary (决定, 解决) dominates; hydraulic burst sense is older and still literary.

况.origin: 况 (trad. 況) is water/ice radical plus phonetic 兄. Early senses include cold water and comparison; it settled as situation / condition and the connective moreover (况且).
况.history: 情况 and 状况 are everyday nouns. Traditional 況 keeps 氵.

冷.origin: 冷 is ice radical plus phonetic 令. Physical cold is primary; metaphorical chill describes manner and atmosphere (冷静, 冷淡).
冷.history: Basic temperature antonym of 热. Wide figurative use in modern Mandarin.

parts.note: dōng; east — simplified phonetic from 東

冻.origin: 冻 (trad. 凍) is ice plus phonetic 東/东. It means freeze or become icy, including cold of weather and gelatinous aspic foods. Clear phono-semantic structure.
冻.history: Common weather and food word (冻结, 冷冻). Simplified phonetic matches 东.

凄.meaning: bleak; dreary; mournful; cold and desolate
凄.origin: 凄 combines ice with phonetic 妻. Cold desolation yields bleak scenery and mournful feeling. Related traditional variant 淒 uses the water radical with the same bleak sense.
凄.history: Literary adjective for chilly sadness (凄凉, 凄惨). More written than spoken in casual style.

准.meaning: standard; accurate; to allow; to prepare
准.origin: Traditional 準 used water (氵) plus phonetic ideas of leveling — a water-level as standard. Simplified 准 keeps accurate, standard, permit, and prepare (准备). Water as a level explains precise.
准.history: Merged some uses of 準 in simplification. 准确, 标准, 批准 are core modern words.

凉.origin: The character 凉 includes the radical 冫 (bīng, ice) on the left, symbolizing coldness, and 京 (jīng, capital) on the right as the phonetic component. Mild cold — cooler than 冷 — and figurative chill of mood follow from that structure.
凉.history: Weather, food (凉菜), and emotion (心凉) uses are all common. Traditional often written 涼 with 氵.

凋.origin: 凋 is ice radical plus phonetic 周. Cold causes plants to wither — fade and decline. The image is seasonal blight rather than sudden freeze alone.
凋.history: Literary verb for withering (凋零, 凋谢). Less colloquial than 枯.

减.origin: The character 减 includes the radical 冫 (ice) on the left, symbolizing something cold or diminishing, and 咸 (xián) on the right as phonetic. Together they convey reducing or lowering. Traditional 減 often uses 氵.
减.history: Arithmetic subtract and general decrease (减少, 减肥). Standard modern simplified form.

几.meaning: which; some; a few; (jī) small table; almost

parts.note: pictograph of a low table; also numeral interrogative by use

几.origin: 几 originally pictured a low stool or side table (reading jī). It was also used for the interrogative/indefinite how many / a few (jǐ). Simplified script further uses 几 as the short form of traditional 幾 for the number senses.
几.history: Table sense survives in 茶几; quantity sense (几个, 几乎 from 幾) is dominant for learners. Tone distinguishes jī/jǐ.

parts.note: early form depicts a flat tray or general frame

凡.origin: 凡 began as a pictograph of a shallow tray or pan, later generalized to mean “common” or “ordinary.” From that sense of what is typical or all-encompassing, it also came to mean “all” or “every.” The rounded modern shape still echoes the open vessel outline of early bronze forms.
凡.history: In classical Chinese, 凡 often introduces a general rule (“in all cases”). It remains a high-frequency function word and adjective in modern Mandarin.

parts.note: bird; traditional form 鳳 has 鳥

凤.origin: 鳳 originally showed a fabulous bird with a phonetic element; it named the mythical phoenix associated with virtue and auspiciousness. The simplified form 凤 reduces the complex traditional structure while keeping a bird-related cue. Paired with 凰, it forms the compound 凤凰 “phoenix.”
凤.history: Simplification retained cultural meaning while cutting stroke count. 凤 appears in names, idioms, and literary imagery for beauty and good fortune.

parts.note: related to leaning/relying sense in traditional 憑

凭.origin: 凭 (traditional 憑) conveys leaning on a stand or relying on something for support. The idea of physical leaning extended metaphorically to depending on evidence, authority, or a basis. Modern usage centers on “relying on,” “by virtue of,” and “proof/voucher.”
凭.history: Classical texts use 憑 for leaning and reliance; simplified 凭 kept the core meaning. It is common in phrases like 凭借 and 凭证.

凰.origin: 凰 names the female phoenix and was formed with 皇 as a phonetic cue for huáng. Together with 凤 it completes the paired mythical bird 凤凰. The character’s prestige associations come from both the phoenix myth and the “imperial” overtones of 皇.
凰.history: 凰 is less common alone than in 凤凰. It appears mainly in literary, naming, and cultural contexts.

出.meaning: to go out; to exit; to produce
出.origin: 出 depicts something emerging from an enclosure or pit—classically explained as a foot or plant sprout leaving a cavity. From “coming out” it developed senses of exiting, appearing, producing, and exceeding. The stacked modern shape preserves that upward-exit idea.
出.history: One of the most frequent verbs in Chinese, 出 underlies compounds like 出去, 出现, and 出版. Its core spatial meaning stayed stable from oracle bones to today.

击.meaning: to hit; to strike; to attack
击.origin: 擊 originally combined hand action with a phonetic element to mean striking or attacking. Simplified 击 is a drastically reduced form that keeps the hitting sense without the full traditional structure. It covers physical blows and figurative attacks.
击.history: After simplification, 击 became the standard mainland form in words like 打击 and 攻击. Traditional 擊 remains in some regions and older texts.

凿.meaning: to chisel; chisel; to cut a hole
凿.origin: 鑿 pictured chiseling or boring into a hollow, with metal/tool associations in the traditional form. Simplified 凿 keeps the idea of cutting an opening with a chisel. Extended senses include digging into a topic or forcing a forced explanation (穿凿).
凿.history: Used for tools and actions of chiseling; also appears in fixed expressions. Pronunciation záo is common for the tool/verb; older readings exist in compounds.

parts.note: mark pointing to the cutting edge

刃.origin: 刃 is 刀 with a mark highlighting the sharp edge—an indicative ideograph for the blade’s cutting part. From “edge” it extends to sharpness, killing power, and metaphors like 刀刃. It is a classic example of pointing to a specific part of a pictograph.
刃.history: Appears early in weapon and craft vocabulary. Remains transparent to learners because the knife shape is still visible.

分.origin: 分 shows a knife separating something into parts (八 suggesting division). The core meaning is to split or distribute; later it measured portions, minutes, scores, and distinctions. The reading fèn appears in “portion/role” senses.
分.history: Extremely productive in modern Chinese (分开, 分数, 分钟). The graphic logic of cutting apart stayed clear across script reforms.

切.origin: 切 pairs the knife radical with 七 as a phonetic to mean cutting or slicing. From physical cutting it developed senses of closeness, urgency, and correspondence (合适/切合), especially with the qiè reading. Both cooking and abstract “pertinent” uses coexist.
切.history: Common in daily verbs (切开) and literary adverbs (迫切). Tone/reading splits track meaning differences in modern Mandarin.

刊.origin: 刊 originally meant to cut or carve, especially trimming wood or engraving text with a knife. Publishing and periodicals grew from the idea of cutting blocks or issuing fixed text. The knife radical marks the cutting/editing action.
刊.history: Modern compounds like 刊物 and 刊登 keep the publication sense; older “carve/correct” meanings survive in set phrases.

划.meaning: to plan; to row; to scratch; to transfer
划.origin: 划 relates to making strokes, dividing, or propelling with an oar; traditional 劃 emphasizes planning and drawing lines. The knife/weapon elements suggest cutting marks. Readings huá (row/scratch) and huà (plan/divide) split by sense.
划.history: Mainland 划 covers several traditional distinctions; learners meet it in 计划, 划船, and 划算.

parts.note: remnant of older phonetic; bone/flesh form

列.origin: 列 originally involved cutting or arranging in order—rows of things set apart. From lined-up ranks it came to mean listing, series, and columns. The knife radical supports the sense of separating into ordered parts.
列.history: Frequent in 排列, 列车, and 系列. The “row/list” meaning has been stable in administrative and everyday vocabulary.

则.origin: 則/则 historically relates to norms and regulations—cutting a standard as if with a knife, with 貝 suggesting valued rules. From “rule/norm” it grammaticalized into “then/thus” connecting clauses. Modern meaningHint focuses on that connective use.
则.history: Classical Chinese used 则 heavily as a logical connector; modern writing still uses it in formal style (否则, 原则).

刚.origin: 剛/刚 combines a phonetic with the knife radical to mean hard, firm, or strong—like a tough blade. Temporal “just now” and adverbial “exactly” are later extensions of firmness and immediacy. The simplified left side 冈 keeps the phonetic link.
刚.history: Common in 刚才, 刚刚, and 坚强-related compounds. Simplification did not change core meanings.

创.origin: 創/创 centers on cutting—originally a wound (chuāng) and later “to found/create” (chuàng) as opening a new path. The knife radical signals the cutting action; the left component is phonetic. Both medical and innovative senses remain active.
创.history: Reading split tracks meaning: 创伤 vs 创造. Simplified 创 is standard in mainland usage.

parts.note: knife; cutting cloth to start a garment

初.origin: 初 depicts cutting cloth with a knife—the first step in making clothes—hence “beginning.” From garment-making’s opening cut it generalized to any start or initial stage. The clothing radical still hints at that craft origin.
初.history: Used from early texts for “at first” and calendar beginnings (初一). Remains transparent and high-frequency.

删.origin: The character "删" consists of the radical "刂" (dāo, meaning "knife") on the right, symbolizing cutting or removal, and "册" (cè, meaning "book" or "volume") on the left, indicating its original use in editing or censoring written documents. Cutting strips from a bamboo book meant excising unwanted text. That concrete editing act became the general verb “to delete.”
删.history: From manuscript culture to digital interfaces, 删除 kept the same core idea. It is the standard verb for removing content today.

判.meaning: to judge; to decide; to distinguish

parts.note: bàn; also suggests splitting in half

判.origin: 判 combines half/split imagery with a knife to mean dividing cases or rendering a judgment. Distinguishing right from wrong is framed as cutting apart. Legal and evaluative senses (判断, 判决) dominate modern use.
判.history: Long associated with courts and decision-making. Remains central in legal and everyday “judge/decide” vocabulary.

別.origin: 別 shows separating with a knife—leaving something apart as “other” or “different.” From separation came farewell and the prohibitive “don’t” in speech. This is the traditional form corresponding to simplified 别.
別.history: Still used in traditional-script regions and classical texts. Mainland simplified writing prefers 别 for the same meanings.

刨.meaning: to plane (wood); to dig; plane tool
刨.origin: 刨 uses the knife radical with 包 as phonetic for planing wood or scraping a surface. The reading páo appears in “dig/excavate” senses. Tool and action meanings both remain everyday.
刨.history: Common in carpentry (刨子) and colloquial digging metaphors. Phonetic 包 is still recognizable.

parts.note: knife; harvesting with a sharp blade

利.origin: 利 depicts cutting grain with a knife—sharpness that makes harvest efficient, hence “sharp” and “beneficial.” Profit and advantage are natural extensions of a tool that works well. The agricultural image underlies later economic senses.
利.history: Core in 利益, 利用, and 锋利. One of the classic knife-radical characters taught for transparent structure.

别.origin: 别 is the simplified form of 別, built from separation with a knife. Meanings include “other,” “leave,” and colloquial prohibitive “don’t.” The meaningHint highlights the everyday spoken command use.
别.history: In Mandarin speech, 别 is a primary way to say “don’t.” Written language also keeps “separate/other” senses (别人, 特别).

刮.meaning: to scrape; to blow (wind); to shave
刮.origin: 刮 pairs a knife with a phonetic to mean scraping or shaving a surface. Wind “scraping” across the land yields 刮风. Figurative uses include extorting or sweeping away.
刮.history: Everyday verb for weather, cleaning, and shaving. The tongue component is mainly phonetic in the received analysis.

parts.note: dāo; phonetic contribution in traditional analysis

到.origin: 到 is built on 至 “arrive,” reinforced into the standard verb “to reach.” It also became a resultative complement (看到, 做得到). The right-side knife form functions largely as a phonetic/structural companion in the modern graph.
到.history: Among the highest-frequency verbs and complements in Mandarin. Spatial “arrive” and grammatical complement uses both solidified early.

制.meaning: to make; to control; system; regulate

parts.note: upper-left remnant related to cutting cloth/wood

制.origin: 制 involves cutting material to a set form—making and regulating by measure. From crafting came institutional “system” and verbs of control (控制, 制定). Related 製 specialized “manufacture” in traditional orthography.
制.history: Mainland 制 often covers both regulation and manufacture senses. Extremely common in political, legal, and industrial vocabulary.

parts.note: part of left phonetic cluster 刷's body

composition.formula: 刷 = 扌旁关联动作+刂

刷.origin: 刷 denotes scraping or brushing a surface clean, with the knife radical hinting at a scraping implement. It names both the action and the tool (刷子). Modern slang extended it to “refresh” or binge through content.
刷.history: Stable household verb; digital metaphors (刷新) are recent but widespread.

刺.meaning: to stab; thorn; to irritate; to assassinate

parts.note: cì; thorn; also semantic for prickles

刺.origin: 刺 joins thorn imagery (朿) with a knife to mean piercing or stabbing. Physical thorns and figurative stings (刺激) share the same root idea. Assassination compounds like 刺客 preserve the violent sense.
刺.history: Transparent phono-semantic structure still taught in textbooks. Readings and meanings stayed closely tied to “pierce.”

刻.origin: 刻 means to engrave or cut into a surface with a blade. Time “quarter-hour” comes from marked divisions; “harsh” and “deeply impressed” are metaphorical extensions of cutting in. The phonetic 亥 supports the kè sound.
刻.history: Used for carving, clocks (刻), and intensity (深刻). Remains a standard knife-radical example.

剂.origin: 劑/剂 refers to cutting or proportioning ingredients—especially medicine—into a proper dose. From pharmacy it broadened to chemical agents and mixtures. The knife radical marks measured cutting/preparation.
剂.history: Common in 药剂, 剂量, and industrial “-剂” suffixes. Simplification replaced 齊 with 齐.

削.meaning: to pare; to cut away; to weaken; to peel (xiāo)
削.origin: 削 means slicing thin layers away with a knife—paring, peeling, or reducing. Abstract senses include cutting budgets or weakening power. Readings xuē and xiāo distribute across formal vs. colloquial uses.
削.history: Appears in 剥削, 削减, and kitchen talk. Phonetic 肖 remains clear.

parts.note: boat element in seal-script analyses of 前

前.origin: 前 originally conveyed moving forward—often analyzed as a foot advancing relative to a boat or similar early components. Spatial “in front” became temporal “before/ago.” The meaningHint matches the dominant modern senses.
前.history: Core spatial-temporal word from classical to modern Chinese (以前, 前面). Graphic form simplified in strokes but meaning stayed stable.

剔.meaning: to pick out; to scrape out; to reject
剔.origin: 剔 means using a pointed blade to pick meat from bones or remove unwanted bits. From careful picking came “reject/eliminate” in selection. The knife radical signals the precise cutting action.
剔.history: Common in cooking and metaphorical screening (剔除). Less frequent than basic knife verbs but still transparent.

剥.meaning: to peel; to skin; to strip; to exploit
剥.origin: 剝/剥 depicts stripping away an outer layer with a knife—peeling fruit or skinning. Figurative “exploit/strip of rights” (剥削) follows from forcibly taking an outer covering. Reading bāo is common in colloquial “peel.”
剥.history: Simplification merged into 剥 for mainland use. Dual readings track register and sense.

剧.origin: 劇/剧 originally suggested severe, intense action—almost cutting in force—and later specialized as theatrical drama. Both “severe” (剧烈) and “play/show” (戏剧) are standard today. The phonetic 居 remains in the simplified graph.
剧.history: Modern media vocabulary (电视剧) boosted the drama sense. Traditional 劇 is still used outside simplified orthography.

割.meaning: to cut; to sever; to give up
割.origin: 割 means cutting off with a knife—severing flesh, land, or ties. Figurative “give up/part with” (割舍) follows from cutting something away. The left side is phonetic.
割.history: Used in surgery, agriculture, and politics (割据). A straightforward knife-radical verb.

劈.meaning: to split; to chop; to strike (lightning)
劈.origin: 劈 shows splitting with a knife or axe-like force, using 辟 as phonetic. It covers chopping wood, cleaving, and lightning strikes. Reading pǐ appears in some “split toward” colloquial senses.
劈.history: Vivid action verb in speech and writing (劈开, 雷劈). Structure is clearly phono-semantic.

parts.note: depicts a plow or tensed muscle/arm in early forms

力.origin: 力 is a pictograph commonly linked to a plow or a straining arm—symbols of physical effort. From bodily strength it generalized to power, ability, and force in physics and society. It is also a productive radical for effort-related characters.
力.history: Appears from oracle bones onward. Remains a basic vocabulary item and radical in the modern script.

劝.meaning: to advise; to urge; to persuade

parts.note: simplified left remnant of traditional 勸

劝.origin: 勸/劝 means urging someone with effort—using force of speech rather than violence. The 力 component marks exertion; simplification heavily reduced the left phonetic complex. Core sense is advise, encourage, or dissuade.
劝.history: Common in 劝告 and 劝说. Simplified form is standard in mainland textbooks.

parts.note: dots in simplified form from 辦's middle

办.origin: The character "办" is a simplified form of "辦" (bàn), which originally depicted a hand performing an action, symbolizing the idea of managing or handling something. Traditional 辦 emphasizes dealing with affairs through effort; simplified 办 keeps 力 as the effort cue. Modern meanings cover doing, handling, and organizing events.
办.history: Extremely frequent in 办公室, 办法, and 举办. Simplification made a once-complex graph compact.

parts.note: gōng; work; also semantic for labor

功.origin: 功 combines work (工) and strength (力) to mean accomplishment earned by effort. Merit, skill, and scientific “work” all grow from successful exertion. The structure is among the clearest phono-semantic pairs.
功.history: Central in 成功, 功课, and 功能. Taught early as a model 力-radical character.

parts.note: mouth; speaking praise or adding words

加.origin: 加 is often explained as adding force or words—putting more onto something. The arithmetic and general “increase/add” senses dominate modern use. It also forms causative and intensifying compounds.
加.history: Basic math and daily verb (加油, 参加). Graphic composition stayed simple and stable.

务.origin: 務/务 concerns duties that require effort—affairs and tasks one must handle. From concrete business it also yields the adverbial “be sure to.” Simplification preserves 力 as the effort marker.
务.history: High-frequency in 任务, 服务, and 务必. Official and everyday registers both use it heavily.

parts.note: yún; simplified phonetic from 重 in 動

动.origin: 動/动 means movement produced by force—力 provides the semantic core. Traditional 動 used 重 as phonetic; simplified 动 substitutes a lighter left side. Senses cover physical motion, activities, and being touched emotionally.
动.history: Among the most common verbs (运动, 活动). Simplification is fully standard in PRC orthography.

助.origin: 助 adds one’s strength (力) to another—help and assistance. The left component is phonetic. Moral and practical helping senses are both ancient and modern.
助.history: Core vocabulary in 帮助 and 助手. Clear radical-phonetic teaching example.

努.origin: 努 means putting forth strength—striving hard. 努力 “to make an effort” is the dominant compound. The phonetic 奴 and semantic 力 form a classic pair.
努.history: Learned early via 努力. Other senses (pouting lips) are secondary in modern Mandarin.

劫.origin: 劫 combines taking away (去) with force (力)—robbery by violence. Buddhist “kalpa” and general disaster senses are later overlays on the idea of a violent turn. The structure reads like a transparent compound.
劫.history: Used in crime vocabulary (抢劫) and religious/literary disaster terms. Remains graphically clear.

劲.origin: 勁/劲 denotes strength, stamina, or forceful energy. Reading jìng appears in “strong/sturdy” compounds; jìn is common for vigor and mood. Simplification keeps 力 as the meaning anchor.
劲.history: Colloquial Mandarin loves 劲 for “energy/zest” (带劲). Traditional 勁 persists in some print traditions.

劳.meaning: labor; toil; to put to trouble
劳.origin: 勞/劳 depicts exhausting labor—strength spent on work, historically linked to farm toil under lights or similar early imagery. Modern senses cover labor, fatigue, and polite “thanks for your trouble.” The 力 element preserves the effort meaning.
劳.history: Key in 劳动 and political-economic vocabulary. Simplified 劳 is standard in the PRC.

parts.note: zhí; phonetic link via traditional 勢

势.origin: 勢/势 means force configured as influence—power, momentum, or the lie of a situation. 力 marks force; the upper/left complex is phonetic in origin. Abstract “circumstances” (形势) are central today.
势.history: Common in strategy, physics, and daily talk (姿势, 优势). Simplification substantially reduced stroke count.

勃.origin: 勃 suggests a surge of strength—flourishing growth or sudden vigor. Compounds like 蓬勃 and 勃然 keep that bursting energy. The phonetic sits left of 力.
勃.history: More literary than basic 力 words, but still familiar via set phrases. Structure is regular.

勇.origin: 勇 pairs phonetic 甬 with 力 to mean courage—strength of spirit in facing danger. Moral bravery and martial valor both attach to the graph. It is a standard virtue term in Chinese ethics.
勇.history: Frequent in 勇敢 and names. Taught as a clear 力-radical phono-semantic character.

勤.origin: 勤 means applying strength steadily—diligence and industriousness. From hard work it also yields “often/frequently” in some compounds. The left phonetic is complex but the 力 cue is obvious.
勤.history: Core moral vocabulary (勤劳, 勤奋). Remains common in education and workplace language.

parts.note: early form may depict a banner or knife-like mark; became prohibitive adverb

勿.origin: 勿 is an old prohibitive meaning “do not,” used especially in classical and formal writing. Its early pictographic identity is debated, but the grammatical function stabilized early. Modern Mandarin prefers 别/不要 in speech, keeping 勿 for signs and literary style.
勿.history: Seen on notices (请勿吸烟) and in classical texts. Short, high-register negation marker.

包.meaning: to wrap; bag; package; to include
包.origin: 包 shows something wrapped inside an embracing stroke—bundle or fetus-in-womb imagery in traditional explanations. From wrapping came bags, packages, inclusion, and hiring/contracting senses. It is also a productive phonetic.
包.history: Everyday noun and verb (包子, 包括). Graphic idea of enclosure remains intuitive.

化.origin: 化 depicts a person and an inverted person—turning about—hence change and transformation. It became a suffix for processes (-化 “-ize”). Chemical and cultural “transformation” senses are widespread.
化.history: Fundamental in philosophy, science, and daily speech (变化, 文化). Structure is a classic teaching ideograph.

北.origin: 北 originally showed two people back to back—turning away—which yielded “to flee/be defeated” and then the compass direction north (the side one turns from the sun in traditional orientation lore). Directional meaning dominates today.
北.history: Primary cardinal direction word. Classical “defeat” sense survives mainly in set expressions.

区.origin: 區/区 refers to a bounded area or category—things sorted into enclosures. District, region, and “distinguish” (区别) all follow. Simplification replaces the complex interior with a simple cross-like mark.
区.history: Standard in addresses and administration (地区, 社区). Also a phonetic in many characters.

parts.note: symbol for complete count of ten

十.origin: 十 is a simple numeral graph for ten—often explained as a complete count marked by a vertical and horizontal stroke. It anchors the decimal system in Chinese writing. Extended senses include “complete” in phrases like 十分.
十.history: Stable from earliest numerals to modern digits’ Chinese counterpart. Also a radical/component in many graphs.

parts.note: early forms link person stroke with ten for thousand

千.origin: 千 denotes one thousand, built as a numeral graph related to ten with an added stroke historically tied to counting people or units. It is a basic large-number unit in East Asian counting. Figuratively it means “many.”
千.history: Unchanged in function across millennia. Appears constantly in dates, money, and hyperbole (千里).

parts.note: depicts a measuring scoop rising/holding grain

升.origin: 升 began as a pictograph of a measuring ladle or scoop for grain, also naming a volume unit. From lifting the measure came “to rise” and “to promote.” Both capacity and ascent senses remain.
升.history: Used for promotions (升职), rising motion, and the liter-like traditional measure. Simplified/traditional forms are essentially the same graph.

parts.note: ox; dividing a cow in half in traditional explanation

半.origin: 半 is classically explained as dividing an ox (牛) with the split mark 八—hence “half.” It became the general fraction “one half” and the prefix semi-. The image of cutting something into two equal parts stuck.
半.history: Basic quantifier in all periods (一半, 半价). Also a phonetic/component elsewhere (判).

parts.note: huà; phonetic cue in simplified analysis
lower remnant of traditional 華's flower form

华.origin: 華/华 originally pictured flourishing flowers—splendor and glory. It became a name for Chinese civilization (中华) and a surname reading Huà. Simplification compresses the elaborate flower graph.
华.history: Highly symbolic culturally; also common in names and “magnificent” compounds (华丽).

协.origin: 協/协 means joining strength to act together—cooperation and concordance. Traditional form emphasized many 力; simplified 协 blends with 办-like strokes. Modern use centers on joint effort and assistance.
协.history: Key in 合作-related terms (协助, 协议). Official and organizational language uses it constantly.

parts.note: simplified from 單, originally a hunting/weapon pictograph

单.origin: 單/单 began as a pictograph related to a hunting weapon or catch, later meaning single, alone, or a simple list. Odd numbers and “bill/form” senses developed from “one” and itemized sheets. Simplification yields the modern open frame shape.
单.history: Everyday in 简单, 单位, and 菜单. Also a common surname (Shàn) with a different reading.

parts.note: related to 買/买 buying; selling as counterpart

卖.origin: 賣/卖 is the counterpart to buying—offering goods for exchange. Traditional forms link to net/wealth components; simplified 卖 aligns visually with 买. The core commercial meaning is stable.
卖.history: Basic commerce verb (卖出, 卖家). Learners often study 买/卖 as a pair.

parts.note: early graph linked to a musical instrument or southern region marker

南.origin: 南 names the southern direction; early forms are often connected with a hanging instrument or regional emblem later fixed as “south.” It is one of the four cardinal direction characters. Extended uses include southern regions and warmth associations.
南.history: Stable directional term from antiquity. Appears in place names and 南方 compounds.

博.meaning: extensive; plentiful; to win; to gamble
博.origin: 博 means broad and abundant—wide learning or plentiful resources. From striving for gain came gambling and “to win/obtain.” Museums (博物馆) reflect the “broad collection” sense.
博.history: Literary virtue term (博学) and modern institutional vocabulary. Also used in card/gaming contexts.

卡.meaning: card; to block; calorie (loan); to wedge

parts.note: down; stuck between up and down

卡.origin: 卡 depicts being stuck between up and down—wedged or blocked. Loan uses include “card” (kǎ) and other phonetic borrowings. The qiǎ reading keeps the “get stuck/checkpoint” sense.
卡.history: Modern life made 卡 ubiquitous for bank cards and SD cards. The spatial “jammed” sense is older and still active.

卧.origin: 卧 means to lie down or recline, with components suggesting a person in a low posture. Bedroom compounds (卧室) and lying postures follow. It contrasts with sitting/standing verbs.
卧.history: Standard in daily and medical language (卧床). Traditional and simplified forms match.

印.origin: 印 shows a hand pressing to make a mark—hence seals and stamping. Printing technology extended the meaning to print and impressions. Abbreviation for India is a modern geographic use.
印.history: From official seals to 印刷 and 印象. The authentication sense remains culturally important.

危.origin: 危 depicts a person in a high, unstable place—danger on a cliff. From physical peril it generalized to any risk or critical state. “High/steep” readings appear in literary descriptions.
危.history: Core safety vocabulary (危险, 危机). Early warning signs and news language use it constantly.

parts.note: food vessel/person facing food in classical analysis (hint notes 口)
