即.origin: The character "即" consists of the radical "卩" (jié, meaning "seal") on the right, indicating a formal or definite action, and a left component associated with approaching (often linked to mouth/food imagery in pedagogical hints), suggesting announcement or readiness. Together, they symbolize something that is approaching or ready to happen. From “approach/be at” it grammaticalized to “namely” and “immediately.”
即.history: Classical connector and modern formal adverb (即将, 立即). Still common in written Mandarin.

parts.note: qù; to go; also semantic for stepping back

却.origin: The character "却" combines the radical "卩" (jié, meaning "seal") on the right, which originally represented a kneeling person, and the phonetic component "去" (qù, meaning "to go") on the left. The character as a whole came to mean "to decline" or "to refuse," which evolved into the modern usage of expressing contrast or opposition. Adverbial “however/but” is now dominant.
却.history: Frequent contrastive adverb in writing and speech. Older “step back/refuse” senses survive in compounds like 退却.

卷.meaning: to roll; scroll; exam paper; volume
卷.origin: 卷 means to roll up—scrolls of bamboo or paper curled into a cylinder. From rolled documents came book volumes and exam papers. Readings juǎn (roll) and juàn (volume/exam) split by sense.
卷.history: Central to traditional book culture and modern tests (试卷). Also a verb for winding and sweeping up.

历.meaning: history; calendar; to experience; to pass through

parts.note: lì; also effort of passing through

历.origin: 歷/历 concerns passing through places or times—experiencing and undergoing. Calendar sense (曆) merged into the same simplified graph in mainland usage. History (历史) is “what has been passed through.”
历.history: Simplification collapsed related traditional characters into 历. Extremely common in education and timekeeping words.

parts.note: wàn; simplified phonetic remnant of 萬 in 厲

厉.origin: 厲/厉 originally related to a whetstone for sharpening—hence severe, harsh, or intense. Moral severity and fierce manner follow the sharpened edge metaphor. Simplification replaces the complex interior with 万.
厉.history: Common in 厉害 and 严厉. Classical tool sense is mostly historical.

parts.note: cliff or shelter; a surface bearing weight
earth; something pressed down to the ground

压.origin: The character "压" combines the radical "厂" (hǎn, meaning "cliff" or "shelter") on the top, indicating a surface or structure, and "土" (tǔ, meaning "earth" or "soil") on the bottom, suggesting something being pressed down to the ground. Traditional 壓 used a fuller phonetic; simplification keeps the pressing-to-earth image. Meanings cover physical pressure, suppression, and stress.
压.history: Ubiquitous in physics and daily speech (压力, 压抑). Simplified form is compact and standard in the PRC.

厚.origin: 厚 describes thickness of earth or walls—substantial depth rather than thinness. From physical thickness came generosity, richness of flavor, and profound kindness. Cliff/earth imagery supports the “layered mass” idea.
厚.history: Common adjective in material and moral praise (厚道, 深厚). Remains a basic dimensional term.

原.origin: 原 originally depicted a spring issuing from under a cliff or slope, hence 'source' and 'plain' (the open ground where water gathers). From the concrete sense of a water-source came the abstract sense 'origin' or 'original.' The modern form keeps the cliff-like 厂 and a reduced spring element.
原.history: Oracle and bronze forms show a cliff with flowing water. Seal script regularized the spring into a form related to 泉. The character remained largely stable into regular script, with 原 as the standard form in both traditional and simplified Chinese.

composition.formula: 厂 + 豆 + 寸 (厨 ← 廚)

厨.origin: 厨 is the simplified form of 廚. The traditional character combines a building/enclosure sense with phonetic and semantic elements related to a kitchen space and vessels for food. It denotes the place where meals are prepared.
厨.history: Traditional 廚 shows a richer structure with 广/厂 and an inner phonetic-semantic complex. Simplification reduced strokes while preserving the kitchen meaning, yielding the common modern form 厨.

去.origin: 去 originally suggested leaving a place: early forms are often analyzed as a person departing from a location (sometimes linked to a lid-like or vessel form above earth). The core sense is movement away—'to go' or 'to leave.' It later also marked past or completed action in some constructions.
去.history: Bronze and seal forms vary, but the 'depart' sense is ancient. Regular script settled as 去, unchanged in simplified Chinese, and it remains one of the most frequent motion verbs.

参.origin: 参 (traditional 參) is commonly explained as related to a constellation or clustered form with 彡 contributing sound. Meanings branched: 'take part' / 'consult' (cān) and the plant name ginseng (shēn), plus 'uneven' in compounds like 参差.
参.history: Seal forms of 參 are more pictorial. Simplification to 参 reduced the upper complex while keeping the multi-reading usage common in modern Mandarin.

又.origin: 又 is a pictograph of a right hand. From the concrete 'hand' it became a grammatical word meaning 'again,' 'also,' or 'and,' and it also serves as a common radical in characters involving the hand or action.
又.history: Oracle-bone 又 clearly shows a hand. As an independent word it drifted into adverbial use early; as a component it remains productive in compounds like 友, 取, and 受.

及.origin: 及 depicts a hand catching up to a person—hence 'to reach' or 'to catch up with.' From physical reaching came the connective sense 'and / as well as,' still common in written Chinese.
及.history: Early graphs show a person pursued or grasped by a hand. Seal and clerical forms compressed the person into the left stroke cluster of modern 及.

友.origin: 友 shows two hands acting together, an image of companionship and mutual aid. The meaning 'friend' (and 'friendly') follows naturally from hands joined in cooperation.
友.history: Oracle and bronze forms already use paired hands. The character stayed stable; traditional and simplified share 友.

双.origin: 双 is the simplified form of 雙, which traditionally showed two birds (隹) under a hand-related or paired structure, meaning 'a pair.' The simplified character uses two 又 (hands) to signal duality.
双.history: Traditional 雙 emphasizes a pair of birds. Mainland simplification replaced that with the transparent 双, now standard for 'pair/double.'

反.origin: 反 shows a hand turning something over against a cliff-like or board-like surface—hence 'to flip' and, by extension, 'opposite,' 'against,' or 'to rebel' in compounds. The core idea is reversal of direction or stance.
反.history: Early forms already suggest a hand reversing an object. The character is identical in traditional and simplified script and remains highly productive (反对, 反映, 造反).

发.origin: Simplified 发 merges two traditional characters: 發 'issue / send forth' (fā) and 髮 'hair' (fà). The launch/develop sense comes from 發; the hair sense is a separate etymon collapsed into one simplified graph.
发.history: Traditional Chinese still distinguishes 發 and 髮. Mainland simplification unified them as 发, so tone and context disambiguate the readings in modern usage.

取.origin: 取 depicts a hand taking an ear—an ancient image linked to seizing a trophy or captive—and generalized to 'take' or 'obtain.' The hand component 又 marks the grasping action.
取.history: Bronze inscriptions already show ear-plus-hand. The form stabilized early and is unchanged in simplified Chinese.

受.origin: 受 shows one hand giving and another receiving (often with a middle element resembling a boat or object being passed). The core meaning is 'to receive,' later also 'to endure' or 'to be subjected to.'
受.history: Seal script makes the dual-hand exchange clearer. Regular 受 kept that structure; simplified Chinese uses the same form.

变.origin: 变 is simplified from 變. The traditional form pairs a complex phonetic (related to 䜌) with 攵 'action,' conveying transformation through action. MeaningHint 'to change; to become' matches the core sense.
变.history: Traditional 變 is stroke-heavy; simplification produced 变 while retaining the change/become meanings across modern Mandarin.

叙.origin: 叙 (traditional 敘/敍) combines a phonetic element with a hand/action component, meaning to set things in order or to narrate in sequence. The storytelling sense ('recount') grows from ordered arrangement of events.
叙.history: Traditional forms use 攴/攵 or 又 with 余. Simplified 叙 is standard in Mainland usage for narrative verbs like 叙述.

叛.origin: 叛 joins phonetic 半 with semantic 反 'turn against,' yielding 'to rebel' or 'to betray.' The 反 element carries the idea of reversing loyalty.
叛.history: The phono-semantic analysis is transparent in seal and later scripts. The character is the same in traditional and simplified Chinese.

口.origin: 口 is a simple pictograph of an open mouth. Beyond the body part, it means any opening or entrance and is one of the most common radicals for speech, eating, and oral sounds.
口.history: Oracle-bone 口 already looks like a mouth square. It has remained essentially unchanged from antiquity through modern script.

古.origin: 古 is traditionally explained as speech (口) that has passed through many generations (十 as 'many'), hence 'ancient' or 'old.' It marks things belonging to former times.
古.history: Early graphs vary slightly, but 古 was established by seal script. The form is identical in traditional and simplified Chinese.

句.origin: 句 originally related to a bent or hooked shape (hence classical gōu 'hook') and to phrasing speech into units. The dominant modern reading jù means a sentence or clause—speech wrapped into a complete unit.
句.history: Seal forms emphasize the wrapping stroke around 口. In modern Mandarin, jù 'sentence' is primary; gōu survives in names and classical compounds.

另.origin: 另 is often analyzed as separating something off—speech or force marking something as distinct—hence 'another' or 'in addition.' Exact oracle origins are less certain than for core pictographs, but the 'separate/other' sense is long-standing.
另.history: The character appears in later standard script as 另 and is unchanged by simplification. It is common in everyday words like 另外 and 另一.

叨.origin: 叨 places the mouth radical with phonetic 刀. As dāo it suggests talkative chatter; as tāo (as in 叨扰) it means to receive another's kindness—both speech-related extensions of oral/social interaction.
叨.history: As a phono-semantic compound it fits the common 口-series pattern. Both readings remain in modern Mandarin, distinguished by context.

只.origin: Simplified 只 covers several traditional uses: adverb 'only' (related to 祇/秖) and the measure word for animals (traditional 隻, a bird in a hand). The modern graph looks like mouth over divided strokes.
只.history: Simplification collapsed 隻 and related 'only' graphs into 只. Tone and grammar distinguish zhǐ 'only' from zhī as a classifier.

叫.origin: 叫 combines mouth with phonetic 丩, denoting calling or shouting with the mouth. It also means 'to be called' (as a name) and can mark causative or passive-like patterns in colloquial speech.
叫.history: The mouth-radical speech verb pattern is classical. Form 叫 is shared by traditional and simplified Chinese.

可.origin: 可 is often explained as approving with the mouth (or as a phonetic-ideographic blend with 丁). The core sense is possibility or permission—'can / may'—and it forms many adjectives of capability (可爱, 可靠).
可.history: Seal script shows the hooked 丁-like stroke beside 口. The character has been stable and extremely frequent from classical through modern Chinese.

台.origin: Simplified 台 stands for traditional 臺 'terrace/platform' and also appears in 颱→台 for typhoon. The platform sense—raised place for standing or performing—is primary in everyday nouns like 舞台 and 平台.
台.history: Traditional Chinese still prefers 臺 for the platform meaning in many contexts. Mainland simplification made 台 the default graph for platform and related senses.

史.origin: 史 originally depicted a hand holding a writing tablet or documents—the official scribe. From the scribe's office came 'historical records' and then 'history' in general.
史.history: Oracle and bronze graphs show a hand with a centered document element. Regular 史 preserves that compact form in both traditional and simplified scripts.

右.origin: 右 shows the right hand (又) with 口; traditionally it also meant to assist (as the right-hand helper). Spatial 'right' became the dominant everyday meaning, paired with 左 'left.'
右.history: Early forms emphasize the hand. Distinguishing 左 and 右 by adding mouth/work elements appears in bronze/seal analysis; modern 右 is stable.

叶.origin: In simplified Chinese, 叶 replaces 葉 'leaf' (plant radical plus phonetic). The simplified graph looks like 口+十 and also continues classical xié 'harmony' senses in limited use; yè 'leaf' is the common modern reading.
叶.history: Traditional 葉 keeps the grass radical. Mainland simplification mapped leaf to 叶, which learners should not confuse with unrelated lookalikes.

号.meaning: number; day of month; call (hào); howl (háo)
号.origin: 号 is simplified from 號, a mouth-related character for calling out or a designation. MeaningHint matches common uses: ordinal number, day of the month, and various 'title/mark' senses; háo is the howling reading.
号.history: Traditional 號 has an extra tiger-like component (虎). Simplification to 号 kept the call/number meanings central to modern Mandarin.

司.origin: 司 depicts authority issuing commands—often analyzed as a person overseeing with the mouth—hence 'to be in charge.' It appears in titles and in 公司 'company,' where the managing sense remains visible.
司.history: Bronze and seal forms already encode official charge. The modern graph 司 is shared across script standards.

叹.origin: 叹 simplifies traditional 嘆/歎, mouth plus a phonetic/semantic complex for exhaling emotion. It covers sighing, lamenting, and admiring exclamations (感叹).
叹.history: Traditional forms retain 堇 or 欠 elements. Simplified 叹 is the Mainland standard for sigh/exclaim vocabulary.

各.origin: 各 originally suggested arriving at a place (a foot approaching an opening/place), then generalized to 'each one' or 'every'—distributive reference to separate items or people. (Batch park hints refer to 公园 and are unrelated to this character.)
各.history: Oracle forms support the 'arrive' analysis. By classical Chinese, 各 was firmly a distributive 'each/every,' unchanged in simplified script.

合.origin: 合 shows a lid fitting onto a mouth or container—perfect closure—hence 'to join,' 'to combine,' or 'to suit.' Measure reading gě (one-tenth of a shēng) is secondary.
合.history: The fitting-lid image is clear in seal script. 合 remains a high-frequency verb and adjective element (合适, 合作, 合适).

同.origin: 同 depicts a covered opening or shared enclosure, suggesting people speaking or acting under one cover—hence 'same' and 'together.' It is fundamental in words of agreement and companionship.
同.history: Early graphs emphasize a shared space. The character is identical in traditional and simplified Chinese.

名.origin: 名 traditionally combines evening (夕) and mouth (口): in the dark one must say a name to identify oneself. The core meaning is 'name,' extending to fame and reputation. (Batch 'ten persons' hint is incorrect for this graph.)
名.history: The dusk-plus-mouth explanation is classical. 名 has been stable since seal script and is unchanged by simplification.

composition.formula: 后 ← 後 (sequence); also 后 queen

后.origin: In simplified Chinese, 后 covers traditional 後 'after/behind' and the royal title 后 'queen/empress.' MeaningHint 'after; later' matches the dominant temporal/spatial sense from 後.
后.history: Traditional writing keeps 後 for 'after' and 后 for the sovereign consort. Mainland use of 后 for both is a major simplification merge.

向.origin: 向 originally depicted a window under a roof—an opening to look out—hence facing a direction or 'towards.' OriginHint matches this window/facing analysis. Extended senses include orientation and preference.
向.history: Oracle forms already suggest a window in a dwelling. The character stayed compact as 向 in later scripts.

吓.origin: 吓 simplifies 嚇: mouth plus a phonetic, for startling someone with a shout or threat. Common reading xià means to frighten; hè appears in 恐吓 'to intimidate.'
吓.history: Traditional 嚇 includes 赫 as phonetic. Simplified 吓 is standard in Mainland Mandarin scare vocabulary.

吗.origin: 吗 is a speech particle formed with mouth radical and phonetic 马. As MeaningHint notes, it marks yes-no questions at the end of a sentence in modern Mandarin.
吗.history: The particle is relatively late colloquial grammar written with this phono-semantic graph; simplified 吗 matches traditional 嗎 minus the horse's extra strokes.

君.origin: 君 shows one who governs and issues orders with the mouth—the ruler or lord. It later became a polite term for a gentleman (君子) and appears in classical second-person address.
君.history: Bronze forms link to official authority. 君 remained prestigious vocabulary from classical texts through modern compounds.

吟.origin: 吟 pairs mouth with phonetic 今 for vocalizing in a drawn-out way—chanting poetry, humming, or moaning. Literary usage favors poetic chanting and intoning.
吟.history: As a classic 口-series phono-semantic, it appears in premodern literature and remains in words like 呻吟 and 吟诗.

否.origin: 否 combines 'not' (不) with mouth (口)—verbal negation or denial. Reading fǒu is 'no / or not'; pǐ (as in 否极泰来) means clogged or bad fortune.
否.history: The composition is transparent from seal script onward. Both readings survive, with fǒu common in formal questions (是否).

吧.origin: 吧 uses mouth plus phonetic 巴. As MeaningHint states, sentence-final ba softens suggestions, requests, or commands. Separately, bā transliterates 'bar' (酒吧).
吧.history: The modal particle is modern colloquial Chinese; the same graph was borrowed for the loanword 'bar.'

听.origin: 听 simplifies traditional 聽, which prominently featured an ear (耳) for listening. MeaningHint 'listen' is exact; the simplified form keeps a mouth-side structure with 斤.
听.history: Traditional 聽 makes the ear semantics obvious. Mainland 听 is shorter but still the default verb for hear/listen.

吸.origin: 吸 places mouth with phonetic 及 for drawing in with the mouth or nose—inhale, suck, or absorb. Extended senses include attracting (吸引).
吸.history: Phono-semantic structure is clear in regular script. Same form in traditional and simplified Chinese.

吹.origin: 吹 shows the mouth with the breath component 欠—air leaving the mouth. Meanings include blowing, playing wind instruments, and colloquial 'boast' or 'break up' (吹了).
吹.history: Seal forms already pair mouth and breath. The character has been stable across script reforms.

吾.origin: 吾 combines phonetic 五 with 口. As OriginHint notes, it became the classical first-person pronoun 'I/my,' especially in literary Chinese, rather than a concrete mouth action.
吾.history: Common in premodern texts (吾辈, 吾等); modern Mandarin prefers 我, but 吾 remains recognizable in set phrases and names.

呀.origin: 呀 is mouth plus phonetic 牙. MeaningHint matches usage as an interjectional or modal particle (surprise, emphasis), and yā can be an exclamation 'ah.'
呀.history: A modern colloquial particle graph following the productive 口 + phonetic pattern; simplified and traditional forms align as 呀.

呆.origin: 呆 is often read as a wooden, blank expression—mouth over wood suggesting stiffness or stupidity. Meanings include dull-witted, dumbstruck, and 'to stay' (呆在).
呆.history: Later popular form; related traditional variants exist for some senses. Mainland standard uses 呆 widely in colloquial adjectives and verbs.

告.origin: 告 traditionally relates to announcing with the mouth, sometimes linked to reporting an ox sacrifice to the spirits. Core modern senses are 'tell,' 'inform,' and 'accuse' (告状).
告.history: Oracle/bronze analyses vary on the sacrificial link, but 'announce/report' is ancient. Form 告 is shared across script standards.

呗.origin: OriginHint is apt: mouth plus 贝 marks speech. As MeaningHint says, sentence-final bei suggests mild command, resignation, or 'that's how it is.' Buddhist bèi relates to chanting (梵呗).
呗.history: Simplified 呗 corresponds to traditional 唄. The modal particle is strongly colloquial northern Mandarin.

员.meaning: member; person engaged in a role
员.origin: 员 simplifies 員, often linked to roundness (圆) and people counted as members of a group. Modern usage centers on roles: 会员, 演员, 员工.
员.history: Traditional 員 is still used outside Mainland norms. Simplified 员 dropped strokes while keeping the 'member' sense.

呢.origin: 呢 pairs mouth with phonetic 尼. As a particle (ne) it softens questions or follows up ('and what about…?'); ní refers to woolen fabric (呢子).
呢.history: Particle use is modern Mandarin grammar written with this phono-semantic character; same graph in traditional/simplified.

周.origin: 周 originally related to denseness or a circuit around fields—hence 'all around,' 'circumference,' and careful thoroughness. MeaningHint 'Zhou' reflects the dynasty/surname; 'week' (一周) is a later calendrical extension.
周.history: Bronze forms connect to the Zhou polity name. Modern senses include week, periphery, and surname, identical across script standards.

味.origin: 味 is mouth plus phonetic 未 for what the mouth perceives—taste, flavor, and by extension smell or interest (味道, 趣味).
味.history: Standard phono-semantic formation visible in seal/regular script; unchanged by simplification.

命.origin: 命 builds on 令 'to order,' reinforced with mouth—issuing a binding command. From a ruler's decree came 'destiny/fate' and 'life' as the allotted span one is ordered to live.
命.history: Closely related to 令 in early script. Classical and modern Chinese keep the fate/life/order cluster of meanings.

和.origin: 和 joins grain phonetic 禾 with mouth—often explained as voices in tune or peaceful accord. MeaningHint 'and' is a major modern conjunction use; older senses center on harmony and mildness (和平, 温和).
和.history: Ancient musical/harmonious senses remain in compounds. Conjunction hé is ubiquitous in modern Mandarin; other readings appear in 暖和 (huo) and response chants (hè).

咒.origin: 咒 (also written 呪) involves mouths and a ritual element for spoken spells—curses or sacred formulas. It covers both malign cursing and religious mantras.
咒.history: Variant 呪 is older in some dictionaries; 咒 is the common modern standard for curse/incantation vocabulary.

咖.origin: 咖 is a modern phono-semantic used mainly to transliterate foreign syllables, especially kā in 咖啡 'coffee.' It has little independent classical meaning outside loan compounds.
咖.history: Created/standardized for phonetic borrowing in modern Chinese; traditional and simplified share 咖.

咚.origin: 咚 uses mouth/sound radical with phonetic 冬 to write the onomatopoeic thud or drumlike 'dong.' It is primarily mimetic rather than a classical content word.
咚.history: Modern colloquial/onomatopoeic graph following 口 + phonetic; common in comics and informal writing.

咬.origin: 咬 places mouth with phonetic 交 for closing the teeth on something—to bite or gnaw. Extended uses include articulating speech clearly (咬字).
咬.history: Transparent phono-semantic compound in regular script; same in traditional and simplified Chinese.

咱.origin: 咱 combines mouth with phonetic 自. MeaningHint 'we; us' matches the inclusive first-person plural common in northern Mandarin (咱们).zá appears in some dialectal or fused readings.
咱.history: Colloquial pronoun written with a late phono-semantic; strongly associated with northern speech styles.

咳.origin: 咳 is mouth plus phonetic 亥. Primary modern sense ké is 'to cough'; hāi is an interjection of mild regret or greeting in some contexts.
咳.history: Medical and everyday 'cough' usage dominates; the graph is shared across script standards.

composition.formula: 戌 + 口 (analysis) / 咸 ← also 鹹

咸.origin: OriginHint notes mouth tasting and phonetic 戌: in simplified Chinese 咸 covers traditional 鹹 'salty' and classical 咸 'all/together.' Everyday xián means salty.
咸.history: Mainland simplification merged salty 鹹 into 咸. Classical 'all' reading jiān survives mainly in literary phrases.

品.origin: 品 stacks three mouths, suggesting many people evaluating or ranking—hence grade, quality, product, and the verb 'to taste/appraise.' The triple-mouth image implies collective judgment.
品.history: The three-mouth structure is ancient and visually stable. Modern compounds include 产品, 品味, and 品质.

哈.origin: 哈 is mouth plus phonetic 合, chiefly onomatopoeic laughter (哈哈) and related exclamations. Other readings appear in dialectal or specialized uses (哈达, etc.).
哈.history: Modern colloquial and loan/transcription uses are common; form identical in traditional and simplified scripts.

响.meaning: sound; make a sound; loud; echo
响.origin: 响 simplifies 響: sound related to echoing or ringing out. It means a sound, to resound, or 'loud/powerful' in effect (影响 'influence' uses related traditional 響).
响.history: Traditional 響 includes 音 'sound.' Simplified 响 keeps phonetic 向 with the mouth radical for acoustic meanings.

哑.origin: 哑 (traditional 啞) marks impaired or silenced speech—mute or hoarse—via mouth plus phonetic 亚. yā appears in some mimetic uses.
哑.history: Simplification replaced 亞 with 亚 inside the character, yielding 哑 as the Mainland standard.

哒.origin: 哒 writes a sharp tapping or hoof-like clack, mouth radical plus phonetic 达. It is mainly onomatopoeic in informal writing.
哒.history: Simplified phonetic 达 (from 達) produces 哒 corresponding to traditional 噠.

哦.origin: 哦 uses mouth with phonetic 我. MeaningHint 'oh' matches the common interjection ò/ó for realization or mild surprise; literary é relates to chanting.
哦.history: Modern interjection usage is frequent in dialogue; the graph is the same in traditional and simplified Chinese.

哪.origin: 哪 adds mouth to phonetic 那 for interrogative 'which/where' (nǎ) and related forms. Sentence-final na is a modal particle variant in some transcriptions.
哪.history: Interrogative use is standard modern Mandarin; traditional/simplified share 哪 (phonetic 那 already simplified in Mainland).

哭.origin: 哭 shows two mouths above a dog—howling mouths—an image of wailing. The meaning is to cry or weep, including figurative lament.
哭.history: Seal forms support the howling analysis. The character is unchanged in simplified Chinese.

唇.origin: 唇 places the mouth radical with phonetic 辰 for the lips as part of the mouth. It is the standard anatomical and figurative term for lip.
唇.history: Phono-semantic structure is clear in regular script; traditional and simplified both use 唇.

唠.origin: 唠 (traditional 嘮) is mouth plus phonetic 劳/勞 for garrulous talk—to chatter or have a long chat (唠嗑 in northern Mandarin).
唠.history: Simplification of the phonetic yields 唠. Strongly colloquial northern usage.

唤.origin: 唤 combines mouth with phonetic 奂 for calling someone to come—summon or call out. Common in 呼唤 and 召唤.
唤.history: Traditional 喚 uses 奐; simplified phonetic 奂 produces 唤.

唬.origin: 唬 puts mouth with tiger phonetic/semantic cue—roaring or scaring like a tiger. Colloquial senses include bluffing and intimidating (吓唬 overlaps with 吓).
唬.history: Transparent composition; used in informal Mandarin for scare/bluff expressions.

唯.origin: 唯 is mouth plus phonetic 隹. Core senses are 'only/alone' (唯一) and, classically, a respectful 'yes' in response. It marks exclusivity in modern compounds.
唯.history: Classical texts use 唯 frequently; modern Chinese keeps it mainly in formal or compound 'only' vocabulary.

商.origin: 商 relates to estimation and exchange inside a structured enclosure—trade and discussion. It names the Shang dynasty, means commerce (商业), and in math means quotient.
商.history: Oracle forms connect to the Shang polity and ritual-economic life. The graph remained important from bronze inscriptions through modern business vocabulary.

啡.origin: 啡 is mouth plus phonetic 非, used almost only in transliterations such as 咖啡 'coffee' and 吗啡 'morphine.' It is not a classical free content word.
啡.history: Modern phonetic loan character; identical in traditional and simplified Chinese.

啦.meaning: (exclamation particle); pull (lā, rare as this graph)
啦.origin: OriginHint matches: mouth radical plus phonetic 拉. MeaningHint describes sentence-final la marking completion, realization, or emotional tone in colloquial speech.
啦.history: A modern Mandarin modal/exclamatory particle written with the productive 口 + phonetic pattern; same form across script standards.

啸.meaning: to whistle; to howl; to scream

parts.note: sù; provides sound in the traditional form 嘯

啸.origin: 啸 is a phono-semantic compound: the mouth radical marks a loud vocal sound, while the right side (from 肅/肃) supplies the phonetic. It originally described a clear whistle or prolonged cry, as of wind through trees or an animal’s howl. The simplified form keeps the mouth radical and a reduced phonetic.
啸.history: Classical texts use 啸 for poetic whistling and natural howls. Modern Chinese retains both literal whistling and figurative cries of emotion or protest.

喂.origin: 喂 combines mouth 口 with phonetic 畏. Early senses center on putting food into the mouth—feeding animals or people. The spoken interjection “hello?” on the telephone is a later colloquial extension of calling out through the mouth.
喂.history: As a verb of feeding it is long-standing; the phone greeting 喂 became widespread with telephony in the twentieth century.

喜.origin: 喜 depicts festive celebration: a drum (壴) above a mouth (口), suggesting music and singing at a joyful occasion. The compound ideograph thus encodes delight rather than a single pictograph of an emotion. From “festive joy” it extended to liking and being pleased.
喜.history: Oracle-bone and bronze forms already link drums and mouths to celebration. It remains a core everyday word for happiness and preference.

嗓.origin: 嗓 pairs the mouth radical with phonetic 桑. It names the throat and by extension the singing or speaking voice. The semantic side points clearly to the oral cavity and sound production.
嗓.history: Common in compounds like 嗓子 and in talk about singers’ voices; a relatively late but transparent phono-semantic graph.

嗜.meaning: to be fond of; to have a craving for
嗜.origin: 嗜 is mouth plus phonetic 耆, originally suggesting a strong taste or appetite expressed through the mouth. It came to mean a habitual fondness or craving—especially for food, but also for habits more generally.
嗜.history: Literary Chinese uses 嗜 for addictions and strong preferences (嗜好). The sense of “craving” remains standard in modern usage.

parts.note: ēn; phonetic representation of the hummed sound

嗯.origin: 嗯 is composed of the mouth radical 口, which indicates that it is related to speech or sound, and 恩 as a phonetic cue for the hummed nasal. It is essentially a written stand-in for the conversational sound of agreement, hesitation, or thinking aloud.
嗯.history: A modern colloquial character for an interjection; tone varies (ēn, ń, ň, ǹ) with nuance. It has little classical pedigree and exists mainly in spoken-style writing.

parts.note: shuò; phonetic; also related to sucking/breath

嗽.origin: 嗽 marks a cough with the mouth radical and a phonetic related to forceful breath (欶). The graph points to an abrupt expulsion of air through the mouth and throat. It is the standard character in 咳嗽.
嗽.history: Long attested in medical and everyday vocabulary for coughing; still the default written form for that meaning.

嘉.origin: 嘉 joins the drum element 壴 (festivity, fine music) with phonetic 加. It originally praised what was fine or auspicious at a celebration, then generalized to “excellent” and “to commend.” The shared festive imagery with 喜 is not accidental.
嘉.history: Frequent in classical eulogy and formal names (嘉奖, 嘉宾). Remains a literary-positive word in modern Chinese.

嘛.origin: The character 嘛 combines 麻 (má) and 口 (mouth), functioning as a modal particle in modern Chinese speech. The mouth radical signals a spoken particle; 麻 supplies the sound. It softens statements that the speaker treats as obvious (“you know…”).
嘛.history: A modern colloquial particle graph; widespread in informal writing and dialogue. Distinct from question particle 吗 despite similar sound.

嘲.origin: 嘲 is mouth plus phonetic 朝, pointing to mocking speech. The semantic radical situates the meaning in talk; the whole came to mean ridicule or satire (嘲笑).
嘲.history: Established in literary Chinese for derision and later in everyday 嘲笑. Also appears in 嘲讽.

parts.note: zī; beak/snout; phonetic and related sense

嘴.origin: The character 嘴 combines the radical 口 on the left, indicating relation to the mouth, and 觜 (zī), which is a phonetic component historically linked to an animal’s beak or snout. Together they name the human mouth and, by extension, speech or a spout-like opening.
嘴.history: 觜 alone once meant beak; adding 口 reinforced the oral sense. 嘴 is now the ordinary word for mouth in spoken Mandarin (嘴巴).

parts.note: dog; often explained as guarding vessels

器.origin: 器 shows four mouth-like openings around 犬 (dog). Traditional explanations treat the mouths as vessels and the dog as a guardian of household utensils, yielding “implement” or “vessel.” From concrete tools it extended to organs, instruments, and figurative “capacity” (成器).
器.history: A high-frequency classical and modern character (器具, 武器, 器官). The four-口 structure is stable from seal script onward.

parts.note: simplified from 頁 (head); center of the traditional form 囂

composition.formula: 口×4 + 页 → 嚣 (簡 囂)

嚣.origin: 嚣 (traditional 囂) stacks many mouths around a head element, vividly suggesting a crowd shouting. The ideograph encodes hubbub and uproar rather than a single spoken word. It also shades into arrogance or bluster (气焰嚣张).
嚣.history: Literary word for din and public clamor; simplified by reducing 頁 to 页 while keeping the mouth cluster.

parts.note: inner coil in modern analysis; originally a spiral

composition.formula: 囗 enclosing a coil → 回

回.origin: 回 began as a pictograph of a spiral or circular turn—motion that comes back on itself. That image of turning around naturally yields “return,” “go back,” and “reply.” The boxed form in modern script still hints at a circuit within an enclosure.
回.history: Extremely common from classical through modern Chinese (回来, 回答). Also used as a measure word for chapters or events.

因.origin: The character 因 consists of the enclosure 囗 outside and 大 inside, representing a person or main point contained within something—symbolizing a cause or reason held inside circumstances. From “rely on / based on” it developed the conjunction “because.”
因.history: Core grammatical and philosophical term (原因, 因为). Seal forms already show a person-like figure inside a border.

parts.note: simplified phonetic stand-in; traditional used 專

composition.formula: 囗 + 才 → 团 (簡 團)

团.origin: 团 is the simplified form of 團, an enclosure around a phonetic. The enclosed shape suggests something rounded or gathered together—a dumpling, a lump, or a group of people. Meanings of “团圆,” “团体,” and “一团” all flow from that cluster image.
团.history: Traditional 團 used 專 as phonetic; simplification replaced the interior with 才. Highly frequent in modern social and military vocabulary.

composition.formula: 囗 + 元 → 园 (簡 園)

园.origin: 园 simplifies 園: a fenced enclosure with a phonetic inside. The enclosure radical marks a bounded planted space—garden, orchard, or park. The idea is land set apart and tended within walls or hedges.
园.history: 園/园 appears widely in place names and compounds (公园, 花园). Simplification swapped 袁 for 元 while keeping 囗.

困.origin: 困 shows a tree 木 locked inside an enclosure 囗—an image of being hemmed in and unable to grow free. From physical confinement it extended to hardship, poverty, and later colloquial “sleepy” (tired as if weighed down).
困.history: Classical sense is straits or siege; modern Mandarin adds 困了 for drowsiness. Still transparent as a compound ideograph.

composition.formula: 囗 + 韦 → 围 (簡 圍)

围.origin: 围 (traditional 圍) places phonetic 韦/韋 inside the enclosure radical, meaning to surround or hem in. The outer box is literally the idea of encirclement; the phonetic guides pronunciation.
围.history: Used for siege, wrapping, and circumference (周围). Simplification reduced 韋 to 韦.

composition.formula: 囗 + 玉 → 国 (簡 國)

国.origin: 国 simplifies 國, which enclosed 或 (territory/armed domain) inside 囗. The simplified form puts 玉 (jade) inside the border, still suggesting a precious realm within bounds—hence “state” or “country.” The enclosure radical remains the semantic core.
国.history: 國/国 is among the most frequent characters. Simplification standardized the 玉 interior in mainland script.

composition.formula: 囗 + 冬 → 图 (簡 圖)

图.origin: 图 is simplified from 圖, historically an enclosure around components related to planning or granaries, later read as a chart within a frame. The boxed shape suits maps, diagrams, and plotted intentions (企图).
图.history: 圖/图 covers pictures, maps, and schemes. Mainland simplification uses a 冬-like interior.

composition.formula: 囗 + 员 → 圆 (簡 圓)

圆.origin: 圆 (traditional 圓) is enclosure plus phonetic 员/員, naming roundness—a coin, a circle, or something complete. The outer 囗 reinforces the circular outline; figurative “圆满” extends roundness to completeness.
圆.history: Also the yuan currency concept historically tied to round coins. Simplification reduced 員 to 员.

圈.meaning: circle; ring; to enclose; pen (for animals)
圈.origin: 圈 puts phonetic 卷 inside 囗, yielding a ring, loop, or fenced pen. The enclosure radical carries the spatial sense; rolling 卷 echoes circular motion. Readings quān (circle) and juàn (pen/sty) share the same graph.
圈.history: Common for circles, social circles, and animal pens. Tone/reading splits by sense in modern Mandarin.

parts.note: mound of earth on the ground line

composition.formula: pictograph of a soil mound → 土

土.origin: 土 is a pictograph of a lump or mound of earth rising from the ground. It names soil, land, and by extension locality or rustic character. As a radical it marks many characters about dirt, building, and geography.
土.history: One of the foundational radicals (Kangxi 32). Semantic range from dirt to territory has been stable since early scripts.

在.origin: 在 is generally analyzed as earth 土 with a phonetic related to 才, meaning to exist or be located somewhere. Being “on the ground” became the basic locative verb and preposition “at/in.” It also marks progressive aspect (正在).
在.history: Among the highest-frequency function words in Chinese. Graphical details of the upper component shifted across seal and clerical forms.

地.origin: 地 pairs earth 土 with phonetic 也 to mean the ground or land beneath us. From physical earth it extended to places, regions, and the structural particle that turns adjectives into adverbials (慢慢地).
地.history: Core vocabulary from classical cosmology (天地) to modern grammar. Reading de for the particle is a later grammaticalization.

场.meaning: a measure word for events, happenings; field; scene

parts.note: yáng; traditional phonetic (場); simplified stroke form

composition.formula: 土 + 昜 → 场 (簡 場)

场.origin: 场 simplifies 場: earth plus phonetic 昜, originally an open threshing ground or plaza. From a physical field it became a scene of activity and a measure word for spells of rain, performances, or events.
场.history: 場/场 is ubiquitous (机场, 市场, 一场雨). Simplification compresses the right side.

圾.origin: 圾 is earth plus phonetic 及, used almost exclusively in the binome 垃圾 for trash. The earth radical suggests discarded matter mixed with dirt. Alone it has little independent use in modern Mandarin.
圾.history: 垃圾 became the standard modern word for garbage; 圾 is learned as the second syllable of that compound.

composition.formula: 土 + 不 → 坏 (簡 壞)

坏.origin: 坏 simplifies 壞, which involved earth and a complex phonetic, originally “to collapse” (walls of earth falling). From ruined structures it generalized to spoiled, broken, or morally bad.
坏.history: 壞/坏 covers decay, damage, and wickedness. The simplified form uses 不 on the right.

坑.meaning: to cheat; to trick; pit; hole
坑.origin: The character 坑 consists of 土 on the left and 亢 on the right. It originally depicted a depression in the ground and now broadly represents any pit or hole. Figuratively, luring someone into a pit became “to cheat” or “to scam.”
坑.history: Literal pits appear in classical and archaeological contexts; slang “坑人” is a lively modern extension.

parts.note: simplified remnant of traditional 臣+又 above 土 in 堅

坚.origin: 坚 simplifies 堅, classically earth under components suggesting grasping or overseeing—firm ground that holds. The meaning is hardness, solidity, and by extension resolute character (坚持).
坚.history: 堅/坚 is standard for firmness physical and moral. Simplification heavily reduced the upper right.

composition.formula: 土 + 文 → 坟 (簡 墳)

坟.origin: The character 坟 consists of 土 and 文, symbolizing a mound of earth used as a grave. Traditional 墳 used a fuller phonetic; the simplified form keeps earth plus 文. It names tumuli and burial mounds.
坟.history: 墳/坟 appears in funerary vocabulary (坟墓). Simplification replaced the complex right side with 文.

垃.origin: 垃 is earth plus phonetic 立, the first half of 垃圾. Like 圾, it points to discarded matter associated with dirt. It is rarely used outside that compound in modern Mandarin.
垃.history: Standardized with 圾 as the written form of spoken lājī for trash.

composition.formula: 龙 + 土 → 垄 (簡 壟)

垄.origin: 垄 simplifies 壟: dragon phonetic over earth, naming the raised ridges between field furrows. From agricultural ridges it extended metaphorically to monopolizing a market (垄断)—controlling a “ridge” of trade.
垄.history: 壟/垄 is agricultural in origin; 垄断 is a key modern economic term.

型.origin: 型 places earth under phonetic 刑, originally a clay mold for casting. From the physical mold it came to mean pattern, type, or model (类型, 型号).
型.history: Classical casting vocabulary broadened into abstract typology; extremely common in technical and everyday modern Chinese.

parts.note: simplified stacked 厶 marks from 壘

垒.origin: 垒 simplifies 壘, traditionally fields stacked over earth—piled earthworks or ramparts. The sense is building up layers of soil into a fortification or base (as in baseball 垒).
垒.history: 壘/垒 covers fortifications and figurative accumulation. Simplification replaces triple 田 with lighter strokes.

埋.meaning: to bury; to hide; to immerse
埋.origin: 埋 is earth plus phonetic 里: to cover with soil. Burial of the dead is the core sense; extensions include hiding, burying feelings, or being buried in work.
埋.history: Stable burial verb from classical through modern Chinese (埋葬, 埋怨 has a related but distinct path).

城.origin: 城 combines earth with phonetic 成, naming the rammed-earth city wall and then the city itself. Early Chinese cities were defined by their earthen fortifications.
城.history: Central political-geographic term (城市, 长城). The wall sense survives in compounds like 城墙.

域.origin: 域 is land 土 plus 或, which itself relates to bounded areas. Together they mean a region or domain—geographic, administrative, or abstract (领域).
域.history: Classical territorial term; productive in modern technical domains (区域, 域网).

基.origin: 基 sets phonetic 其 over earth 土, the soil foundation beneath a structure. From building footings it generalized to any basis or fundamental (基础, 基因 in modern coinages).
基.history: Architectural sense is ancient; abstract “basic” is especially prominent in modern vocabulary.

堆.origin: 堆 is earth plus phonetic 隹, a mound or heap of soil (or anything piled). The earth radical keeps the image of a raised pile.
堆.history: Everyday verb and noun for stacking; also appears in place names and 堆栈.

堵.origin: 堵 pairs earth with phonetic 者, originally a section of earthen wall. Blocking a passage with a wall yielded the verb “to obstruct,” now familiar in 堵车.
堵.history: Classical wall measure and verb; traffic-jam sense is modern and very frequent.

填.meaning: to fill; to stuff; to fill in (a form)
填.origin: 填 is earth plus phonetic 真: to fill a pit or space with soil. Filling blanks on paper (填写) is a natural extension of packing something full.
填.history: From civil engineering and burial fill to paperwork and emotional “filled” states (填补).

境.origin: 境 combines land with phonetic 竟, the edge of a territory. From borders it broadened to environment and circumstances (环境, 处境)—the “land” one finds oneself in.
境.history: Classical frontier term; 环境 is a cornerstone of modern discourse.

墓.origin: 墓 places phonetic 莫 over earth 土, a grave mound. 莫’s old associations with dusk and covering plants poetically suit a burial place covered by soil.
墓.history: Standard funerary term (公墓, 墓地), distinct from but related to 坟.

parts.note: sè; simplified remnant of traditional 嗇 in 牆

composition.formula: 土 + 啬 → 墙 (簡 牆)

墙.origin: 墙 simplifies 牆 (also related to 牆 with 爿): an earthen or timber wall. The earth radical in the simplified form highlights masonry of soil and brick. It names any upright dividing wall.
墙.history: 牆/墙 is the basic word for walls (墙壁, 防火墙). Simplification aligns it with the 土 series.

增.origin: 增 is earth plus phonetic 曾: originally adding soil to raise a mound. From piling up earth it generalized to any increase or augmentation.
增.history: High-frequency modern verb (增加, 增长). The earth image of accretion still fits abstract growth.

墟.origin: 墟 pairs earth with phonetic 虚 (empty), suggesting abandoned earthen mounds or deserted ground. It names ruins and also traditional rural market sites (赶墟).
墟.history: Literary for ruins (废墟); dialectally for periodic markets in South China.

壁.origin: 壁 sets phonetic 辟 over earth, a wall of earth or stone. It covers house walls, cliff faces, and figurative barriers (墙壁, 壁障).
壁.history: Standard architectural and geographic term; also in 壁虎, 壁垒.

士.origin: 士 is an early graph often linked to a standing person or an axe emblem of rank—the male elite who served as warriors and later as scholars. It became the title for the shi class between nobility and commoners.
士.history: Central to Zhou social vocabulary (士农工商) and later the literati ideal; also “soldier” in compounds like 士兵.

composition.formula: 丬 + 士 → 壮 (簡 壯)

壮.origin: 壮 simplifies 壯: a bed/board element 爿 plus 士, portraying a robust adult male. Strength and grandeur (壮大, 壮丽) grow from that physical vigor.
壮.history: 壯/壮 is common for health, youth, and impressive scale. Also the ethnonym 壮族.

声.origin: 声 is the simplified form of 聲, which combined a musical-stone or striking element with 耳 (ear)—sound as something heard. Simplification keeps a truncated upper form. Meanings cover noise, voice, and fame (名声).
声.history: 聲/声 is fundamental vocabulary. The ear component in the traditional form makes the auditory origin explicit.

处.meaning: place; point; to handle; to dwell

parts.note: simplified remnant of traditional 処/處

处.origin: 处 simplifies 處, historically tied to stopping or dwelling in a place (often analyzed with foot/stop components under a shelter-like element). It means a location and the act of dealing with affairs there.
处.history: 處/处 is both noun (到处) and verb (处理). Readings chù and chǔ split by sense.

备.origin: 备 simplifies 備, classically linked to preparing equipment or defenses (often analyzed with a person and a quiver/gear element). The core meaning is readiness and provision.
备.history: 備/备 is ubiquitous (准备, 设备). Simplification drastically reduced stroke count.

复.meaning: again; to return; complex; to recover

parts.note: not always written; traditional 復 has 彳
simplified merger of 復/複 senses

复.origin: 复 is the simplified graph covering traditional 復 (return/again, with step radical 彳) and often 複 (layered/complex). Going back over a path yields “again” and “recover”; layering yields “complex.”
复.history: Mainland script merges several traditional forms into 复 (复习, 复杂, 恢复). Taiwan/HK often keep distinctions.

外.origin: 外 is commonly explained as evening 夕 plus a divination mark 卜—activity or signs outside the main dwelling/daytime sphere. It means outer, foreign, and external as opposed to 内.
外.history: Basic spatial and social contrast term from classical Chinese onward (国外, 意外).

多.origin: 多 stacks two 夕 (evening) components, an ideograph of plurality—“evening upon evening” as many. It became the basic adjective for large quantity.
多.history: Extremely frequent quantifier. The doubled-夕 analysis is the standard schoolbook etymology.

parts.note: yì; phonetic remnant in the form

夜.origin: 夜 builds on 夕 (evening) with a phonetic related to 亦, naming the night. The evening radical anchors the time-of-day meaning.
夜.history: Core temporal noun (夜晚, 半夜). Closely tied to 夕 in the writing system.

composition.formula: 多 + 句 → 够 (also 夠)

够.origin: 够 (also written 夠) combines 多 “many” with phonetic 句, suggesting having many enough to meet a need. Sufficiency and “reaching” a standard (够得着) both derive from that abundance.
够.history: Everyday adjective/verb of adequacy. Traditional 夠 places 多 on the right; mainland often uses 够.

parts.note: upper grass-like cover in traditional form

夢.origin: 夢 is the traditional form of 梦, built around evening 夕 and components often linked to covered eyes or a darkened setting—images of dreaming at night. It names both night dreams and aspirations.
夢.history: 夢/梦 is literary and everyday (做梦, 梦想). Simplified 梦 reduces the upper complexity.

大.origin: 大 depicts a person standing with arms spread wide—the visual of largeness. From physical size it extended to greatness in rank, degree, and importance.
大.history: Fundamental adjective and radical element in dozens of characters (天, 太, 夫). Stable pictograph since oracle bones.

天.origin: 天 adds a line above 大 (a person), marking the sky over the head. From the heavens it also means day (as a unit of time under the sky) and weather. The hint sense “day” is the common temporal reading.
天.history: Cosmological key term (天地) and everyday time word (今天). One of the earliest and most frequent characters.

太.origin: 太 is 大 with an extra mark, intensifying “big” into “greatest” or “excessive.” It functions as “too/very” and in titles of supreme status (太平, 太阳).
太.history: Differentiated from 大 early on; remains a high-frequency adverb and name element.

parts.note: hairpin/crest mark of an adult male

夫.origin: 夫 shows 大 (person) with a horizontal stroke often explained as a hairpin—the mark of an adult man. It means husband, man, and laborers in compounds (渔夫).
夫.history: Classical male adult term; modern 丈夫 still means husband. Also a classical function word in some texts.

失.origin: 失 is typically explained as a hand letting something slip away—loss through dropping. From physical loss it covers missing, failing, and errors (失败, 失望).
失.history: Core verb of loss and failure from classical through modern Chinese.

头.origin: 头 simplifies 頭, which combined a phonetic with 頁 (head radical). It names the anatomical head and extends to tops, starts, leaders, and a versatile noun suffix.
头.history: 頭/头 is among the most frequent nouns. Simplification yields the compact 头 shape.

夺.meaning: to seize; to snatch; to contend for
夺.origin: 夺 simplifies 奪, traditionally a bird under a hand-related component—snatching a bird away. The sense is to seize or strive to take by force.
夺.history: 奪/夺 covers robbery, contention, and dazzling “seizing” attention (夺目). See also traditional form 奪 in this batch.

奇.origin: 奇 is often analyzed as 大 plus phonetic 可, something unusual about a person or thing—odd, rare, or marvelous. Readings qí (strange) and jī (odd number) share the graph.
奇.history: Literary and colloquial for wonder and oddity (奇怪, 奇迹). Mathematical “odd” is a specialized reading.

奉.meaning: to offer; to receive respectfully; to believe
奉.origin: 奉 depicts presenting something with both hands—an offering to a superior. From tribute and respectful giving it extended to receiving orders and holding beliefs (信仰-related 信奉).
奉.history: Formal classical verb of service and offering; survives in 奉献, 奉命.

奏.meaning: to play (music); to memorialize the throne; to achieve
奏.origin: 奏 originally involves advancing or presenting—reporting to the ruler—and performing music before him. Both “play an instrument” and “submit a memorial” preserve the idea of presenting upward.
奏.history: Court memorials and musical performance are classical senses; 演奏 remains standard for concerts.

composition.formula: 大 over covering element → 套

套.origin: 套 suggests a large outer covering slipped over something—a case, sheath, or matching set. From physical covers it extended to sets of things and formulaic routines (套路).
套.history: Productive modern noun/verb (手套, 配套). Relatively late popular graph compared with classical core characters.

奠.meaning: to establish; to pour a libation; foundation
奠.origin: 奠 shows a wine vessel set down—pouring a libation to spirits. Settling the offering firmly became “to establish” or “lay a foundation” (奠定).
奠.history: Ritual libation sense is ancient; abstract founding sense is common in modern formal Chinese.

奥.origin: 奥 simplifies 奧, the deep inner southwest corner of a house where offerings were made—hence hidden and profound. Abstract “mystery” and “depth” (深奥) follow from that inner recess.
奥.history: 奧/奥 also appears in transliterations (奥运). Classical architectural-religious sense underlies the figurative one.

奪.meaning: to seize; to snatch; to contend for
奪.origin: 奪 is the traditional form of 夺: a bird (隹) in the upper complex with 寸 (hand), vividly “snatching a bird.” It means to seize, deprive, or strive to win.
奪.history: Full traditional form used in Taiwan/HK print; mainland simplified counterpart is 夺. Same semantic range.

女.origin: 女 is a pictograph of a kneeling woman, one of the clearest early human figures in the script. It means woman, female, and daughter, and serves as the radical for many female-related characters.
女.history: Oracle-bone forms already show the kneeling posture. Extremely frequent as both word and radical.

奴.origin: 奴 joins woman 女 and hand 又, traditionally explained as a captive or servant under another’s hand. It names slaves and menial dependents; later it appears in humble first-person and compounds like 奴隶.
奴.history: Reflects ancient social hierarchy; modern use is mostly historical or figurative (奴役).

她.origin: 她 is a modern phono-semantic creation: female radical plus phonetic 也, parallel to 他. It specifically marks the third-person feminine pronoun “she/her,” distinguishing gender in writing.
她.history: Popularized in the early twentieth century under Western pronoun influence; now standard in vernacular Chinese.

好.origin: 好 places woman and child side by side—an image of what is cherished and wholesome. From that family ideal it became the general word for “good,” and as a verb “to like” (hào).
好.history: Among the most frequent characters. Dual readings hǎo/hào are ancient and still productive.

parts.note: woman; compliance imagery in traditional glosses
mouth; saying / going along with words

如.origin: 如 combines 女 and 口; traditional explanations link it to following or resembling—going along as spoken or as a woman following instruction. It became the standard word for “like/as” and conditional “if” (如果).
如.history: High-frequency classical and modern function word (如此, 例如). Exact early motivation of 女+口 is debated, but the comparative sense is long stable.

parts.note: zhuāng; traditional form uses side phonetic

妆.origin: 妆 (traditional 妝) is a phono-semantic compound: the woman radical 女 marks feminine appearance, while the right side supplies the sound zhuāng. It originally referred to applying makeup or dressing up. The sense extends to costume, adornment, and theatrical makeup.
妆.history: Seal and clerical forms already show a woman component with a phonetic side. Traditional 妝 was simplified to 妆 by reducing the right-hand phonetic.

妈.origin: 妈 is a common word for mother, written as a phono-semantic compound with 女 (woman) and 马 (mǎ) as phonetic. It belongs with other kinship terms that use the woman radical. Colloquial mā contrasts with more formal 母.
妈.history: The character became widespread in vernacular writing; traditional 媽 simplifies the horse component to 马, yielding 妈.

妥.origin: 妥 depicts a hand (爪) over a woman (女), conventionally explained as securing or settling someone—hence proper, suitable, done. Early glosses link it to arranging affairs so they are safe and fitting. From settled come senses like reliable and satisfactory.
妥.history: Oracle and bronze forms already suggest a hand above a kneeling figure. The modern graph keeps the claw-hand atop 女 through seal and regular script.

始.origin: 始 combines 女 with phonetic 台. Classical explanations often connect beginning with birth or the start of life, so the woman component is semantic. It means to commence and also first or originally.
始.history: Attested from early bronze and seal script as a woman-plus-phonetic structure. Form has remained relatively stable into regular script.

姓.origin: 姓 joins 女 and 生. Early Chinese surnames were often matrilineal in lore, so the woman radical is meaningful; 生 (birth) doubles as phonetic and a hint of lineage. It denotes one's clan name or surname.
姓.history: Present in classical texts from early Zhou onward. The composition is already clear in seal script and carries through to the modern form.

委.origin: 委 shows grain (禾) over a woman (女). Traditional readings include grain bent under weight and a woman entrusted with affairs—hence entrust, appoint, commission. Related senses include crooked, indirect, and committee.
委.history: Seal forms already stack 禾 above 女. Meanings branched in classical Chinese from physical bending to administrative entrustment.

姨.origin: 姨 is phono-semantic: 女 marks female kinship and 夷 provides the sound yí. It chiefly means mother's sister, and by extension wife's sister or informal auntie.
姨.history: A later kinship graph built on familiar radical-plus-phonetic pattern; traditional and simplified forms match aside from general stroke norms.

姻.origin: 姻 pairs the woman radical with phonetic 因. It refers to marriage and affinity by marriage (as in 婚姻). The semantic focus is the marital bond rather than the wedding act alone.
姻.history: Used in classical compounds for marriage ties. Structure is stable from seal-derived regular script into modern use.

姿.origin: 姿 uses 女 for the semantic realm of feminine form and bearing, with 次 as phonetic. It means posture, looks, or elegant manner, later generalized to style and attitude.
姿.history: Appears in literary Chinese for graceful bearing. The phono-semantic layout is retained in modern regular script.

parts.note: woman; sometimes read as constrained figure

威.origin: 威 is commonly analyzed as a weapon/axe component (related to 戌) with 女, conveying intimidating force or majestic power. It means authority that inspires awe, and by extension prestige and threat.
威.history: Bronze and seal forms show the weapon element enclosing or beside a figure. The sense of martial or moral awe is already classical.

娇.origin: 娇 (traditional 嬌) is phono-semantic: 女 plus phonetic 乔/喬. It describes something tender, cute, or spoilt—often a delicate woman or child, also soft beauty of flowers.
娇.history: Traditional 嬌 uses 喬; simplification replaces it with 乔, giving 娇, while keeping the woman radical.

娈.origin: 娈 (traditional 孌) is a literary graph for graceful or lovely, especially of a youth or woman. It shares phonetic material with related graphs like 恋. The woman radical marks the aesthetic/human sense.
娈.history: Traditional 孌 is complex; simplified 娈 reduces the upper phonetic tangle. Now mostly literary or in names.

娘.origin: 娘 combines 女 with phonetic 良. It means mother in many dialects and also young woman or bride (新娘). Address forms like 姑娘 grew from the young-woman sense.
娘.history: Common in vernacular from medieval times. Form is essentially unchanged between traditional and simplified scripts.

娱.origin: 娱 (traditional 娛) uses 女 plus phonetic 吴/吳. It means to amuse or divert, and as a noun, recreation (娱乐). Early senses include joyful company.
娱.history: Traditional 娛 simplifies the 吳 component to 吴, producing 娱.

婆.origin: 婆 is phono-semantic with 女 and phonetic 波. It denotes an older woman, grandmother, or husband's mother, and appears in compounds like 老婆.
婆.history: Stabilized in regular script as woman beside 波; widely used in vernacular kinship vocabulary.

婚.origin: 婚 joins 女 with 昏 (dusk). Phonetic 昏 also evokes dusk ceremonies of traditional weddings. The character means to marry and marriage itself (婚姻).
婚.history: Classical texts already use 婚 for marriage rites. Composition is transparent from seal-influenced regular forms onward.

媒.origin: 媒 uses 女 plus phonetic 某. Originally a go-between arranging marriages; by extension any medium or intermediary (媒体).
媒.history: Marriage-matchmaker sense is ancient; modern media is a semantic extension. Graph form has been stable.

嫌.origin: 嫌 pairs 女 with phonetic 兼. It means to dislike, mind, or suspect, and as a noun a grudge or cause for offense.
嫌.history: Literary and legal Chinese use 嫌 for suspicion and discontent. Phono-semantic structure persists in modern script.

嫩.origin: 嫩 places 女 beside a phonetic often linked to 敕. It describes tender flesh, young plants, or soft colors—anything not yet toughened. Also inexperienced.
嫩.history: A later regular-script form; traditional and simplified share the same graph in most standards.

parts.note: infant with large head and body

子.origin: 子 is a pictograph of an infant, often shown with a large head and swaddled body. Core meanings are child and son; extended uses include seed, egg, and honorific master (孔子). It is also the first Earthly Branch.
子.history: Clear in oracle-bone script as a baby figure. Simplified strokes through bronze, seal, and regular script while keeping the infant outline.

孔.origin: 孔 is often explained as 子 with a stroke marking an opening—hence hole, aperture. It is also the surname of Confucius (孔子). Related senses include peacock (孔雀) via compound words.
孔.history: Early forms vary; seal and clerical scripts settle the child-plus-mark shape used today.

字.origin: 字 shows a child (子) under a roof (宀), linked to bearing and naming children at home. It came to mean written graphs and to write, and also a courtesy name taken in adulthood.
字.history: Bronze and seal forms already combine roof and child. The shift from name/breed to written character is well established by Han times.

存.origin: 存 is traditionally analyzed with a phonetic related to 才 and semantic 子, suggesting life that continues or is preserved. Meanings include exist, remain, and deposit/store.
存.history: Seal script shows the modern components in essence. Abstract senses of existence and storage developed in classical usage.

季.meaning: season; youngest; end of a period
季.origin: 季 stacks grain (禾) over child (子). It meant the youngest of brothers and the last month of a season, then generalized to season (四季). Surname use is also common.
季.history: Attested early as youngest; seasonal sense is classical. Form is stable from seal to regular script.

孤.origin: 孤 combines child radical 子 with phonetic 瓜. Primary meaning is orphan (child without father, classically); extended to lonely, solitary, and humble self-reference by rulers.
孤.history: Phono-semantic layout is clear in seal-derived forms. Emotional and political extensions are ancient.

学.origin: 学 (traditional 學) depicts learning: traditional forms show hands arranging teaching aids under a roof over a child. The idea is a child receiving instruction. Meanings cover learn, study, and school.
学.history: Oracle and bronze ancestors are complex teaching scenes. Clerical/regular 學 was simplified to 学 by truncating the upper component.

宇.origin: 宇 places phonetic 于 under roof 宀. Originally the eaves or expanse of a building; metaphorically the vault of heaven, hence universe in 宇宙.
宇.history: Classical poetry already uses 宇 for vast space. Structure unchanged into modern script.

守.origin: 守 shows a hand/inch component (寸) under a roof (宀)—keeping watch at a post. Core senses are guard, observe, and abide by rules. Official titles like 太守 grew from this.
守.history: Early bronze usage already means to defend a place. Form is consistent through seal and regular scripts.

安.origin: 安 depicts a woman under a roof, a classic image of domestic security and calm. Meanings include peaceful, safe, content, and to install or settle.
安.history: Extremely common from oracle-bone contexts of safety onward. The roof-plus-woman shape is iconic across script stages.

完.origin: 完 puts phonetic 元 under 宀. The roof suggests something intact within walls—complete, whole, finished. Also to pay in full in some uses.
完.history: Seal script already shows roof over 元. Completion senses dominate modern Mandarin.

宗.origin: 宗 shows an altar component 示 under a roof—the ancestral temple. It means lineage, ancestor, school of thought, and to take as model.
宗.history: Central in bronze inscriptions about kinship cult. Form remains roof over 示 into modern writing.

官.origin: 官 depicts a building associated with administration—hence government official. Extended to sense organs (五官) and public/official matters.
官.history: Frequent in bronze administrative vocabulary. Modern form preserves the roofed structure through seal and regular script.

宙.origin: 宙 pairs roof 宀 with phonetic 由. Classically it points to extended time or the temporal aspect of the world, paired with 宇 (space) in 宇宙.
宙.history: Literary graph mainly known via 宇宙. Composition is stable in regular script.

定.origin: 定 places 正 under 宀. Being upright under a roof suggests something settled and fixed—decide, determine, stable, and calm (安定).
定.history: Common from classical Chinese onward. Seal forms already show the roof-plus-正 arrangement.

宜.origin: 宜 is linked to offerings or arrangements under a roof that are fitting—hence suitable, proper, and should. Also used in names and set phrases (事宜).
宜.history: Oracle and bronze forms relate to sacrificial appropriateness. Modern graph keeps roof over a 且-like element.

实.origin: 实 (traditional 實) originally shows valuables (貫, strings of cowries) under a roof—wealth made concrete, hence solid, real, and fruit. Extended to honest and indeed.
实.history: Traditional 實 was simplified by replacing the complex interior with 头, yielding 实, while keeping 宀.

审.origin: 审 (traditional 審) concerns careful examination. Traditional form has a complex interior under 宀; simplified 审 uses 申 under the roof. Meanings include investigate and judicial trial.
审.history: Simplification replaced 審's interior with 申. Legal and investigative senses are classical and modern.

客.origin: 客 puts phonetic 各 under roof 宀—someone who comes to the house, a guest. Extended to traveler, customer, and certain person types (刺客).
客.history: Well attested in classical Chinese. Form stable from seal script into modern writing.

宣.origin: 宣 shows 亘 under 宀, associated with a royal hall for announcements. It means to proclaim, publicize, or diffuse (宣传).
宣.history: Bronze inscriptions already use related forms for royal declaration. Regular script preserves roof over 亘.

室.origin: 室 places phonetic 至 under 宀. It denotes an inner room or chamber, and by extension household or wife's chambers in classical usage.
室.history: Common in early texts for rooms of a dwelling. Composition clear across seal and regular scripts.

害.origin: 害 is often explained as damage occurring under a roof—injury, calamity, or to harm. Exact early depiction is debated, but the negative sense is consistent in classical Chinese.
害.history: Appears early with meanings of injury and misfortune. Modern form matches seal-derived regular structure.

parts.note: pig; domestic animal under the roof

家.origin: 家 shows a pig (豕) under a roof (宀)—a household keeping livestock, the archetypal home. Meanings cover house, family, and specialist -ist/-er (科学家).
家.history: Oracle-bone and bronze forms already depict an animal under a roof. One of the most stable and frequent graphs in Chinese writing.

容.origin: 容 places 谷 under 宀. A contained space suggests capacity: to hold, appearance/looks, and to tolerate (容忍).
容.history: Classical senses of containing and countenance are both early. Form is stable in regular script.

宾.origin: 宾 (traditional 賓) means honored guest. Traditional form is more complex; simplified 宾 uses a reduced lower part. Related to hospitality vocabulary.
宾.history: Traditional 賓 simplified to 宾 in mainland orthography. Guest sense is ancient.

寂.origin: 寂 combines roof 宀 with phonetic 叔. A still house suggests silence and solitude—quiet, lonely, desolate.
寂.history: Literary Buddhist and poetic usage reinforced the stillness sense. Graph form unchanged in simplified script.

密.origin: 密 is built from phonetic 宓 (itself 宀+必) plus 山, suggesting thickly wooded or close-set mountains. From density come intimate closeness and secrecy (秘密). The mountain element reinforces the idea of something tightly packed or hard to penetrate.
密.history: Seal forms support the mountain-plus-phonetic analysis. Secrecy and density senses are both classical.

寞.origin: 寞 puts phonetic 莫 under 宀. Like 寂, it evokes an empty dwelling—lonely, still, desolate, often paired as 寂寞.
寞.history: Mainly literary; composition transparent in regular script. Simplified form identical to traditional for this graph.

察.origin: 察 places phonetic 祭 under 宀. It means to scrutinize, investigate, or perceive clearly (观察). Official titles like 监察 use the investigative sense.
察.history: Classical administrative vocabulary. Structure roof + 祭 is stable historically.

寰.origin: 寰 uses a large phonetic under 宀 for a vast enclosed realm—the world or a wide domain (人寰). Literary rather than colloquial.
寰.history: Retained in literary Chinese and place/name compounds. Traditional and simplified forms match.

对.origin: 对 (traditional 對) involves hands responding or matching—opposite, correct, pair, and the preposition toward/regarding. Simplified form keeps a hand-like left and 寸.
对.history: Traditional 對 was simplified to 对 by drastically reducing the left side. Core facing/responding senses are ancient.

寺.origin: The character 寺 consists of the radical 寸 (cùn, meaning inch or hand) at the bottom, which originally indicated a small unit of measure, combined with 土 (tǔ, meaning earth) on the top. Historically, it referred to a government office, but over time it came to denote a temple or place of worship.
寺.history: Early meaning court office shifts in medieval Chinese to Buddhist temple. Form 土 over 寸 is already standard in regular script.

导.origin: 导 (traditional 導) is to lead along a path: traditional form uses 道 plus 寸 (hand). Meanings include guide, conduct, and transmit (导引, 领导).
导.history: Simplification cuts 導 down to 导. Leadership and guidance senses span classical and modern Chinese.

封.origin: 封 depicts planting boundary markers or handling a jade tablet with the hand (寸)—to enfeoff land or seal something closed. Meanings include confer a title, seal, and measure for letters.
封.history: Bronze inscriptions use 封 for granting fiefs. Envelope and stamp senses are later but natural extensions.

将.origin: The character 将 originally depicted a hand holding a shield, symbolizing leadership or bringing something forward. It now broadly represents future actions or intentions, as well as the act of leading or bringing. It also reads jiàng for general.
将.history: Traditional 將 simplified the left 爿 to 丬, giving 将. Verbal about-to and military general readings both remain current.

小.origin: 小 is a pictograph of small particles or a tiny object—often explained as three grains of sand or a similar minute mark. It means small, young, or minor.
小.history: Oracle-bone forms already show three short strokes. The shape simplifies slightly but remains recognizable through all script stages.

少.origin: 少 builds on 小 with an added stroke, marking few or less, and the reading shào for young. It contrasts quantity (few) with size (small).
少.history: Closely related to 小 from early scripts. Two modern readings shǎo/shào specialize the senses.

尖.origin: 尖 stacks 小 atop 大: something large below tapering to a small point above—hence tip, sharp, and pointed. The image is progressive narrowing toward the peak.
尖.history: A transparent associative compound in regular script; used for physical points and figurative acuity.

尚.origin: 尚 is linked to aiming upward or esteeming what is high—hence to value, and adverbial still/yet. Also appears in surnames and compounds like 高尚.
尚.history: Early forms relate to a window/hall facing upward. Abstract esteem and still senses are classical.

尝.origin: 尝 (traditional 嘗) concerns tasting with the mouth—try by tasting, and by extension once or ever. Simplified 尝 reduces the complex traditional top.
尝.history: Traditional 嘗/嚐 simplified to 尝 in mainland usage. Experiential have-ever sense is common in classical and modern Chinese.

parts.note: hand with a mark; something unusual

尤.origin: 尤 is often explained as a hand with an extra mark—something peculiar or outstanding. Senses include especially, fault/blame, and remarkable.
尤.history: Early graphs vary; seal script yields the modern hooked form. Adverbial especially is very frequent today.

尬.origin: 尬 is almost only used in 尴尬 (awkward, embarrassed). The 尤 side belongs with words of limping or uneven going; 介 is phonetic. Alone it is rare.
尬.history: Specialized binome character; form is the same in traditional and simplified sets for this graph.

就.origin: 就 combines 京 (high mound/capital) with 尤. Original sense is to approach or move toward a high place—hence come to, accomplish, and the grammatical then/at once.
就.history: Grammaticalization into a high-frequency connective is late classical to medieval. Character form has long been 京+尤.

尴.origin: 尴 pairs with 尬 in 尴尬. Traditional 尷 uses a more complex phonetic; simplified 尴 uses 监. The binome means awkward or embarrassed.
尴.history: Traditional 尷 simplified the phonetic to 监. Bound morpheme outside the set phrase.

尸.origin: 尸 pictographically shows a person lying down or a crouched figure, used for corpse and as a radical for body, dwelling, and related meanings. Ritual personator of the dead is an early cultural sense.
尸.history: Oracle forms depict a bent human figure. As a radical it heads many characters (屋, 尼, 尺, etc.).

尺.origin: 尺 marks a measured span on a body-like outline—the traditional Chinese foot (chi). It also means ruler and, by extension, small amounts in idioms.
尺.history: Ancient measure character; form stabilizes early. Still used for the chi unit and measuring tools.

尼.origin: 尼 shows a person component with 匕. Senses include to approach (classical) and Buddhist nun (比丘尼). Also common in transliterations (尼龙).
尼.history: Early near/stop senses; Buddhist nun is a later loan usage via Sanskrit. Form is simple and stable.

尽.origin: 尽 (traditional 盡) originally relates to emptying a vessel—using up contents—hence exhaust, finish, and all. Simplified 尽 is a drastic reduction of 盡.
尽.history: Traditional 盡 simplified to 尽. Completive and exhaustive senses are both classical and modern.

尾.origin: 尾 attaches hair (毛) to a body radical (尸)—the animal's tail. Extended to the end of anything and classifier for fish.
尾.history: Clear associative compound from early scripts. Meaning end is a natural metaphor.

局.origin: 局 is explained as a constrained space or office—hence bureau, situation, and a round/game (棋局). Also means narrow or constrained in some compounds.
局.history: Administrative office and abstract state of affairs both develop early. Form is compact in regular script.

屁.origin: 屁 uses body radical 尸 with phonetic 比. Literal meaning is flatulence; colloquially it dismisses something as nonsense (放屁).
屁.history: Vernacular character; structure is transparent phono-semantic in modern script.

层.origin: 层 (traditional 層) means layered storeys of a building. Traditional phonetic is 曾; simplified 层 uses 云 under 尸. Extended to abstract layers and classifiers for layers.
层.history: Traditional 層 simplified to 层. Architectural and figurative layer senses are both common.

居.origin: 居 combines 尸 with phonetic 古. It means to live at a place, residence, and occupy a position. Related to 住 but more formal/literary.
居.history: Frequent in classical Chinese for dwelling. Composition unchanged into modern writing.

屈.origin: 屈 places phonetic 出 with 尸—a body bent or forced out of shape. Meanings include bend, yield, and wrongful treatment.
屈.history: Classical moral and physical senses. Form is stable; surname Qu also uses this graph.

屋.origin: 屋 uses the 尸 dwelling radical with phonetic 至. It means house or room, slightly more the building structure than 家 (household).
屋.history: Common residential term from early texts. Seal-derived regular form matches today's graph.

parts.note: complex phonetic interior (now looks partly like 共 strokes)

展.origin: 展 shows a body radical with a complex phonetic interior, linked to unfolding clothes or stretching out. Meanings include open out, exhibit, and put to use (发展). The image is of something spread wide.
展.history: Clerical and regular scripts fix the modern outline. Exhibition sense is a natural extension of spread out.

属.origin: 属 (traditional 屬) means to belong, be subordinate, or connected; also category. Simplified 属 shortens the traditional form.
属.history: Traditional 屬 simplified to 属. Affiliation senses are longstanding.

屠.origin: 屠 pairs 尸 with phonetic 者. It means to slaughter animals or massacre people; also butcher as a noun in compounds (屠夫).
屠.history: Harsh classical verb of killing. Phono-semantic structure is clear in regular script.

山.origin: 山 is a pictograph of mountain peaks—typically three joined summits. It means mountain or hill and appears in countless place names.
山.history: One of the clearest oracle-bone pictographs. The three-peak outline survives with only minor stylization into modern script.

岁.origin: 岁 (traditional 歲) originally relates to the planet Jupiter's cycle and the harvest year—hence year and years of age. Simplified 岁 is a much reduced form.
岁.history: Traditional 歲 simplified to 岁. Age-counting and calendar-year senses remain primary in modern Mandarin.

岔.origin: 岔 combines 山 (mountain, terrain) with 分 (divide; also phonetic). The idea is a place in the landscape where a path or stream splits. From mountain forks it generalized to any divergence or digression.
岔.history: Attested as a later phono-semantic form for branched routes. Seal and clerical forms already show mountain above or beside 分; the modern regular script keeps that reading.

岛.origin: The character 岛 includes 山 (mountain, land) and 鸟 (bird), suggesting a remote landmass birds fly to—an island. The mountain radical marks solid ground amid water; the bird element supplies sound and the sense of a place reached by flight.
岛.history: Traditional 島 places bird above mountain; simplified 岛 keeps the same structure in a more compact form. Bronze and seal graphs already link bird and mountain imagery for islets.
