敲.history: Classical and vernacular Chinese both use 敲 for striking lightly or persistently. Figurative uses include 敲诈 (“extort”) and 敲门砖.

整.meaning: whole; tidy; put in order; exactly

parts.note: upright, correct; putting things in proper order

整.origin: 整 combines a phonetic related to 敕 with 正 (“upright/correct”). The idea is arranging things so they are orderly and complete. Meanings range from “tidy up” and “整洁” to “whole” and “exactly (整数).”
整.history: Widely used from Han texts onward for regulation and completeness. Remains extremely common in modern Mandarin.

數.origin: 數 is the traditional form of 数. 婁 provides the sound and 攵 the sense of manual enumeration. From counting arose the senses “number,” “several,” and literary shuò “repeatedly.”
數.history: Standard in traditional-script regions and classical editions. Mainland China uses the simplified 数 in everyday writing.

parts.note: early form shows a person with patterned chest markings or intersecting strokes

文.origin: 文 began as a pictograph of decorative markings or tattoo-like patterns on the body. It later generalized to pattern, writing, and civil culture. It contrasts with martial force and names literature, language, and refinement.
文.history: One of the most culturally loaded characters in Chinese. Oracle-bone forms already show crossed pattern strokes; later it anchors words like 文化 and 文字.

斗.meaning: dipper; unit of volume (dǒu); fight; struggle (dòu)

parts.note: ladle or dipper shape; later also used for fighting via traditional 鬥 merger in simplification

斗.origin: As dǒu, 斗 is a pictograph of a ladle or measuring dipper and a traditional volume unit. The fighting sense dòu largely continues traditional 鬥 (“contend”), which mainland simplification merged into 斗.
斗.history: Astronomy kept 北斗 (Big Dipper). Vernacular “fight” uses the dòu reading; learners must track both lineages.

料.origin: 料 shows grain (米) measured with a dipper (斗), originally meaning to measure provisions. From assessing amount it extended to material and to anticipate. Related compounds such as 料理 keep the sense of handling or managing.
料.history: Classical usage emphasizes measuring and estimating. Modern Chinese keeps both concrete 材料 and abstract 料想.

parts.note: simplified left from 㡭/絲-related cutting of threads in 斷

断.origin: 断 simplifies 斷, which depicts cutting connected strands with an axe (斤). The graphic idea is severing continuity. Meanings include physical breaking, interrupting, and making a firm decision (判断).
断.history: Seal forms emphasize axe and severed links. Simplified 断 keeps 斤 and a reduced left side.

斯.meaning: this; then; (literary demonstrative); used in transliteration

parts.note: axe; original sense related to splitting/cutting

斯.origin: 斯 originally related to splitting with an axe (斤) with 其 as phonetic. In classical Chinese it grammaticalized into a demonstrative “this” and a connective “then.” Modern use is literary or in names and loanwords.
斯.history: Frequent in classical prose (如斯). Today mainly appears in set phrases and transliterations (斯文, 阿富汗).

parts.note: qīn; phonetic (from 辛/亲 lineage in analyses)
axe; cutting fresh wood—hence newly made

新.origin: 新 traditionally depicts chopping wood with an axe (斤), yielding fresh timber—hence “new.” The left side functions phonetically (linked to 辛/亲). The meaning broadened from newly cut to anything novel or recent.
新.history: Core adjective from earliest texts. Anchors compounds like 新闻, 创新, and 新年.

parts.note: early forms suggest a tool, plow, or framed direction marker; later 'square/side'

方.origin: 方 is an ancient graph whose early forms are debated (implement, frame, or directional sign). It came to mean “square,” “side/direction,” and by extension “method” (方法) and adverbial “just.” Spatial and methodological senses coexist.
方.history: Extremely productive: 方面, 地方, 方案. Also a classical adverb of time.

施.origin: 施 combines a banner-related radical (㫃) with phonetic 也. Early senses involve spreading or setting out (as a flag is unfurled), then “to apply,” “to implement,” and “to bestow” (施舍).
施.history: Classical verb of enacting policy or kindness. Modern usage includes 施工 and 实施.

parts.note: people following; a band on the move

旅.origin: 旅 shows a banner (㫃) with people following, originally a military contingent under a standard. From “troop on the move” it generalized to travel and tourists (旅行, 旅客).
旅.history: Bronze inscriptions already use 旅 for army units. Civilian travel senses dominate modern Mandarin.

parts.note: related phonetic element (often cited as 疋/足 lineage)

旋.origin: 旋 associates a banner radical with a phonetic foot-related element, suggesting turning or circling. Core meanings are revolving, returning, and “presently.” A separate reading xuàn covers whirling or machining.
旋.history: Classical Chinese uses 旋 for swift return. Scientific and everyday compounds include 旋转 and 旋律.

parts.note: banner of a kinship or military group
arrow; shared arms of a clan band

族.origin: 族 depicts a banner with an arrow, symbolizing a kin group mustered under one standard and armed together. It means clan, then ethnicity (民族) and broader classes of beings or things.
族.history: Central to kinship and ethnic terminology. Modern compounds include 家族 and 种族.

parts.note: full traditional form; dance-related pictograph origin debated

无.origin: 无 is the simplified form of 無, historically linked to a complex graph sometimes associated with dancing figures. That form was later borrowed or reinterpreted as “not have.” It is the basic negative of possession and existence opposite 有.
无.history: Philosophical texts (especially Daoist) elevate 無/无. Mainland writing uses 无; traditional regions keep 無.

parts.note: person turning away after eating; done

既.origin: 既 shows a food vessel and a figure turning aside, picturing a meal already finished. From “completed eating” it grammaticalized to “already,” “since,” and correlative 既…又….
既.history: A staple classical and modern function word. Remains frequent in written Chinese connectors.

parts.note: circle with a mark; the sun

日.origin: 日 is a pictograph of the sun—a circle with an inner stroke. By metonymy it means “day” and appears in countless time words. As a radical it marks weather, light, and temporal meanings.
日.history: Among the earliest oracle-bone graphs. Unchanged in essence from antiquity to modern script.

旧.origin: 旧 simplifies 舊, whose traditional structure involves a bird-related phonetic complex. That graph was historically tied to the sense old or former. The meaning contrasts with 新: previous, used, or long-standing.
旧.history: Common in 旧社会, 依旧, and everyday “used/old.” Simplified form is standard in mainland China.

parts.note: marks the upper/beginning part of the day

早.origin: 早 places a mark above the sun, signaling the sun still low in the sky. That image yields the meaning morning, and thus early. It covers early in the day and early relative to an expected time.
早.history: Stable meaning from classical to modern Chinese. Greets the day in 早上好 and 早餐.

composition.formula: 日 + 寸 ← 時 (日 + 寺)

时.origin: 时 is the simplified form of 時, originally 日 plus phonetic 寺. The sun radical situates the graph in the realm of days and seasons; the whole means time, hour, or occasion.
时.history: Fundamental temporal noun and adverbial. Simplified 时 is used in mainland orthography; 時 elsewhere.

明.origin: 明 joins sun and moon, the two great luminaries, to mean brightness and clarity. Extended senses include understanding (明白) and the Ming dynasty name.
明.history: Iconic associative compound taught in every primer. Oracle forms already combine sun and moon motifs.

parts.note: often linked to a lizard/chameleon or sun-and-rays motif of change

易.origin: 易 is an ancient graph commonly connected with a changing creature such as a lizard, or with solar imagery. From that came the senses change and exchange. A parallel sense easy, as in 容易, became equally central, and the Book of Changes is named 易经.
易.history: Philosophically charged via the Yijing. Everyday Mandarin uses both “easy” and “change/trade” readings of the same graph.

parts.note: celestial light; sun radical for heavenly bodies

星.origin: 星 combines 日 (celestial brightness) with phonetic 生. It names stars in the sky and, by metaphor, famous people (明星). Early writing sometimes showed multiple dots for starlight.
星.history: Astronomy and folklore both feature 星. Modern media extended it to pop idols.

映.origin: 映 pairs sun (日) with phonetic 央, depicting light cast onto a surface. Meanings include reflecting, mirroring, and screening images (电影 literally “electric shadow”).
映.history: Literary Chinese favors scenic reflection. Film and media vocabulary later boosted its frequency.

是.origin: 是 originally combined sun and uprightness to mean this, correct, or right. It later grammaticalized into the copula “to be” in modern Mandarin. Older demonstrative and affirmative uses still survive in set phrases.
是.history: Shift from classical demonstrative/affirmative to modern copula is a major syntactic change. Still means “yes/right” in answers.

parts.note: traditional form with 頁 and silk/sun complex for conspicuousness

显.origin: 显 simplifies 顯, a graph emphasizing something made conspicuous (often analyzed with sun/silk elements and 頁). It means to appear clearly, to display, or to be illustrious.
显.history: Common in 明显, 显示, and 显然. Simplified orthography shortens the complex traditional form.

時.origin: 時 is the traditional form of 时: sun radical plus phonetic 寺. It denotes time, seasons, and occasions—the measured flow marked by the sun’s cycle.
時.history: Standard in traditional-script printing. Mainland simplified counterpart is 时.

晃.origin: 晃 includes 日 (sun) on the left and 光 (light) on the right, symbolizing shimmering or unsteady movement of light, which later extended to general swaying or shaking. Readings distinguish dazzle (huǎng) from sway (huàng).
晃.history: Descriptive verb in vernacular for rocking motion and flashing glare. Appears in 摇晃 and 晃眼.

晒.meaning: to sun; to dry in the sun
晒.origin: 晒 includes 日 (sun) on the left, indicating connection to sunlight, and 西 (west) on the right as phonetic. It originally referred to drying or exposing something to the sun, and still means to sun-dry or get a tan.
晒.history: Everyday household and agricultural verb. Internet slang also uses 晒 for “showing off” posts.

晓.origin: 晓 simplifies 曉: sun plus phonetic 堯/尧. It names daybreak and extends metaphorically to “knowing” or “being clear about” something (晓得).
晓.history: Poetic for dawn; colloquial for understanding in southern Mandarin-influenced speech.

晕.meaning: dizzy; faint (yūn); halo; rings around sun/moon (yùn)

parts.note: sun; optical halo or light-related sensation

晕.origin: 晕 (trad. 暈) places phonetic 軍/军 beside 日. It first described a halo around sun or moon, then the sensation of spinning or fainting—light and dizziness linked metaphorically.
晕.history: Medical and everyday words include 头晕 and 晕车. Astronomical 日晕 keeps the older halo sense.

晚.origin: 晚 combines sun with phonetic 免 to mark the later part of the day. From evening it extended to the general sense late. It pairs with 早 in the day’s cycle.
晚.history: Standard for 晚上 and 晚餐. Also figurative lateness (晚了).

普.origin: 普 is traditionally explained as sunlight spreading widely, hence universal or general. The upper element relates to things arrayed together. The 日 component reinforces the idea of all-pervading light.
普.history: Key in 普通, 普遍, and 普通话. Abstract “general” sense dominates modern use.

景.origin: 景 pairs sun with phonetic 京, suggesting a brightly lit prospect—scenery or a view. Extended meanings include circumstances (情景) and admiration (景仰).
景.history: Landscape literature made 景 central. Tourism and photography keep it highly frequent.

晰.origin: 晰 places 析 beside 日: light that lets one discern details. It means clear or distinct, especially of understanding or articulation (清晰).
晰.history: Mostly bound in compounds rather than used alone. Popular in modern descriptive prose.

晴.origin: 晴 combines sun with phonetic 青 to mean a clear, cloudless sky. It is the everyday weather term opposite 阴.
晴.history: Stable meteorological vocabulary from classical poetry to weather forecasts (晴天).

parts.note: zhī; know; supplies sound and sense of knowledge

智.origin: 智 builds on 知 (“know”) plus 日, suggesting knowledge made clear—wisdom and intelligence. It names both innate smarts and cultivated insight.
智.history: Philosophical and educational keyword (智慧, 智商). Closely tied to 知 historically.

暂.origin: 暂 (trad. 暫) uses phonetic 斬/斩 with 日 to mean a brief stretch of time—temporary or provisional. The sun radical situates it among temporal words.
暂.history: Common adverb 暂时. Formal writing preserves the temporary/interim sense.

暖.origin: 暖 places phonetic 爰 beside 日, linking solar heat to physical and emotional warmth. It covers mild temperature and a warm manner.
暖.history: Everyday climate and comfort vocabulary (暖和, 温暖). Also figurative kindness.

parts.note: sun; light realm—here its absence implied

暗.origin: 暗 combines 日 with phonetic 音 to mean dim or dark from insufficient light. By extension it covers secret or underhanded action, as in 暗示 and 暗中. The sun radical places it among light-related graphs, here marking the absence of brightness.
暗.history: Antonym of 明 in many contexts. Literary and colloquial uses both thrive.

parts.note: hands/shared action elements in traditional analyses
lower water-like strokes in some forms; fierce outpouring

暴.origin: 暴 traditionally depicts intense exposure—sun and forceful elements—yielding “fierce,” “sudden,” and “to expose” (暴露). Violence and cruelty are later dominant senses (暴力).
暴.history: Classical Chinese already uses 暴 for harshness and suddenness. Modern compounds stress force and disclosure.

parts.note: guǎng; phonetic and hint of breadth

曠.origin: 曠 joins 日 with phonetic 廣, suggesting a wide, empty stretch under open sky—vastness, wasteland, or leaving something unused (曠課). Mainland simplified form is 旷.
曠.history: Literary landscape term and verb of neglect. Traditional script retains 曠.

曲.origin: 曲 pictographs something bent or crooked, giving the reading qū for curved or unjust. The musical sense qǔ for song or melody is an extended reading of the same graph. Both readings remain active in modern Chinese.
曲.history: Both readings remain active: 弯曲 vs. 歌曲. Classical texts play on moral “crookedness.”

parts.note: originally 'change/replace'; also night watch; comparative gèng is grammaticalized

更.origin: 更 originally depicted the act of replacing or changing, but in modern usage it is commonly used as a comparative term indicating something greater or an increase in degree (gèng). The older gēng reading still means “to change” or a night watch.
更.history: Grammaticalization produced today’s high-frequency adverb 更. Classical gēng senses survive in 更改 and 打更.

parts.note: often linked to a steamer/cooker graphic; later grammaticalized

曾.origin: 曾 began as a pictograph commonly tied to a cooking steamer, then was borrowed for grammatical “once; ever” (céng) and kinship “great-” (zēng), plus a surname. Aspectual 曾经 is the dominant modern use.
曾.history: Classical particles and lineage terms both employ 曾. Tone distinguishes adverb from surname/kin readings.

parts.note: daybreak/day; a turn or period of duty

替.origin: 替 consists of elements analyzed as 旦 (sunrise/daybreak) and 夫 (man), symbolizing replacing or taking someone’s place for a stretch of time. The core meaning is substitution or standing in for another.
替.history: Traditional Shuowen-linked analyses also stress paired figures. Modern verbs include 代替 and 替换.

parts.note: speech/say element in upper structure of some analyses

最.origin: 最 is tied to 取 (“take”) with an upper component, originally suggesting taking the foremost portion. From that image came the sense of utmost degree. In modern Mandarin it is the standard superlative marker meaning “most.”
最.history: Became the standard superlative adverb in Mandarin (最好, 最后).

會.origin: 會 depicts gathering—elements suggesting coming together and speaking—so “meeting” and “association.” Extended senses include ability (会) and accounting (會計, kuài). Mainland simplifies to 会.
會.history: Institutional and modal uses exploded in modern Chinese. Traditional 會 remains in Taiwan/Hong Kong print.

parts.note: crescent moon; also means moon; calendar month from lunar cycle

月.origin: 月 is a pictograph of the crescent moon. Because months were counted by lunar cycles, it also means “month,” the sense highlighted in everyday calendar use. As a radical (often looking like ⺼) it marks flesh/body parts.
月.history: Oracle-bone moon graphs are unmistakable. Timekeeping and astronomy keep both “moon” and “month” alive.

有.origin: 有 shows a hand holding meat (月/肉), the classic image of possession meaning to have. It negates with 无 or 没. The graph underpins everyday existence and ownership expressions.
有.history: Among the highest-frequency verbs. Philosophical contrasts of 有/无 are ancient.

parts.note: two strings of cowries or twin moon-like units; companion set

朋.origin: 朋 originally depicted paired strings of cowrie currency or twin units, suggesting a matching set—hence companions and friends. 朋友 lexicalized the social sense.
朋.history: Early economic graphic shifted to kinship/friendship vocabulary still used daily.

parts.note: phonetic right side; also boat/hand nuances in old forms

服.origin: 服 relates clothing to the body (月/肉) with a phonetic right component. From garments it extended to “wear,” “submit/obey,” official service, and taking medicine.
服.history: Semantic range is wide but historically connected through fitting oneself to roles, garb, or regimen.

parts.note: person standing upright to look afar

望.origin: 望 traditionally shows a person looking toward the moon or into the distance—gazing, hoping, or expecting. Full-moon day was also called 望. The meaning spans look toward and long for.
望.history: Poetic and prosaic staple (盼望, 望见). Astronomical calendrical sense survives in classical dating.

期.origin: 期 pairs phonetic 其 with 月, anchoring time spans counted by moons—periods, deadlines, and expectations (期待). A rare jī reading appears in anniversary terms.
期.history: Administrative and everyday scheduling vocabulary (日期, 学期) made it indispensable.

parts.note: tree with extra upper branches; growth not yet complete

未.origin: 未 depicts a tree whose upper foliage is still developing. From that unfinished growth comes the meaning not yet. It is also the eighth earthly branch in traditional calendrics.
未.history: Negator 未 competes with 没 in written style. Branch use continues in zodiac and time names.

parts.note: mark at the treetop; the tip

末.origin: 末 marks the top of a tree (木), indicating the tip or end—opposite 本 (root). Extended senses include final phase and fine powder (粉末).
末.history: Classic teaching pair with 本. Remains common in 周末 and 末尾.

本.meaning: root; origin; book; classifier for books

parts.note: mark at the base; the root

本.origin: 本 highlights the root of a tree, meaning origin, foundation, or one’s own. Later it named bound volumes. It also became a classifier for books.
本.history: Philosophical 本末 and modern 本来/课本 show the semantic spread from root to text.

术.meaning: art; technique; method (shù); a plant name (zhú)

parts.note: traditional: 行 + 术 phonetic; technique/road of practice

术.origin: 术 simplifies 術, which places a phonetic inside 行 (“walk/go”), suggesting a practiced way or technique. Modern 术 covers arts, skills, and methods (技术).
术.history: Mainland simplification dropped the 行 envelope. Traditional 術 persists outside the PRC.

机.origin: 机 simplifies 機: wood radical plus phonetic 幾/几. Originally a wooden trigger or loom mechanism, it broadened to machines, aircraft, and abstract “opportunity” (机会).
机.history: Industrial vocabulary exploded the “machine” sense. 危机 and 机会 keep the pivotal/chance meanings.

parts.note: traditional has 殳 striking component; violent action

杀.origin: 杀 is the simplified form of 殺, whose traditional right side 殳 (weapon/hand with stick) marks violent striking. The meaning is to kill or do deadly harm, with figurative extensions like “reducing” (杀价).
杀.history: Simplification truncates the phonetic-semantic complex. The verb remains blunt and common.

parts.note: traditional complex form for mixed gathering of things

杂.origin: 杂 simplifies 雜, a graph for things gathered in a jumble—mixed, miscellaneous, impure. Everyday senses cover clutter, variety, and non-specialized mix (杂志).
杂.history: Simplified form is compact; traditional 雜 remains in some regions. High frequency in modern prose.

parts.note: wood; originally a balance weight/scale related sense

权.origin: 权 simplifies 權, historically a wooden sliding weight on a balance. From weighing it developed the senses authority and rights. Political and legal compounds such as 权利 and 权力 dominate modern use.
权.history: From scale-weight to abstract power is a classic semantic shift. Simplified 权 is mainland standard.

材.origin: 材 joins wood with phonetic 才, first meaning usable timber, then materials in general and human talent (人材). Resource quality links the concrete and figurative senses.
材.history: Parallel to 才 in “talent” talk. Industrial 材料 is ubiquitous today.

束.origin: 束 shows branches tied with a loop—binding into a bundle. Meanings include a bunch, to tie, and to restrain (拘束).
束.history: Classifier-like and verbal uses continue (一束花, 结束 historically related via 束).

条.meaning: (used for long, thin objects, a road, a dog, etc.)

parts.note: traditional: 攸 + 木; long thin wooden strip

条.origin: 条 simplifies 條, originally a long thin wooden branch or strip. It became a major classifier for elongated items—roads, fish, dogs, news items—and also means “clause” or “strip.”
条.history: Classifier use is central in modern Mandarin pedagogy. Traditional 條 appears in older texts.

来.origin: 来 simplifies 來, originally a pictograph of wheat. That graph was later borrowed phonetically for the verb come. Direction toward the speaker is the core modern sense, with many aspectual compounds such as 起来 and 下来.
来.history: Loan from the grain graph is a textbook rebus case. One of the most frequent motion verbs.

杯.origin: 杯 pairs wood with phonetic 不, naming a wooden drinking vessel. The sense later covered cups and glasses of any material. By further extension it also names championship trophies (奖杯).
杯.history: Material shifted from wood to ceramic/glass while the graph stayed. Classifier 一杯 remains basic.

松.origin: 松 names the pine via wood plus phonetic 公. A separate but homophonous development yields “loose/relax” (放松), possibly via softness or loosening associations overlapping the same graph in modern writing.
松.history: Botanical 松树 is ancient; “loosen” senses are common in vernacular Mandarin.

板.origin: 板 joins wood with phonetic 反 to mean a flat wooden board or plank. Extended uses include stiff manner, printing plates, and school blackboards (黑板).
板.history: Carpentry sense is primary; modern tech keeps 主板, 看板-type compounds.

极.origin: 极 simplifies 極: wood plus phonetic 及, originally the ridgepole or highest beam. From that height came extremity, pole, and the adverb extremely. Geographic poles and polar concepts follow the same image.
极.history: Adverbial 极了 is colloquial; scientific 北极/电极 keep the pole sense.

parts.note: gōu; contributes sound (构 is simplified from 構)

构.origin: 构 is the simplified form of 構. The traditional character combines 木 (wood) with a phonetic component related to assembling beams, pointing to wooden construction. The idea of joining timber pieces extended to constructing plans, sentences, and abstract structures.
构.history: Seal and clerical forms emphasize wood plus a hand/assembly-related phonetic. Modern simplified 构 keeps the wood radical and a shorter phonetic. Core senses of build, compose, and structure remain.

析.origin: 析 depicts an axe (斤) applied to wood (木): splitting timber. From physical cleaving it came to mean analyzing by dividing a whole into parts. The metaphor of cutting apart remains central to words like 分析.
析.history: Bronze and seal forms show wood beside an axe blade. The character stayed visually stable into modern script and kept both literal and figurative senses.

枕.meaning: pillow; to rest the head on

parts.note: yín/yóu; phonetic, associated with leaning or sinking

枕.origin: 枕 combines wood with a phonetic that suggests reclining or sinking the head. Early pillows were often wooden blocks, so the wood radical fits the original object. The character covers both the pillow and the act of resting one’s head.
枕.history: Attested from seal script onward as a wooden headrest. Material culture shifted to softer pillows, but the wood radical remained. Verb use (to pillow / rest on) is classical and still literary.

林.origin: 林 places two 木 side by side to show a cluster of trees—more than one tree, less dense than 森. It is a classic associative compound: repeating the tree graph multiplies the meaning into woods or a grove.
林.history: Oracle and bronze forms already use doubled trees. The shape has been stable for millennia and remains a high-frequency nature and surname character.

parts.note: fruit hanging on a tree (田-like top over 木)

果.origin: 果 originally pictured fruit on a tree: a rounded or field-like mass above a tree trunk. From the concrete ‘fruit’ it extended to outcome or result—what a situation ‘bears.’ Adverbial use (‘as expected; indeed’) grew from the sense of a realized result.
果.history: Early graphs show fruit clearly on a tree; later forms flattened the fruit into a 田-like top over 木. Simplified and traditional share the same modern shape.

枝.meaning: branch; twig; classifier for stick-like things

parts.note: zhī; also means branch/support, reinforcing sense

枝.origin: 枝 pairs the wood radical with 支, which is both phonetic and semantically close (‘branch; prop’). Together they specify a tree branch or twig. The character also became a classifier for long, slender objects.
枝.history: Seal forms already show wood plus 支. Usage for botanical branches and measure-word senses both appear early in classical Chinese and continue today.

枯.origin: 枯 uses wood/plant as meaning and 古 as sound to denote a dried or withered tree. From vegetation it generalized to anything dried out, depleted, or lifeless in tone (e.g. 枯燥).
枯.history: Phono-semantic structure is clear from seal script. Literary Chinese often contrasts 枯 with living green growth; modern compounds keep that dryness metaphor.

染.meaning: to dye; to stain; to catch (a disease)
染.origin: Traditional analysis treats 染 as water plus plant matter and ‘nine’ dipping—immersing cloth in dye again and again. The core image is coloring by soaking. From staining cloth it extended to infection and moral or cultural ‘contamination.’
染.history: Seal forms already combine water and plant elements. Dyeing technology kept the character productive; figurative senses of infection and influence are classical and modern.

parts.note: wood that can bend; pliant plant matter

柔.origin: 柔 shows pliant wood (木) with 矛 as phonetic, pointing to something bendable rather than brittle. Softness of young wood or flexible branches became the model for gentle temperament and soft materials.
柔.history: Attested in seal script as a wood-related phono-semantic. Classical texts pair 柔 with 刚 (hard/firm); that contrast still frames modern usage.

parts.note: wood; originally a kind of raft or wooden stake
dàn; approximate phonetic in later analysis

查.origin: 查 originally related to wooden stakes or rafts (a wood-based object). Through phonetic and semantic shift it came to mean examining or looking into something carefully. Modern primary sense is to check or investigate.
查.history: Early meanings include wooden markers and place names; bureaucratic and scholarly ‘investigate’ senses rose later. Simplified and traditional share the same form 查.

parts.note: wood; tree used as a marker
simplified remnant of 票 phonetic in 標

标.origin: 标 is simplified from 標 (木 + 票). Originally a wooden marker or treetop used as a signpost. From physical marks it broadened to standards, labels, and titles (目标, 标准).
标.history: Traditional 標 keeps 票 as phonetic; simplified 标 shortens the right side to 示. Both still center on marking and criteria.

栏.origin: 栏 (traditional 欄) is a wooden railing or barrier, with wood as meaning and a phonetic matching lán. From fences and balustrades it extended to newspaper columns and form fields—bounded ‘slots’ of content.
栏.history: Traditional 欄 uses 闌 as phonetic; simplified 栏 uses 兰. Senses of enclosure and printed column both remain common.

parts.note: simplified phonetic standing in for 尌 in 樹

树.origin: 树 is simplified from 樹, which combines wood with a phonetic/semantic element related to planting or standing upright. The core meaning is a tree; verb uses include to plant or establish (树立).
树.history: Traditional 樹 is older; mainland simplified 树 replaces the right side with 对. Meaning ‘tree’ is stable across scripts.

parts.note: wood; originally wooden stocks or a wooden enclosure

校.origin: 校 originally involved wooden restraints or a wooden enclosure for comparing/aligning things (hence jiào ‘to collate; proofread’). The school sense (xiào) developed from places of training and disciplined learning associated with that checking/ordering idea.
校.history: Classical Chinese keeps both readings: military/school xiào and textual collation jiào. Modern Mandarin still splits them by context (学校 vs 校对).

parts.note: tree; originally a kind of oak
yǔ; phonetic; feathers also suggest lively motion

栩.origin: 栩 once named a type of oak (wood + feather phonetic). In literature, reduplicated 栩栩 came to mean vividly alive—as if fluttering with life—famously in Zhuangzi’s butterfly dream. Standalone use is rare; the vivid sense dominates.
栩.history: Botanical sense faded; literary 栩栩如生 fixed the ‘lifelike’ reading for modern learners.

样.origin: 样 is simplified from 樣 (木 + 羕/related phonetic). It pointed to a wooden model or pattern for copying. From physical samples it broadened to appearance, manner, and type (样子, 这样).
样.history: Traditional 樣 is longer on the right; simplified 样 uses 羊. Everyday Mandarin relies heavily on this character for ‘way/kind/look.’

核.meaning: pit (of fruit); nucleus; to check thoroughly
核.origin: 核 names the hard pit or stone inside fruit, hence anything central and hard: nucleus, core. Verb sense ‘to examine thoroughly’ (核对, 审核) treats checking as getting to the core.
核.history: Phono-semantic from seal times. Scientific ‘nucleus’ and administrative ‘verify’ are later extensions that remain transparent from ‘core.’

根.meaning: root; basis; classifier for long thin objects
根.origin: 根 is the plant root: wood/plant meaning plus 艮 sound. Roots anchor and feed the plant, so the character came to mean foundation or source. As a measure word it counts stick-like items.
根.history: Stable phono-semantic since seal script. Metaphorical ‘root cause’ and measure-word uses are both well established in modern Chinese.

格.meaning: grid; pattern; standard; personality; to hinder
格.origin: 格 began as a wooden lattice or framed compartment. From squared frames it meant pattern, rule, and standard; from blocked frames, ‘to obstruct.’ Personality sense (性格) treats character as a patterned frame.
格.history: Classical uses include shelves, rules, and investigation (格物). Modern compounds like 合格 and 风格 keep the ‘standard/pattern’ thread.

案.origin: 案 is a low wooden table or desk (安 sound + wood). Documents laid on the table became ‘cases’ and files; plans submitted for decision became proposals (方案). Legal and office senses grow from the desk as workspace.
案.history: Furniture sense is oldest; bureaucratic ‘case/record’ dominates modern usage. Structure (安 over 木) is unchanged in simplified script.

parts.note: dāng; simplified phonetic (from 當 in 檔)

档.origin: 档 (traditional 檔) was a wooden crosspiece or frame that held things in place. Shelving for documents led to ‘file; archive,’ and ranked slots led to ‘grade/level’ (高档).
档.history: Simplified 档 shortens 當 to 当. Office and quality-grade senses are the everyday modern readings.

parts.note: qiáo; phonetic (from 喬 in 橋)

桥.origin: 桥 is simplified from 橋: wood plus a phonetic meaning tall/raised, fitting a raised wooden span over water or a gap. The meaning has stayed tightly focused on ‘bridge,’ literal and metaphorical.
桥.history: Traditional 橋 uses 喬; simplified 桥 uses 乔. Engineering and idioms (桥梁) keep the spanning metaphor.

parts.note: simplified remnant of upper components in 夢

梦.origin: 梦 is simplified from 夢, traditionally analyzed with night (夕) and elements suggesting the eyes or bed of sleep. It denotes the dream experience and, by extension, aspirations (梦想).
梦.history: Oracle/bronze forms relate to sleep and night; clerical and later forms stabilize 夢. Simplified 梦 compresses the upper complex into a grass-like top over 夕.

parts.note: shū; phonetic (often abbreviated in analysis as 㐬-related)

梳.origin: 梳 is a wooden comb: wood meaning plus a phonetic close to shū. Verb use ‘to comb’ and figurative ‘to sort out’ (梳理) follow from drawing teeth through hair or tangled matter.
梳.history: Everyday implement character from early imperial texts onward. Wood radical recalls traditional wooden combs.

检.meaning: to inspect; to check; to restrain

parts.note: qiān; simplified phonetic (from 僉 in 檢)

检.origin: 检 (traditional 檢) involved wooden labels or book-slips used to seal and examine documents. From checking seals and texts it became general inspection and self-restraint (检点).
检.history: Traditional 檢 uses 僉; simplified 检 uses 佥. Modern compounds (检查, 检验) dominate daily use.

棍.origin: 棍 is a wooden rod or cudgel (wood + 昆 sound). From the weapon/tool it slangily labeled a ruffian or swindler (骗子棍类). The physical stick sense remains primary.
棍.history: Later imperial and vernacular texts fix both the stick and pejorative person senses. Form is the same in simplified script.

森.origin: 森 stacks three 木 to show a thick stand of trees—denser than 林. The multiplied tree graph is an iconic associative compound for deep forest and, by extension, solemn or imposing atmosphere (森严).
森.history: Present from early script stages with triple trees. Remains a transparent nature character and a common surname element in compounds like 森林.

棱.origin: 棱 names the sharp edge or ridge of wood (and later of any solid). The wood radical recalls squared beams; the phonetic 夌 supplies the sound. Geometric and optical compounds (棱角, 三棱镜) build on that edge sense.
棱.history: Also written 稜 in traditional contexts. Modern simplified usually uses 棱 for the edge meaning.

植.origin: 植 combines plant/wood with 直 (straight/upright) as phonetic, fitting the act of setting a plant upright in soil. It covers planting, vegetation (植物), and figurative establishment.
植.history: Clear phono-semantic from seal script. Agricultural and biological vocabulary keep it highly productive.

parts.note: wooden mallet; also body ‘wood’ frame metaphor for spine

椎.origin: 椎 pairs wood with bird-phonetic 隹. One line of meaning is a wooden hammer (chuí); another is the vertebrae of the spine (zhuī), seen as segmented ‘blocks.’ Context chooses the reading.
椎.history: Classical texts show both mallet and anatomical uses. Modern medicine favors 椎 for spine compounds (脊椎); 锤 often takes the hammer sense instead.

楚.meaning: clear; neat; name of the Chu state; to punish with rods

parts.note: shū/pǐ; phonetic component (often seen as 疋)

楚.origin: 楚 originally related to thorny bushes or wooden rods (林 above a phonetic). Rods used for beating gave punitive senses; clarity/neatness (清楚) and the ancient southern state name Chu are major later readings.
楚.history: Prominent as the Zhou-era state name. Modern everyday sense ‘clear’ is mostly bound in compounds like 清楚.

parts.note: lóu; simplified phonetic (from 婁 in 樓)

楼.origin: 楼 (traditional 樓) is a wooden multi-story building: wood meaning plus 娄/婁 sound. It names storied houses and, by extension, floors of a building.
楼.history: Traditional 樓 uses 婁; simplified 楼 uses 娄. Remains the default character for buildings with stories.

概.meaning: general; approximate; bearing; to level grain
概.origin: 概 was a wooden tool for scraping grain level in a measure—hence ‘generally; roughly’ as a leveled average. From that leveling came abstract overview (概念, 大概) and composed bearing (气概).
概.history: Agricultural implement sense is oldest; philosophical and colloquial ‘approximate/general’ senses dominate today.

榜.meaning: posted list; notice board; to flog (rare)
榜.origin: 榜 is a wooden board where names or notices are posted—exam results, rankings, announcements. From the public list it means a roll of honor or ranking chart (榜样 is related but uses a different etymological path for ‘model’).
榜.history: Imperial exam culture made 榜 iconic for published results. Modern media still ‘posts lists’ with this character.

榭.meaning: pavilion; open hall on a terrace
榭.origin: 榭 names a wooden open-sided pavilion or platform building, common in gardens and terraces. Wood marks the material; 射 supplies the sound. It is mainly a literary/architectural term.
榭.history: Classical poetry and garden design texts preserve 榭 for elegant open halls. Rare in everyday speech outside place names and literature.

parts.note: jiān; simplified phonetic (from 監 in 檻)

槛.origin: 槛 (traditional 檻) is wooden railing, animal cage, or door threshold—wood structures that enclose or mark a boundary. Reading jiàn favors rails/cages; kǎn favors the doorsill (门槛).
槛.history: Traditional 檻 uses 監; simplified 槛 uses 监. Threshold sense is very common in modern Mandarin via 门槛.

槽.origin: The character "槽" combines the radical "木" (mù, meaning "wood") on the left, indicating its association with wooden structures or containers, and "曹" (cáo, a character used for its phonetic value) on the right. The character "曹" itself includes elements suggesting organization or arrangement, which complements the idea of a structured container or trough. From wooden feeding troughs and channels it generalized to any groove or slot.
槽.history: Early uses center on troughs for animals or water. Mechanical and everyday ‘groove/slot’ senses (槽) are continuous with that channel shape.

標.meaning: mark; standard; label (traditional form of 标)
標.origin: 標 is the traditional form of 标. Wood plus 票 originally suggested a marked treetop or wooden sign. It generalized to marks, labels, and standards—the same semantic range as simplified 标.
標.history: Used in traditional-script regions and classical texts. Mainland simplified writing replaces it with 标 in most contexts.

模.origin: 模 is a wooden mold or pattern for copying shapes. From physical molds came models to imitate (模仿) and abstract modules/patterns. Reading mú is common in 模样/模具 in some dialects/standards.
模.history: Craft and casting vocabulary fixed the mold sense; education and art fixed imitation/model senses. Form identical in simplified script.

parts.note: yawn/deficient; suggests ranking after the first

次.origin: 次 is often analyzed as related to arranging in sequence—what comes after the primary. It means order, the next item, a secondary grade, and a count of occurrences (‘times’). The ‘deficient/second’ nuance of 欠 supports the ‘not first’ idea.
次.history: Oracle/bronze usage already includes sequence and encampment order. Modern Chinese uses it constantly as a verbal measure (一次).

parts.note: simplified left side replacing 雚 in 歡
breath/yawn component; related to emotional utterance

欢.origin: 欢 is simplified from 歡, which used a complex phonetic 雚 with 欠 (breath/emotion). It expresses joy, cheerfulness, and liking (喜欢). The simplified form keeps 欠 and shortens the left to 又.
欢.history: Traditional 歡 remains in Taiwan/Hong Kong print; mainland 欢 is standard. Emotional vocabulary (欢乐, 欢迎) is highly frequent.

欧.meaning: Europe; surname Ou; to vomit (classical, rare)

parts.note: qū; simplified phonetic (from 區 in 歐)

欧.origin: 欧 (traditional 歐) combines a phonetic with 欠. Classical senses include vomiting; the modern dominant use is the phonetic loan/transcription for Europe (欧洲) and the surname.
欧.history: As a geographic shorthand 欧 is modern; traditional 歐 is used in traditional-script regions. Everyday mainland form is 欧.

欲.origin: 欲 pairs 谷 (sound) with 欠 (breath/deficiency) to mean desire—feeling a lack and wanting to fill it. It covers appetite, wish, and the grammatical ‘about to’ (欲出).
欲.history: Philosophical texts (欲望) made it central to ethics. Simplified script keeps the traditional form 欲 unchanged.

欺.origin: 欺 uses 其 for sound and 欠 for an oral/breath element, pointing to dishonest speech or taking advantage. Core meanings are to cheat and to bully or insult (欺负).
欺.history: Moral vocabulary from classical times (欺瞒). Modern compounds remain strongly negative.

parts.note: upper element in complex left side
breath; related to sincere utterance / itemization

款.origin: 款 historically relates to sincere words or enumerated items (with 欠). It came to mean articles/clauses, funds of money, and styles or models of products (款式). Hospitality ‘treat’ (款待) preserves a courteous sense.
款.history: Bronze/seal analyses vary on the left component. Modern finance and fashion uses (贷款, 新款) are especially common.

歉.origin: 歉 combines 兼 (sound) with 欠 (lack): a shortfall. Agricultural shortfall meant poor harvest; emotional shortfall became regret and apology (道歉, 抱歉).
歉.history: Crop-failure sense is classical; apology sense dominates contemporary speech. Form is the same in simplified Chinese.

歌.origin: 歌 pairs the phonetic 哥 with 欠 (breath/voice) to mean singing or a song. The breath component marks vocal performance; the sound match is transparent in Mandarin.
歌.history: A standard phono-semantic in seal and later scripts. Remains the basic character for songs and singing.

止.origin: 止 began as a drawing of a foot or footprint. From the foot at rest it meant to stop or cease. It is also a productive radical in characters about walking and stance.
止.history: Oracle-bone foot shape simplified into the modern three-stroke form. Verbal ‘stop’ is the main modern reading; ‘foot’ survives mainly as a component explanation.

parts.note: top line; target or straightness marker
foot; stepping straight toward a point

正.origin: The character "正" originally depicted a foot stepping forward, symbolizing moving straight ahead or being upright. It consists of the radical "止" (zhǐ, meaning "stop") on the bottom, representing a foot, and a horizontal stroke on top, indicating correctness or straightness. From marching straight at a target it meant upright, correct, principal, and ‘just/exactly’ in time.
正.history: Oracle forms show a foot under a target-like mark. Political and moral ‘correct/orthodox’ senses are ancient; adverbial ‘currently/just’ is everyday Mandarin.

parts.note: person/ladle-like mark; deixis at a spot

此.origin: 此 is analyzed as a foot stopping at a pointed place—‘this (here).’ It is a basic proximal demonstrative contrasting with 彼 ‘that.’ Literary Chinese uses it constantly; modern speech often prefers 这 but keeps 此 in formal phrases.
此.history: Stable since early script as a demonstrative. Compounds like 因此 and 彼此 remain high-frequency.

parts.note: inverted/opposite foot form in origin (止 reversed)

步.origin: 步 originally showed one foot following the other—left and right footprints—hence a step or pace. From footsteps it means walking, stages of a process, and units of progress.
步.history: Oracle graphs depict successive feet; later forms fossilize the lower component into a 少-like shape. Measure and verb uses both thrive in modern Chinese.

武.origin: Traditional analysis (and early forms) links 武 to stopping weapons (止 + 戈): martial power that can end conflict, or marching with arms. It denotes military force, martial arts, and valor.
武.history: Bronze forms show weapon and foot elements more clearly than the modern fused shape. Cultural compounds (武术, 武装) keep the martial core.

歪.origin: The character "歪" contains the radical "不" (bù, meaning "not") and "正" (zhèng, meaning "correct" or "straight"), symbolizing something that is not correct or straight, hence crooked or askew. The composition is a transparent opposite of 正.
歪.history: A later popular associative form that spread widely in vernacular writing. Modern Mandarin uses it for tilted objects and figuratively improper ways.

死.origin: 死 joins the ‘bone/death’ component 歹 with a person-like mark, depicting a dead person or remains. It is the basic verb and noun for dying and death; colloquial intensifier uses (‘to death’) are later.
死.history: Early graphs are grimly pictorial. The character has been central and taboo-sensitive across Chinese history; form is stable in simplified script.

殷.meaning: abundant; earnest; Yin/Shang dynasty name (yīn); dark red (yān)

parts.note: body/weapon elements in early forms; analyses vary

殷.origin: 殷 has complex early forms linked to flourishing, grand music, or wounding; scholars debate exact pictorial details. It became the name of the late Shang capital/dynasty (Yin) and means abundant or solicitous (殷勤). Reading yān is ‘dark red.’
殷.history: As a dynasty name it is foundational in Chinese historiography. Modern learners mainly meet 殷勤 and historical 殷商.

殿.meaning: palace hall; rear of an army

parts.note: left complex often linked to hall/rear elements

殿.origin: 殿 denotes a grand hall at the rear of a palace complex, and by extension the rear guard of an army. The 殳 component marks forceful construction or arrangement; the left supplies sound/meaning related to a spacious hall.
殿.history: Imperial architecture made 殿 the word for throne halls (太和殿). Military ‘rear’ sense is classical.

每.origin: 每 is built on 母 as phonetic (and historically related graph), with an added top mark. It generalized to ‘each/every’—recurring instances—rather than kinship. The mother graph’s fertility/recurrence associations are sometimes cited pedagogically.
每.history: Demonstrative/quantifier use is ancient. Modern Chinese uses 每 constantly before measure phrases (每天).

parts.note: lower component; analyses vary (often linked to 毋/母)

毒.origin: 毒 is traditionally tied to toxic plants—harmful vegetation affecting life. It means poison, venom, and by extension cruel or addictive harm (病毒, 恶毒).
毒.history: Seal forms emphasize plant toxicity. Medical and moral metaphors kept the character highly productive.

parts.note: person-like mark (right); two people side by side

比.origin: 比 shows two people (or spoon-like marks) side by side, suggesting lining up for comparison. From placing things together it means to compare, to compete, and mathematical ratio.
比.history: Oracle forms already depict paired figures. Grammaticalized uses (比…更) are central in modern Mandarin.

毕.origin: 毕 is simplified from 畢, originally a hunting net (field + a netting element)—capturing game ‘completely.’ From finishing a hunt it meant to conclude and ‘all.’ Simplified 毕 reshapes the net into 比 + 十.
毕.history: Traditional 畢 preserves the net structure; simplified 毕 is standard on the mainland. Graduation compound 毕业 is ubiquitous.

毫.meaning: fine hair; tiny bit; milli-; writing brush tip

parts.note: upper phonetic remnant related to 高-like sound

毫.origin: 毫 centers on 毛 ‘hair,’ denoting fine animal hair or brush tip. Extreme fineness yields ‘a tiny bit’ (毫不) and the metric prefix milli- in scientific Chinese.
毫.history: Brush culture linked 毫 to calligraphy tips. Modern intensifiers and unit prefixes keep it common.

民.origin: 民 originally referred to common people as opposed to rulers. Early graphic explanations often involve an eye or a bound/commoner symbol; exact pictography is debated. The social sense ‘the people’ is stable.
民.history: Central in political vocabulary from antiquity (民本) through modern 民主, 人民. Form is unchanged in simplified script.

氓.origin: 氓 combines people (民) with 亡 (lost/fleeing) as phonetic and hint: displaced folk. Classical méng means commoners; modern máng in 流氓 means hooligan or vagrant.
氓.history: Classical demographic sense faded; vernacular 流氓 fixed the pejorative reading for most learners.

气.meaning: air; gas; breath; vital energy; anger
气.origin: 气 pictures swirling vapor or breath. It names air and gases, physiological breath, cosmic/vital energy in Chinese thought, and by extension mood or anger. Simplified 气 is the streamlined vapor graph without the rice of 氣.
气.history: Oracle vapors evolve into seal and modern forms. Mainland simplified dropped the 米 of traditional 氣; philosophical and everyday senses both remain huge.

氣.meaning: air; gas; breath; vital energy (traditional form of 气)

parts.note: mǐ; phonetic; also linked to grain offerings / steam from cooking rice

氣.origin: 氣 adds 米 under/within the vapor graph 气. Traditional explanations connect steam from cooking grain or use 米 as phonetic. Meaning matches simplified 气: air, breath, energy, temper.
氣.history: Standard traditional form in Taiwan/Hong Kong and classical texts. Mainland orthography uses 气 for most modern writing.

parts.note: flowing water with central current and ripples

水.origin: 水 is a pictograph of flowing water: a central stream with side ripples. It is the basic word for water and a major radical (氵) in liquid-related characters.
水.history: Oracle-bone water graphs are already recognizable. One of the most frequent characters and radicals in the writing system.

永.origin: 永 depicts a long, branching stream—water that continues far. From endless flow it means everlasting or permanent. Calligraphy tradition treats 永’s strokes as a model set (永字八法).
永.history: Early forms emphasize prolonged water. Abstract ‘forever’ became the dominant lexical sense early on.

汁.origin: 汁 is liquid extracted from something: water radical plus a simple phonetic. It covers fruit juice, plant sap, and savory broths.
汁.history: Culinary and botanical uses are longstanding. Form is identical in simplified and traditional scripts.

求.meaning: to seek; to request; to demand

parts.note: often linked to a fur coat / reaching hand; later phonetic in 裘

求.origin: 求 is commonly connected to seeking a fur garment (related to 裘) or reaching out to obtain something. The abstract verb ‘to seek/ask for’ became primary. It covers requests, pursuits, and demands.
求.history: Bronze/seal forms vary; later standardizes to the modern shape. Extremely frequent in verbs like 要求, 求学.

汉.meaning: Han ethnicity; Chinese; man; Han dynasty

parts.note: simplified remnant of 堇/𦰩 phonetic in 漢

汉.origin: 汉 is simplified from 漢, originally the Han River (water + complex phonetic). The river name labeled the Han dynasty and then the majority ethnicity and the Chinese language/script (汉语, 汉字).
汉.history: Traditional 漢 keeps a complex right side; simplified 汉 uses 又. Cultural identity senses overshadow the river sense for most speakers.

汗.origin: 汗 is water/liquid plus 干 sound, naming sweat. The meaning stays close to perspiration, with figurative ‘ashamed sweat’ in some idioms.
汗.history: Straightforward phono-semantic retained from seal script into modern writing.

江.origin: 江 is a major river: water radical with 工 as phonetic. In northern usage 河 is common for rivers; 江 often points to southern rivers and especially the Yangtze (长江).
江.history: Ancient southern river term that became the proper name core of 长江. Remains a basic geography character.

沉.origin: 沉 shows something going down in water (氵) with a phonetic related to sinking/leaning. From physical submersion it means deep, heavy, and emotionally subdued.
沉.history: Also written 沈 in some traditional contexts for the surname/place; verb ‘sink’ standardizes as 沉 in simplified Chinese.

沒.meaning: not have (méi); to sink/die (mò); traditional form of 没
沒.origin: 沒 depicts action in water—sinking or disappearing beneath the surface (mò). The extremely common méi reading ‘not have / not’ grammaticalized from ‘gone; disappeared.’ Traditional form of simplified 没.
沒.history: Classical mò ‘sink/end’ coexists with vernacular méi negation. Traditional-script regions keep 沒 as the standard glyph.

parts.note: gōu; phonetic (simplified from 溝’s 冓)

沟.origin: 沟 is simplified from 溝: a water channel or ditch. Water marks the meaning; the phonetic matches gōu. It covers irrigation ditches, gutters, and metaphorical gaps (鸿沟).
沟.history: Traditional 溝 uses 冓; simplified 沟 uses 勾. Everyday drainage and figurative ‘divide’ senses both continue.

没.origin: 没 is the simplified form of 沒. Originally ‘to sink under water’ (mò); the méi reading ‘have not / did not’ is the dominant modern grammatical use, from the idea that something is gone.
没.history: One of the highest-frequency function words in Mandarin (没有). Literary mò senses survive in compounds like 没收, 沉没.

沦.meaning: to sink; to fall into (decline); ripple

parts.note: lún; simplified phonetic (from 侖 in 淪)

沦.origin: 沦 (traditional 淪) is water plus a phonetic lún: ripples or sinking into water. Figuratively it means falling into a bad state (沦为, 沦陷).
沦.history: Poetic ripple senses and political ‘fall/subjugation’ senses are both classical. Simplified 沦 replaces 侖 with 仑.

油.origin: 油 is a liquid (氵) with 由 as sound: oil or grease. From cooking and lamp oils it extended to oily texture and slick, crafty manner (油滑).
油.history: Clear phono-semantic retained into modern script. Industrial and culinary compounds are numerous.

治.meaning: to govern; to cure; to manage
治.origin: 治 pairs water with 台 sound. Early governance imagery includes regulating floods and waterways; bringing chaos to order became general rule, management, and medical treatment (治病).
治.history: Political philosophy (治国) and medicine share this character from classical times. Form is the same in simplified Chinese.

沿.origin: 沿 combines the water radical with a phonetic once linked to a riverbank or mouth-like opening. The core image is moving along a watercourse or following its edge. From that riverside sense it extended to “along,” “follow,” and figurative borders.
沿.history: Classical usage already covers following a stream and bordering something. Modern Chinese keeps both literal (沿岸) and abstract (沿用) senses.

parts.note: water; even standard like still water
in older 灋, removing bias; retained in modern form

法.origin: 法 is historically linked to 灋, which combined water, a fairness-judging creature, and 去 “to remove.” The idea was a standard that washes away injustice—hence law and proper method. Modern 法 keeps water plus 去 as a simplified continuation of that legal graph.
法.history: Bronze and seal forms preserve the older legal composition. Simplified 法 became the everyday graph for law, Dharma, and technique.

注.meaning: to pour; to focus; note; annotate

parts.note: zhǔ; also suggests a focal point

注.origin: 注 pairs water with 主. Pouring liquid toward a target became “to pour into,” then “to concentrate attention” and “to annotate.” The phonetic 主 also hints at focusing on a main point.
注.history: Early senses center on pouring and channeling. Commentary and attention senses (注意、注解) are well established in classical and modern Chinese.

泪.origin: The character 泪 includes the radical 氵 (water) on the left, signifying liquid, and 目 (eye) on the right, indicating that tears come from the eyes. Together they depict the liquid that flows from the eyes. It is also the common simplified counterpart related to traditional 淚.
泪.history: 泪 is standard in simplified Chinese; traditional writing often uses 淚 with a different phonetic. The eye-plus-water reading remains the most intuitive modern analysis.

泼.origin: 泼 is the simplified form of 潑: water plus a phonetic related to 發. The image is water thrown outward—splashing. Figurative uses for bold or rude behavior grew from that forceful scatter of liquid.
泼.history: Traditional 潑 uses a fuller phonetic. Simplified 泼 keeps the water radical and a shorter right side; splash and personality senses both remain common.

parts.note: xī; phonetic in the simplified form of 灑

洒.origin: 洒 is the simplified form of 灑. Water plus a phonetic yields “to sprinkle or scatter liquid.” Lightly distributing water over a surface underlies both literal sprinkling and some literary free-and-easy senses.
洒.history: Traditional 灑 has a more complex right side. Modern 洒 is standard in simplified texts for sprinkle, spray, and related idioms.

parts.note: zhōu; also tied to land amid streams

洲.origin: 洲 adds water to 州 to mark land in or among waters—sandbanks and islets—later continents. 州 itself once depicted land amid streams; the water radical makes the geographic sense explicit.
洲.history: Classical texts use 洲 for river islets and literary place-names. Modern Chinese also uses it for continents (亚洲、非洲).

活.origin: 活 combines water with phonetic 舌. Flowing water suggested vitality and movement, so the character came to mean living, lively, and workable or flexible. Running water as a metaphor for life is long-standing.
活.history: Attested early with senses of life and activity. Compounds like 生活 and 活泼 keep the living/active core into modern usage.

派.meaning: to send; to dispatch; faction; style
派.origin: 派 combines the water radical with a right-side component associated with branching or allocating. Like streams splitting from a river, it came to mean sending people out, factions, and styles or schools. Water imagery supports distribution and divergence.
派.history: Early senses include tributaries and dispatching. Modern 派 covers factions, schools, assignment (派遣), and stylish manner.

流.meaning: to flow; stream; class; to spread
流.origin: 流 is water plus a phonetic historically tied to flowing shapes. It names the movement of liquids and then any continuous spread—people, rumors, currents of fashion. Class or category (一流) is a social extension of “stream.”
流.history: Oracle-to-seal development already emphasizes flowing. Extremely frequent in modern Chinese for flow, circulate, and grade.

浊.origin: 浊 is the simplified form of 濁. Water plus a phonetic yields cloudy, unsettled liquid. From muddy water it extended to impure sound, murky morals, and heavy atmosphere.
浊.history: Traditional 濁 uses 蜀 as phonetic. Simplified 浊 shortens the right side; the turbid/impure meaning is unchanged.

测.meaning: to measure; to survey; to conjecture
测.origin: 测 pairs water with 则/則. Measuring water depth or level became general measurement, then estimate and conjecture. The water radical recalls practical gauging of rivers and wells.
测.history: Classical usage includes surveying and assessing. Modern 测 covers science, testing, and guessing (推测).

济.meaning: to cross a river; to aid; to help
济.origin: 济 (trad. 濟) is water plus phonetic 齐/齊. Crossing a river successfully led to “help someone through” and related aid senses. The river-crossing origin remains visible in the water radical.
济.history: Famous river name 济水 and verbs of aid/crossing are classical. Simplified 济 is standard today.

浓.origin: 浓 is simplified from 濃: liquid plus phonetic 农/農. Thick, rich liquid—dew, ink, tea—extended to intense feelings and deep colors. Density of fluid is the semantic core.
浓.history: Traditional 濃 is common in older texts. Modern 浓 covers concentrated solutions and figurative intensity.

浩.origin: 浩 combines water with phonetic 告 to describe great, rolling waters. Vastness of flood or sea became a literary adjective for grandeur and abundance (浩瀚).
浩.history: Frequent in classical poetry for mighty waters. Remains a literary and name character in modern Chinese.

浪.origin: 浪 is water plus 良. It names billows and then free, wandering, or prodigal behavior—movement without restraint, like waves. Natural and human “wave-like” motion share the graph.
浪.history: Bronze/seal forms already show the water-plus-phonetic structure. 浪人、浪费 and ocean waves keep the range of senses alive.

海.origin: 海 pairs water with phonetic 每 for the great body of salt water. From the sea it extended to vast quantities and crowds (人海). It is one of the basic geographic water characters.
海.history: Attested early and culturally central. Compounds for overseas, seafood, and metaphoric seas are ubiquitous.

消.meaning: to disappear; to eliminate; to digest
消.origin: 消 is water plus 肖. Melting ice or washing something away suggested vanishing and canceling. Digestion and spending (消费) are further extensions of using up or dissolving.
消.history: Classical senses include melt, vanish, and dispel. Modern usage is extremely broad: cancel, digest, consume, news (消息).

涉.meaning: to wade; to involve; to experience
涉.origin: 涉 joins water and steps: walking through a stream. From wading it came to mean going through an experience and being involved in a matter. Crossing water is the concrete root of involvement.
涉.history: Oracle and bronze forms support the wading analysis. Legal and narrative 涉及 keep the “enter into” sense today.

涕.origin: 涕 combines water with phonetic 弟 for fluids from the eyes or nose. Classical Chinese often uses 涕 for tears of grief; later it also covers mucus. Weeping and lament are closely tied senses.
涕.history: Common in classical emotion vocabulary. Modern speech still uses 鼻涕; literary 涕泪 pairs tears and weeping.

涨.origin: 涨 (trad. 漲) is water plus 张/張. Rising water level became rising prices and swelling. The water radical keeps the hydrological origin visible beside economic metaphors.
涨.history: Traditional 漲 appears in older texts. Simplified 涨 is standard for tides, inflation, and swelling.

parts.note: simplified from 澀 with complex traditional right side

涩.origin: 涩 is the simplified form of 澀. It describes an astringent, puckering taste and by extension rough texture or halting, awkward speech and writing. The water radical situates it among taste and moisture words.
涩.history: Traditional 澀 is more complex graphically. Modern 涩 covers unripe fruit taste, rough surfaces, and awkward prose.

涮.meaning: to rinse; to scald briefly in hot pot
涮.origin: 涮 combines water with 刷. Rinsing by swishing in water led to the hot-pot sense: briefly swishing food in boiling broth. The phonetic reinforces a quick brushing motion.
涮.history: Culinary 涮锅 is especially prominent in northern Chinese food culture. The rinse/swish sense remains transparent.

涵.origin: 涵 is water plus 函. Water held within banks or a culvert suggested containing and embracing. Cultivation of mind (涵养) extends the idea of holding depth within.
涵.history: Used for culverts, inclusion, and personal cultivation. Remains moderately literary but familiar in compounds.

淘.meaning: to wash (rice/ore); to eliminate; to browse bargains
淘.origin: 淘 pairs water with phonetic 匋. Washing grain or ore to remove dross became selecting and discarding, then informal shopping around. Water-driven separation is the root image.
淘.history: Classical washing/ore senses precede modern 淘汰 and shopping slang. The water radical stays constant.

淡.origin: 淡 is water plus 炎. Watered-down flavor or color—weak tea, pale ink—extended to calm, indifferent attitudes. Dilution by water is the semantic key.
淡.history: Stable since seal script in food, color, and personality senses. Extremely common in modern Chinese.

淨.origin: 淨 (also written 净) combines water with a phonetic related to 爭/争. Washing with water yields cleanliness and purity, including ritual and moral purity. The water radical marks purification.
淨.history: 淨 is a traditional/variant form alongside simplified 净. Buddhist and everyday cleaning vocabulary both use this family of graphs.

深.origin: 深 is water plus a phonetic associated with depth. Measuring how far down water goes became general depth—space, thought, feeling, and color. The aquatic origin remains in the radical.
深.history: Ancient texts already use deep water and profound meaning. One of the highest-frequency abstract adjectives today.

混.meaning: to mix; muddy; reckless; to muddle along
混.origin: 混 pairs water with 昆. Stirred, muddy water suggested mixing unlike things and living in a confused or careless way. Chaos and blending share the same root image.
混.history: Classical muddy/mix senses lead to modern 混乱、混合、混日子. Very productive in colloquial speech.

清.meaning: clear; pure; to clear up; Qing
清.origin: 清 is water plus 青. Clear, unmuddied water became purity, clarity of mind, and settling accounts. The phonetic 青 also evokes fresh blue-green clarity.
清.history: Central virtue and nature word for millennia; also the dynastic name 清. Remains extremely frequent.

渐.origin: 渐 (trad. 漸) is water plus 斩/斬. Water soaking in little by little yielded the adverb “gradually.” Progressive change like rising damp is the concrete model.
渐.history: Classical gradual and soak senses both exist. Modern 渐渐 is a staple adverb of slow change.

渔.origin: 渔 (trad. 漁) places water with 鱼. Catching fish in water is the transparent core; figurative angling for profit follows. Fish supports both sound and sense.
渔.history: Simplified 渔 shortens traditional 漁. Occupations, hobbies, and idioms about opportunistic gain all use it.

parts.note: wood/structure of a conduit in some analyses

渠.origin: 渠 names an artificial water channel. Analyses often see a large (巨) wooden or engineered conduit associated with water. From canals it also became a pronoun in some dialects.
渠.history: Important in irrigation vocabulary and place names. Still taught as “canal/ditch” in standard Mandarin.

渡.meaning: to cross (water); ferry; to tide over
渡.origin: 渡 is water plus 度. Crossing to the other shore—by boat or ford—extended to getting through hardship and ferry points. The phonetic doubles as a crossing hint.
渡.history: Ferry crossings and Buddhist deliverance language both use 渡. Modern 过渡 keeps the across sense.

渣.origin: 渣 combines water with phonetic 查 for leftover bits after liquids are processed—lees, dregs, sludge. From physical residue it became trashy people or content (渣男、渣滓).
渣.history: Common in food, industry, and slang. The dregs image drives both literal and insulting uses.

温.origin: 温 (trad. 溫) is water plus phonetic 昷. Warm water suggested mild heat and a gentle temperament. Reviewing lessons (温习) is a metaphorical rewarming of knowledge.
温.history: Temperature, personality, and place-name uses are all old. Simplified 温 is the modern standard.

港.origin: 港 pairs water with 巷 for a navigable inlet or harbor where ships shelter. From ports it became the short name for Hong Kong and other harbor cities.
港.history: Geographic and trade vocabulary from imperial times onward. 港口 and 香港 keep it highly visible today.

游.meaning: to swim; to travel; to roam
游.origin: 游 combines water with a phonetic linked to roaming. Moving freely in water became swimming; moving freely on land became travel and leisure. Related 遊 overlaps in travel senses.
游.history: Classical poetry loves roaming 游. Modern 游泳、旅游 are core vocabulary; orthographic 游/遊 variation exists.

源.origin: 源 is water plus 原. The headwaters of a river became any origin or resource. 原 already suggests origin; water makes the fountainhead concrete.
源.history: Used for rivers, funding sources, and abstract origins. Extremely productive in modern compounds (来源、资源).

溜.meaning: to slide; to slip; to sneak off
溜.origin: 溜 includes water on the left, symbolizing smooth or fluid movement, and 留 on the right as phonetic. Sliding as if on a wet surface led to slipping away quietly. Effortless flow is the shared idea.
溜.history: Colloquial senses (溜达、溜走) are very common. The water radical keeps the slick-motion image.

滑.origin: 滑 is water plus 骨. Wet surfaces that make one slip became smoothness of texture and sly, oily character. Water explains the physical slipperiness.
滑.history: From seal script into modern slang (滑头). Technical senses include glide and lubricate.

parts.note: phonetic retained from 滿 in simplified shape

满.origin: 满 is simplified from 滿: water rising to the brim. Fullness of a container became satisfaction and completeness. Ethnic name 满 also uses this graph.
满.history: Traditional 滿 is still seen; simplified 满 is standard. Core full/satisfied senses are ancient and current.

滴.origin: 滴 pairs water with phonetic 啇 for a single bead of liquid and the act of dripping. Counting drops and describing trickle sounds both grow from this image.
滴.history: Onomatopoetic and measure-word uses (一滴) are everyday. Stable phono-semantic structure.

漂.meaning: to float; to drift; to bleach
漂.origin: 漂 is water plus 票. Floating with the current became drift and unstable living; a related reading covers bleaching with water. Motion on a water surface is primary.
漂.history: Multiple readings (piāo/piǎo/piào) distinguish float, bleach, and handsome. All keep the water radical.

漆.origin: 漆 combines liquid with a component tied to the lacquer tree. The sap used as varnish named both the material and the act of coating. Fluid coating is the semantic link.
漆.history: Important craft and art material character. Modern 油漆 extends it to general paint.

演.meaning: to act; to perform; to develop; to drill
演.origin: 演 combines water with phonetic 寅. It suggests performing or unfolding something in a fluid, continuous manner, similar to how water moves. From flowing forth it came to mean evolve, practice, and stage performance.
演.history: Military drill, mathematical derivation, and theater all use 演. Performance senses dominate daily speech.

漫.origin: 漫 is water plus 曼. Water spreading beyond banks became unbound, leisurely, and all-over. Modern 漫画 borrows the freehand/casual sense for comics.
漫.history: Literary flood and free-roaming senses are classical; comic/manga sense is modern but entrenched.

潇.origin: 潇 (trad. 瀟) is water plus 萧/蕭. It describes misty or driving rain and, by extension, a natural, unforced elegance (潇洒). Rain-water atmosphere is the starting point.
潇.history: Famous in river names and poetic weather scenes. 潇洒 remains a high-frequency compliment.

潜.origin: 潜 is simplified from 潛: water plus a phonetic. Going under water became hidden talent, stealth, and latent forces. Submergence is the root metaphor.
潜.history: Traditional 潛 has a different phonetic shape. Modern 潜 covers submarine, potential, and sneak.

潮.origin: 潮 pairs water with 朝. The periodic rise of sea water became dampness inland and, recently, trendiness (潮流). Tidal rhythm is the original sense.
潮.history: Classical tide and moist senses precede slang cool/trendy. Geographic compounds are standard.

激.origin: 激 is water plus 敫. Water dashing against obstacles suggested surge, impact, and stirring emotion. Intense feeling and radicalism extend the force of rushing water.
激.history: Classical surge and excite senses lead to modern 激动、激烈. Very common intensifier family.

火.origin: 火 originally pictured leaping flames—central tongues of fire with side sparks. From the element itself it extended to heat, urgency, and anger. It is also a major radical for burning-related characters.
火.history: Oracle-bone forms already show flame shapes. Remains a basic noun, radical, and metaphor throughout Chinese.

parts.note: cover/line over fire in simplified form from 滅

灭.origin: 灭 is the simplified form of 滅. Putting out fire—historically water plus other components in 滅—became total destruction. The simplified graph visually suggests covering or cutting off flame.
灭.history: Traditional 滅 includes water. Simplified 灭 is standard for extinguish, exterminate, and vanish.

灯.origin: 灯 is simplified from 燈: fire plus phonetic 丁 (traditionally 登). A flame for illumination named lamps and lights. Fire/light is the semantic core.
灯.history: Traditional 燈 uses 登 as phonetic. Modern 灯 covers lamps, traffic lights, and figurative highlights.

parts.note: upper component from simplified form of 靈

灵.origin: 灵 is the simplified form of 靈, an elaborate graph once tied to ritual jade and spiritual efficacy. The modern shape keeps a fire-related element and names spirit, cleverness, and effectiveness. Sacred responsiveness narrowed into everyday “smart/effective.”
灵.history: Traditional 靈 remains in names and religious contexts. Simplified 灵 is standard in mainland usage.

灾.origin: 灾 shows fire under a roof: a house fire as the archetype of calamity. From domestic blaze it generalized to floods, famines, and all disasters. Fire-in-dwelling is a classic associative image; variant 災 also exists.
灾.history: Both 灾 and 災 appear historically. Simplified 灾 is common for natural and man-made disasters.

炀.origin: 炀 (trad. 煬) combines fire with phonetic 昜. Intense heating for melting or roasting is the core. Historical names and technical heating senses keep the fire radical central.
炀.history: Less common in daily speech but attested in classical and technical vocabulary for scorching heat and melting.

炸.origin: 炸 pairs fire with 乍. Sudden fire-force became explosion; intense hot oil cooking became deep-frying. Abrupt heat links both readings.
炸.history: Modern Chinese sharply distinguishes culinary and explosive senses by context. Very high frequency.

点.origin: 点 is simplified from 點: a phonetic with fire marks. Lighting a lamp and marking a small spot share the graph. From dots it extended to points, snacks, and clock times.
点.history: Traditional 點 is still widely recognized. Simplified 点 is among the most frequent modern characters.

烈.origin: 烈 places fire marks under 列. Fierce burning became intense emotion, harsh strength, and martyr-like ardor. Fire supplies the intensity.
烈.history: Classical fierce/bright senses feed modern 强烈、烈士. Stable phono-semantic layout.

烟.origin: 烟 (also 煙) is fire plus phonetic 因/垔. Smoke rising from fire named tobacco smoke and haze. Fire is the clear semantic half.
烟.history: Variant forms 烟/煙 coexist historically. Modern 烟 covers smoke, cigarettes, and misty scenery.

烤.meaning: to roast; to bake; to warm by fire
烤.origin: 烤 combines fire with 考. Heating food or the body by fire is the straightforward sense. The phonetic matches the modern reading closely.
烤.history: Everyday cooking vocabulary (烤鸭、烧烤). Transparent fire-plus-phonetic structure.

烧.origin: 烧 is simplified from 燒: fire plus a phonetic. Burning wood or cooking over flame extended to fever heat. Fire remains the semantic anchor.
烧.history: Traditional 燒 is common in older texts. Simplified 烧 is standard for burn, roast, and run a temperature.

parts.note: zhí; simplified phonetic path from 熱

热.origin: 热 is simplified from 熱. Fire marks under a phonetic yield physical heat and warm enthusiasm. The fire element marks temperature and fervor.
热.history: Traditional 熱 remains familiar. Simplified 热 is core vocabulary for weather, food, and passion.

parts.note: traditional form; danced with streamers, loaned for negation

無.origin: 無 originally related to a dancing figure with flowing sleeves or streamers and was borrowed for the negative “not have.” It is the traditional form corresponding to simplified 无. Negation and absence are the dominant meanings.
無.history: Bronze forms support the dance origin before phonetic loan for negation. Still used in traditional orthography and set phrases.

然.origin: 然 originally meant to burn (fire under a phonetic/semantic complex) and was borrowed for “so; thus.” From affirmation it became a common adverbial and adjectival suffix (-like). The fire element preserves the older burn sense in etymology.
然.history: Classical particles 然、然后 are foundational. Modern compounds (自然、突然) are extremely frequent.

照.meaning: to shine; to illuminate; according to
照.origin: 照 places fire/light under 昭. Illumination became photographs, comparison, and “according to” a model. Light that makes things clear is the semantic bridge.
照.history: Used for sunshine, documents (护照), and compliance (按照). Stable since classical Chinese.

熄.origin: 熄 combines fire with 息. A flame that rests or ceases is extinguished. The phonetic’s “stop/breathe” sense reinforces putting fire out.
熄.history: Common with lamps, engines, and figurative dying-down. Transparent modern composition.

熊.origin: 熊 places fire marks under 能. It names the bear and, in some dialects, fierce scolding or blazing force. The fire element may mark fierce energy rather than literal burning.
熊.history: Animal name is primary in standard Mandarin. Dialectal and surname uses are also common.

熟.origin: 熟 puts fire under 孰. Heat that finishes cooking became ripeness and thorough familiarity—knowledge “cooked through.” Fire explains the culinary origin.
熟.history: Readings shú/shóu both occur. 熟悉、成熟 keep the done/familiar senses central.

爆.meaning: to explode; to burst; to quick-fry
爆.origin: 爆 includes fire and 暴 (violent), symbolizing a sudden, intense release of energy like an explosion. Bursting kernels, sudden fame, and quick high-heat cooking all extend that violent fire image.
爆.history: Modern media loves 爆 for viral bursts. Culinary and explosive senses coexist.

爱.origin: 爱 is simplified from 愛, which traditionally included a heart (心) between upper and lower parts. Affection and cherished care are the core meanings. Simplification removed the explicit heart while keeping the everyday love graph.
爱.history: Traditional 愛 remains standard in traditional regions. Simplified 爱 is among the most emotionally central characters.

爸.origin: 爸 combines the father radical with phonetic 巴. It is a colloquial kinship term for father, more intimate than 父 alone. Sound and kinship meaning align clearly.
爸.history: Modern spoken Chinese heavily prefers 爸爸. Transparent phono-semantic formation.

parts.note: pictograph of a split wood slice

片.origin: 片 originally depicted a split piece of wood—a flat slice. From timber flakes it extended to any thin piece, cards, and film frames. Half of a tree trunk cut vertically is the classic explanation.
片.history: Counterpart 爿 exists in older analyses. Measure-word and media senses (影片) are modern expansions.

版.origin: 版 pairs 片 with 反. A flat board used for printing became editions and versions. The slice/board radical marks the physical plate.
版.history: Publishing vocabulary (出版、版本) is dominant today. Administrative “plates” and media versions follow.

牌.origin: 牌 is a flat board plus phonetic 卑. Signboards and tablets became playing cards and brand names. The flat-piece radical fits notices and cards alike.
牌.history: Everyday objects (门牌、扑克牌) and marketing (品牌) keep it highly frequent.

牙.origin: 牙 pictured interlocking molars or tusks. From teeth it extended to ivory and tooth-like serrations. It is both a basic body-part word and a radical in some dental-related graphs.
牙.history: Oracle and bronze forms already suggest teeth. Remains basic vocabulary and a surname.

parts.note: pictograph of an ox head with horns

牛.origin: 牛 originally showed an ox’s head and horns from the front. Domestic cattle were central to farming, so the graph is both animal name and radical for bovine-related characters. Modern slang also uses it for “awesome.”
牛.history: Among the earliest oracle-bone pictographs. Stable shape and meaning for millennia.

牧.meaning: to herd; to tend livestock; pasture
牧.origin: 牧 shows a hand with a stick (攵) beside cattle (牛): driving or tending livestock. From herding it extended to governing people as a flock and to pastoral lands. The associative scene is vivid and stable.
牧.history: Classical titles and verbs of pasturing are old. Modern 牧民、牧场 keep the livestock sense.

parts.note: ox radical (牛), marking livestock and tangible creatures
