翻.meaning: to turn over; to translate; to flip
翻.origin: 翻 joins phonetic 番 with 羽. A bird turning in flight or flipping wings suggested turning something over. Translation (翻译) is turning meaning from one language into another; overturning and searching (翻找) are related extensions.
翻.history: From physical flipping to linguistic translation, 翻 became highly productive in modern Chinese.

翼.origin: 翼 places feathers above phonetic 異. It names wings and, by metaphor, flanking forces or helpers who “wing” a leader. Aircraft wings and political “wings” reuse the same graph.
翼.history: Classical military and avian uses coexisted. Modern science and politics keep 翼 for wings and factions.

parts.note: bent elder with walking stick elements in early forms

老.origin: 老 originally depicts an elderly person, often analyzed with a bent back and stick. “Old” extended to seniority, familiarity, and adverbial “always/often” in colloquial patterns (老是). The hint sense “always; often” is a common spoken extension of the core “old.”
老.history: As both a word and the 耂 radical, 老 structures many age-related characters. Colloquial adverbial uses are especially frequent in Mandarin.

考.meaning: to examine; to test; deceased father (classical)
考.origin: 考 shares the elder component with 老 and adds phonetic 丂. Examining or verifying—originally including ritual inquiry—became scholastic testing. Classical Chinese also used 考 for a deceased father.
考.history: Imperial examination culture cemented 考 as “test.” Modern schools and research (考古, 考虑-related family) keep it central.

耄.origin: 耄 places 毛 under/with 老 to name advanced old age, traditionally around eighty to ninety. It often pairs with 耋 in the literary phrase 耄耋 for the very elderly. The character is literary rather than colloquial.
耄.history: Age-grade vocabulary in classical Chinese used 耄 for deep old age; modern writing mainly keeps it in set phrases.

parts.note: originally a pictograph later used as a particle and component
shares graphic links with old/sugarcane explanations in various traditions

者.origin: 者 began as a content graph and was borrowed as a classical particle meaning “the one who…” or a topic marker. It nominalizes verbs and phrases (读者, 作者) and remains a prolific word-former. Etymological pictographic details are debated, but grammatical use is ancient and stable.
者.history: Classical prose depends on 者 for structure. Modern Chinese preserves it mainly in compound agent nouns and written style.

耋.origin: 耋 combines 老 with phonetic 至 for old age around seventy to eighty. Together with 耄 it forms 耄耋, a respectful literary label for the very old. Like 耄, it is rare outside formal or classical contexts.
耋.history: Traditional age terms stratified elderhood; 耋 survives chiefly in fixed literary collocations.

而.origin: The character "而" is an ancient pictograph depicting a beard or a tassel, symbolizing connection and continuity. It is used in modern Chinese to connect clauses or phrases, often indicating a contrast or transition. Grammaticalization turned the concrete image into a versatile conjunction and classical connective.
而.history: Bronze inscriptions already show the whisker-like form. As a function word, 而 is fundamental in both classical and modern written Chinese.

耍.meaning: to play; to amuse oneself; to play tricks

parts.note: whiskers; also sound-related in some analyses

耍.origin: 耍 combines 而 and 女. Traditional explanations link it to playful or trifling behavior; modern Mandarin uses it for playing, fooling around, and manipulating (耍手段). It is more colloquial than 玩 in some regions’ speech.
耍.history: Vernacular literature spread 耍 as “play/trick.” It remains informal and vivid in northern Mandarin especially.

耗.meaning: to consume; to expend; to waste; news of loss (classical)
耗.origin: 耗 joins the plow radical 耒 with phonetic 毛. Wear and expenditure in farm work suggested consuming resources. Modern senses cover using up time, energy, or materials (消耗) and mouse-related 耗子 in colloquial naming.
耗.history: From agrarian wear to industrial consumption metrics, 耗 tracks expenditure. Negative “waste” nuances are common.

耽.meaning: to delay; to indulge in; to be addicted to
耽.origin: 耽 places an ear with phonetic 冘. Lingering to listen suggested delay and, later, indulgent immersion (耽搁, 耽于). The ear radical keeps the sense of attention that stretches too long.
耽.history: Literary Chinese used 耽 for both delay and absorption in pleasure. Modern formal writing still prefers it over purely colloquial delay verbs in some set phrases.

聊.meaning: to chat; slightly; to depend on (classical)
聊.origin: 聊 combines ear with phonetic 卯. Listening and casual talk yielded “chat” (聊天). Classical senses include “somewhat” and “to rely on temporarily,” still seen in written idioms.
聊.history: Internet-era speech made 聊天 ubiquitous. Older literary meanings survive in chengyu and formal prose.

聋.origin: 聋 simplifies 聾: phonetic dragon 龍/龙 plus ear. Impaired hearing is the core meaning; figurative “deaf to advice” appears in rhetoric. The ear radical makes the semantic field unmistakable.
聋.history: Medical and everyday language use 聋 for deafness. Simplified orthography replaced 龍 with 龙.

parts.note: zhī; simplified from 戠 in 職

职.origin: 职 simplifies 職, ear plus a phonetic. Duties heard and held suggested office and job. Modern 职业, 职员, and 职责 are core workplace vocabulary.
职.history: Bureaucratic Chinese long centered on 職. Mainland 职 continues that institutional sense.

联.meaning: to unite; to ally; to contact; couplet

parts.note: from traditional 關-related/糸 elements in 聯

联.origin: 联 is simplified 聯, historically a silk-and-ear complex for joining. Connecting parties yields alliances, contacts (联系), and paired literary couplets (对联). Unity and linkage are the stable core.
联.history: Political and telecom vocabulary boosted modern 联. Traditional 聯 remains in overseas print.

聚.meaning: to gather; to assemble; to concentrate

parts.note: modern top resembles ear; historically linked to gathering forms

聚.origin: 聚 is analyzed as gathering people (乑) with a taking/assembling element related to 取. Crowds coming together yield “assemble,” “get-together,” and concentration of things or wealth. Social 聚会 and technical 聚合物 share the root idea.
聚.history: Classical military and social assembly terms used 聚. It remains the basic verb for gathering people or resources.

聩.origin: 聩 (traditional 聵) is ear plus phonetic 贵/貴. It means deaf, often in literary or harsh evaluative phrases (昏聩). Compared with 聋, 聩 is more formal or pejorative in compounds.
聩.history: Classical moral critiques used 聵 for willful deafness. Modern readers mainly meet 聩 inside fixed expressions.

parts.note: zǒng; simplified phonetic from 悤 in 聰

聪.origin: 聪 simplifies 聰: ear plus a phonetic once written 悤. Acute hearing metaphorically became mental acuity—cleverness and intelligence (聪明). The ear radical preserves the sensory origin of “bright.”
聪.history: Praising quick minds with 聰/聪 is ancient. Simplified 聪 is among the most common positive descriptors for children and ability.

parts.note: early forms suggest a person acting with awe or arranging

肃.origin: 肃 (traditional 肅) conveys solemn respect and stern order. Early graphic explanations vary, but the word field centers on seriousness, reverence, and clearing into order (严肃, 肃清). Emotional tone is formal and restrained.
肃.history: Ritual and court language favored 肅. Simplified 肃 appears in school and official style for solemnity.

肆.meaning: wanton; unrestrained; shop; four (banker’s anti-fraud numeral)
肆.origin: 肆 combines action-related components into a graph meaning unrestrained display—hence “wanton” or “do as one pleases.” A marketplace stall sense yields “shop” (酒肆). As a capitalized numeral it writes “four” on checks to prevent alteration.
肆.history: Moral critique and commercial naming both used 肆. The financial numeral usage is a specialized modern/administrative convention.

parts.note: meat radical form used on the left of characters

肉.origin: 肉 pictographically shows cuts of meat. It names animal flesh as food and body flesh in anatomy. As the meat radical (⺼), it builds scores of body-part characters, including several later in this batch.
肉.history: Oracle forms already resemble ribs or meat strips. Culinary and anatomical senses remain primary.

肌.origin: 肌 places the flesh radical beside phonetic 几. It denotes muscle and fleshy tissue; skincare and literary body descriptions also use it (皮肤-related compounds historically overlap). Modern fitness language relies on 肌肉.
肌.history: Medical and aesthetic vocabulary kept 肌 active. It is the default morpheme for muscle in contemporary Chinese.

股.origin: 股 is flesh plus phonetic 殳, originally the thigh. From a divided body part came “share” of a company, a strand of rope, and a section of a whole. Financial 股票 made the share sense dominant in modern news.
股.history: Anatomy, finance, and classifiers for smells or forces all use 股. The thigh sense remains in medical and classical contexts.

parts.note: hù; door-like shape suggesting the shoulder joint area

肩.origin: 肩 combines a door-like component with flesh to name the shoulder. Bearing burdens on the shoulder yields figurative responsibility (肩负). It is a basic body-part term.
肩.history: Everyday and literary Chinese keep 肩 concrete. Metaphorical load-bearing senses are common in formal writing.

肮.origin: 肮 joins flesh with phonetic 亢. Alone it is rare; bound in 肮脏 it means dirty or filthy, both literally and morally. The flesh radical hints at bodily uncleanness.
肮.history: Modern Mandarin almost always uses 肮 inside 肮脏. Literary abuse and hygiene vocabulary sustain the compound.

parts.note: foot / stop; early forms related to flesh of the bone

肯.origin: Early forms depicted flesh adhering to bone, conveying something firm and substantial. From that concrete sense the word extended to mental assent—being willing or ready to agree. In modern writing the lower component is written with the flesh radical 月.
肯.history: Seal and clerical scripts already show the flesh element; the simplified and traditional forms are identical. The concrete anatomical sense faded, leaving the modal meaning 'willing / consent.'

肾.origin: A phono-semantic character for the kidney: the flesh radical marks an internal organ, while the right side is a simplified remnant of the traditional phonetic. In Classical Chinese it named the kidney as one of the five zang organs.
肾.history: Traditional 腎 uses 臤 as phonetic; simplified 肾 compresses that element. The character has long been central in Chinese medicine texts naming the kidneys.

parts.note: field-like enclosure suggesting the stomach pouch

胃.origin: The character depicts a filled pouch-like organ: early graphs show a stomach bag with contents, later standardized with a field-like top and the flesh radical below. It names the digestive organ and, by extension, appetite.
胃.history: Oracle and bronze forms already suggest a bag of food; seal script settles the 田-over-月 layout still used today. Traditional and simplified forms match.

背.meaning: back (of the body); turn one's back; carry on the back (bēi)
背.origin: 北 originally pictured two people standing back-to-back and later meant 'north.' Adding the flesh radical marks the anatomical back. The same graph covers turning one's back, memorizing by rote, and (with tone bēi) carrying on the back.
背.history: Seal forms combine 北 with flesh; the structure is unchanged in modern script. Multiple readings (bèi / bēi) distinguish noun/verb uses in Mandarin.

parts.note: flesh radical; also linked to capacity/strength

胜.origin: Traditional 勝 is phono-semantic with a phonetic related to rising force; simplified 胜 keeps flesh plus 生. Core senses are to prevail, be superior, or be able to bear a load—hence 'victory' and 'surpass.'
胜.history: Clerical and regular scripts stabilize the traditional form; PRC simplification replaces the complex right side with 生. Common in compounds like 胜利 and 名胜.

parts.note: wrap / enclose; also hints at enclosing

胞.origin: 包 suggests wrapping; with the flesh radical it names the placenta that wraps the fetus. By extension it means siblings born of the same womb (同胞) and, in modern scientific Chinese, biological cells (细胞).
胞.history: The phono-semantic structure is stable from seal script onward. Scientific senses of 'cell' are modern extensions of the womb/enclosure idea.

胡.meaning: beard; reckless; non-Han peoples of the north/west; interrogative particle (classical)

parts.note: flesh; early sense related to dewlap/jowl

胡.origin: Originally linked to the fleshy dewlap under an animal's jaw, then to facial hair. It became a label for northern and western peoples (胡人), and later a prefix for foreign goods or reckless action (胡说). Classical Chinese also uses it as 'why.'
胡.history: Bronze and seal forms show 古 plus flesh. Semantic range widened through contact with steppe peoples and everyday compounds; form unchanged in simplified Chinese.

胳.meaning: armpit; upper arm (as in 胳膊)
胳.origin: A body-part graph: flesh plus phonetic 各. Alone it points to the armpit region; in 胳膊 it names the arm. The phonetic does not contribute meaning beyond sound.
胳.history: Appears in dictionaries as a standard flesh-radical phono-semantic. Common in spoken compounds rather than as a free word.

parts.note: flesh/animal tissue from which glue was boiled

胶.origin: Animal glue was made by boiling hide and connective tissue, so the flesh radical is apt. 交 provides the sound and echoes the idea of joining. Meanings cover adhesive, rubber, and sticky colloids.
胶.history: Traditional 膠 uses a more complex phonetic; simplified 胶 uses 交. Industrial senses (rubber, plastic) are later extensions.

能.meaning: can; be able to; ability; energy

parts.note: originally a bear-like animal; later abstract ability

能.origin: Early graphs depict a sturdy bear-like creature, a symbol of strength. From physical power the word shifted to capability—'can / be able to'—and later to abstract ability and (in compounds) energy or skill.
能.history: Oracle and bronze forms are clearly zoomorphic; seal script stylizes the animal into the modern silhouette. Traditional and simplified forms are the same.

parts.note: gives sound in the simplified form

脏.origin: Traditional 臟 (organs) and 髒 (dirty) were distinct; simplified Chinese merges both as 脏. With reading zàng it means visceral organs; with zāng it means filthy. The flesh radical ties both to the body.
脏.history: Simplification collapsed two traditional characters into one graph with two readings. Context and tone usually disambiguate organ vs. dirty senses.

parts.note: flesh radical for a body organ
simplified remnant of traditional phonetic 𡿺/囟-related component

脑.origin: The flesh radical marks an anatomical organ; the right side continues a phonetic that once related to the fontanelle (囟). Together they name the brain and, by metaphor, mental ability (头脑).
脑.history: Traditional 腦 has a denser right component; simplified 脑 keeps flesh plus a compressed phonetic. Usage spans anatomy and 'mind/brains.'

脖.origin: A straightforward flesh-plus-phonetic graph for the neck. It is the everyday word for the anatomical neck (脖子), as opposed to more literary 颈.
脖.history: Stable phono-semantic structure in regular script. Frequent in colloquial compounds; form identical in traditional and simplified.

parts.note: gives sound (traditional phonetic related to 卻)

脚.origin: Flesh plus a phonetic yields the word for foot (and sometimes lower leg). Extended senses include the base of an object and roles in theater (脚色, now often 角色).
脚.history: Traditional 腳 uses 卻 as phonetic; simplified 脚 uses 却. One of the most common limb terms in modern Mandarin.

脱.meaning: take off; shed; escape; leave out
脱.origin: Originally tied to peeling flesh from bone or shedding skin; the flesh radical plus phonetic 兑. Meanings broadened to taking off clothes, escaping, and omitting.
脱.history: Seal forms already show the flesh-plus-兑 layout. Traditional 脫 and simplified 脱 differ only slightly in the phonetic's strokes.

parts.note: flesh radical for the face as body part

脸.origin: A flesh-radical character naming the face. Beyond anatomy it carries social senses of face and dignity (丢脸, 脸面), parallel to 面子.
脸.history: Traditional 臉 uses 僉; simplified 脸 uses 佥. Became the dominant colloquial word for 'face,' partly displacing 面 in speech.

parts.note: meat (often written as 月 in related forms); spoilage of flesh

腐.origin: The graph associates stored or enclosed meat with spoilage—hence 'rotten.' Figurative uses cover moral corruption. In 豆腐 the second syllable keeps this character though the food is soybean curd, not spoiled meat.
腐.history: Shuowen analyzes it with meat under/ beside 府. Form is the same in simplified Chinese; tofu usage is a fixed later lexicalization.

腕.origin: Flesh plus phonetic 宛 names the wrist joint. It is mainly an anatomical and clothing-related term (手腕, 腕表).
腕.history: Standard flesh-radical phono-semantic from dictionaries of the clerical era onward. Unchanged under simplification.

parts.note: gives sound; 要 itself once depicted a person with hands at the waist

腰.origin: 要 originally showed a person with arms akimbo at the midriff and meant 'waist' before shifting to 'want / important.' Adding flesh produced a dedicated body-part character 腰 for the waist and lumbar region.
腰.history: The split between 要 (abstract) and 腰 (anatomical) is clear by Han times. Form identical in traditional and simplified scripts.

腹.origin: A classic organ/torso graph: flesh plus phonetic 复. It names the belly and appears in compounds for anger or drafts (腹稿, 心腹).
腹.history: Traditional 腹 matches the simplified form. Long attested in medical and literary Chinese.

腾.origin: Built around the horse radical to suggest surging forward or upward—galloping, soaring, rising. Extended senses include making room (腾出) and bubbling up.
腾.history: Traditional 騰 has a complex right phonetic; simplified 腾 keeps 马 with a reduced form. Frequent in 腾飞, 奔腾, and brand names.

parts.note: flesh radical (肉), marking a body part
retreat / step back; sound tuì≈tuǐ; evokes leg movement

腿.origin: The character 腿 includes the radical 月 (yuè, often related to the body) on the left, indicating its relation to the body, and 退 (tuì, meaning "to retreat" or "step back") on the right, symbolizing movement associated with legs. Together they name the leg as the limb used in stepping and withdrawing.
腿.history: A relatively transparent modern flesh-plus-phonetic form. Dominant colloquial word for 'leg,' alongside more formal 股 in some compounds.

膀.meaning: shoulder; upper arm; bladder (páng in 膀胱)

parts.note: gives sound; 'side' also suits the shoulder

膀.origin: Flesh plus 旁 ('side') fits the shoulder at the body's side (肩膀, 翅膀 for wings). In 膀胱 (pángguāng) the same graph names the urinary bladder with a different reading.
膀.history: Readings bǎng vs. páng split by compound. Structure unchanged since regular script; identical in simplified Chinese.

膊.meaning: upper arm; arm (as in 胳膊)
膊.origin: Names the upper arm or arm segment. Almost always bound in 胳膊 rather than used alone. Flesh radical marks the body part; 尃 is phonetic.
膊.history: Standard dictionary form from clerical script on. Unchanged under simplification.

parts.note: flesh radical for thin bodily tissue

膜.origin: Describes a thin layer of tissue—membrane—and by extension films, screens, and coatings (薄膜, 耳膜). Flesh plus phonetic 莫.
膜.history: Medical and technical senses proliferated in modern Chinese while the graph stayed the same. Traditional and simplified match.

parts.note: eye turned downward in submission / watchful eye

臣.origin: A pictograph of a vertical eye, often explained as a watchful or downcast gaze of a servant. It came to mean a retainer or minister serving a ruler, and a humble first-person pronoun in court speech.
臣.history: Oracle-bone eyes already tilt like 臣; seal script fixes the modern outline. Same form in simplified Chinese; productive in 大臣, 君臣.

自.origin: Originally a drawing of the nose. People point to their nose when referring to themselves, so the graph came to mean 'self' and then 'from / since.' It is also the phonetic/semantic core of many characters (息, 臭).
自.history: Oracle forms clearly show a nose; the abstract 'self' sense dominates early texts. Form unchanged through simplification.

至.origin: Early graphs show an arrow reaching the earth—arrival at a target. From 'arrive' it extended to the preposition 'to / until' and the adverb 'extremely.'
至.history: Bronze and seal forms preserve the arrow-to-ground idea. Identical in traditional and simplified; common grammatical word.

致.meaning: cause; send; deliver; fine / delicate (致密)

parts.note: hand with stick; action / bringing about

致.origin: Combining 'arrive' with the action radical 攵 yields 'bring about / cause to reach'—hence cause, send, or deliver. Related senses include dedicating effort and fine texture.
致.history: Seal script shows 至 plus 攵. Traditional and simplified forms match. Productive in 导致, 以致, 精致.

parts.note: person under a roof-like top (or tongue/roof analysis varies)
component historically linked in analyses of 舍

舍.origin: Often analyzed as a dwelling: a roof over a structure, meaning a hut or inn (宿舍, 房舍). With reading shě it means to abandon or give up (舍得, 舍弃)—a verbal extension from leaving a place.
舍.history: Early forms vary; modern 舍 serves both noun and verb readings. Simplified Chinese keeps this form (traditional also 捨 for the 'give up' verb in some texts).

parts.note: two feet pointing opposite ways; stepping
traditional upper/phonetic element; simplified still echoes dancing figure with ornaments

舞.origin: Depicts a dancer with hanging ornaments and emphasized feet (舛). The graph captures rhythmic stepping and spectacle, yielding the verb/noun 'dance.'
舞.history: Oracle and bronze dancers with plumes are ancestral; seal script adds clear foot components. Simplified 舞 is close to traditional 舞.

航.origin: Boat plus phonetic 亢 means to sail or navigate. Extended to aviation and spaceflight (航天, 航空) while keeping the travel-by-craft idea.
航.history: Maritime sense is oldest; 20th-century compounds reused it for air and space. Form identical in simplified Chinese.

parts.note: warehouse / storehouse; also hints at enclosed space

舱.origin: A boat's enclosed storage or passenger space: 舟 for vessel and 仓 (warehouse) for an enclosed room. Used for ship holds and aircraft cabins.
舱.history: Traditional 艙 uses 倉; simplified 舱 uses 仓. Modern transport vocabulary expanded its use from ships to planes.

船.origin: The standard everyday word for boat or ship: semantic 舟 plus phonetic. Covers vessels from small boats to large ships and appears in countless nautical compounds.
船.history: Seal and clerical forms already match the modern layout. Traditional and simplified are the same character.

parts.note: person and suggestion of countenance / appearance

色.origin: Early interpretations link it to facial appearance or a person kneeling with distinctive form—hence 'look / countenance,' then 'color,' and further 'type' or sensual desire. It is the basic word for color in modern Chinese.
色.history: Seal script fixes the modern shape. Broad semantic range is already classical; form unchanged by simplification.

艳.origin: Combines lush abundance with color/appearance to mean richly beautiful or brightly colored. Extended to romantic or flirtatious overtones (艳遇).
艳.history: Traditional 豔/艷 uses fuller components; simplified 艳 uses 丰 + 色. Common in literary and advertising language.

艺.origin: Traditional 藝 relates to planting and cultivated skill; simplified 艺 keeps the grass radical with a short phonetic. Meanings cover technique, art, and the arts (艺术, 技艺).
艺.history: From agricultural skill to fine arts is a long semantic path. PRC simplification drastically shortens the traditional graph to 艺.

parts.note: bamboo/grass related; nodes of bamboo in traditional analysis
seal / kneeling person component giving sound

节.origin: Tied to bamboo nodes (节) as natural joints, then to segments, seasons/festivals, moral integrity, and thrift. The grass/bamboo classifier plus a phonetic seal component.
节.history: Traditional 節 is bamboo-radical; simplified 节 uses the grass radical with 卩. Extremely frequent grammatical and lexical item.

芦.meaning: reed; rush (as in 葫芦 gourd)
芦.origin: A plant name for reeds and related tall grasses. Famous in 葫芦 (gourd) and reed instruments or imagery.
芦.history: Traditional 蘆 has a fuller phonetic; simplified 芦 uses 户. Remains a botanical and folk-culture character.

芭.meaning: banana plant (芭蕉); used in plant names
芭.origin: Plant radical plus 巴 names certain broad-leaved plants, especially banana (芭蕉). Mostly bound in plant names rather than free use.
芭.history: Phono-semantic structure long stable. Unchanged under simplification.

花.origin: Grass radical plus 化 yields 'flower'—the bloom that plants transform into. Extended senses include fireworks patterns, spending money (花钱), and variegated designs.
花.history: Appears widely from medieval vernacular on as the everyday word for flower (alongside literary 华). Same form in simplified Chinese.

芽.origin: Names a plant sprout: grass radical with phonetic 牙. Metaphorically used for emerging ideas or budding talent.
芽.history: Clear phono-semantic from seal/clerical traditions. Form identical traditionally and in simplified script.

苇.origin: A plant character for reeds (芦苇). Grass radical plus simplified phonetic 韦.
苇.history: Traditional 葦 uses 韋; simplified 苇 uses 韦. Common in nature imagery and place names.

parts.note: simplified phonetic from traditional 穌/甦-related forms

苏.origin: Linked to plants (perilla 紫苏) and to revival/awakening (苏醒)—traditional variants connected 'fish in grass' or 'more life' analyses. Also abbreviates Jiangsu and the Soviet Union (苏联, historical).
苏.history: Traditional 蘇 is complex; simplified 苏 is much shorter. Multiple readings of history (plant vs. revive) coexist in compounds.

苔.origin: Grass radical plus 台 names mosses and similar low plants carpeting damp surfaces. Used in botanical and scenic descriptions.
苔.history: Stable phono-semantic; same in traditional and simplified Chinese.

若.origin: The character 若 is composed of the radical 艹 (cǎo), which means "grass," and the phonetic component 右 (yòu), which contributes to the pronunciation. Historically, 若 depicted a type of plant and later evolved to express conditions or comparisons. It became a common classical conjunction meaning 'if / as if.'
若.history: Plant sense faded early; grammatical use dominates Classical Chinese and remains in set phrases (若是, 若非). Form unchanged by simplification.

苦.origin: Named bitter-tasting plants, then generalized to any bitter flavor and to suffering or hard work. Grass plus phonetic 古.
苦.history: One of the five flavors in Chinese thought. Form identical in traditional and simplified scripts.

英.origin: Originally the blossom or fine petal of a plant—hence outstanding beauty or talent, then 'hero' (英雄). Also the standard abbreviation for England/English.
英.history: Floral sense is classical; heroic and country-name uses are later specializations. Same graph traditionally and simplified.

parts.note: plant radical in the simplified/common form

范.origin: Related to molds and norms: traditional 範 (bamboo radical) meant a casting mold or model; simplified writing often uses 范 for those senses and as a surname. Conveys pattern, example, and scope (范围).
范.history: Simplification merged or preferred 范 for many 範 uses. Extremely common in 范围, 模范, and names.

草.origin: The basic word for grass and herbaceous plants. Extended to straw goods, cursive 'grass script' (草书), and draft documents (草稿)—quick, rough writing like wild grass.
草.history: Ubiquitous from earliest texts. Form unchanged under simplification.

荐.origin: Tied to straw mats or fodder offered up, then to presenting a person for office—hence 'recommend.' Grass radical marks plant material.
荐.history: Traditional 薦 is more complex; simplified 荐 is shorter. Common in 推荐 and formal recommendation language.

parts.note: gives sound; water/hot water ties to washing

荡.origin: Traditional 盪/蕩 relates to washing or shaking with water; meanings include to swing, rinse, loaf about, and barren emptiness (荡然). Simplified 荡 uses grass plus 汤.
荡.history: PRC simplification standardizes 荡. Appears in 动荡, 荡漾, and 扫荡.

parts.note: simplified tree/cover phonetic remnant from 榮

荣.origin: Traditional 榮 shows twin fires under a tree—brilliant flowering and glory. Simplified 荣 keeps a plant top over a simplified base. Meanings: flourish, honor, glory (光荣, 繁荣).
荣.history: Bronze imagery of lush brilliance underlies the glory sense. Simplification shortens 榮 to 荣.

药.origin: Chinese medicine began with herbs, so the grass radical is natural. Phonetic 约 yields 药, the general word for medicine or chemicals used as drugs.
药.history: Traditional 藥 is complex; simplified 药 uses 约. Central vocabulary in medical and daily life.

莫.origin: Originally the sun sinking into the grass—sundown. That word was later written 暮, while 莫 was borrowed for the negative 'do not / there is none,' its main classical and modern function.
莫.history: Famous rebus/loan path from 'dusk' to negative adverb. Form stable; also phonetic in 膜, 摸, etc.

parts.note: plants / field harvest in simplified merge
dog radical in traditional hunting sense (獲)

获.origin: Traditional 獲 (catch with hound) and 穫 (grain harvest) both simplify to 获. Core meaning is to get or capture, whether game, grain, or abstract gains.
获.history: Simplification merges two traditional characters. Extremely common verb: 获得, 捕获, 收获.

parts.note: pick / gather; also suggests harvesting greens

菜.origin: Plants you gather to eat: vegetables, then any cooked dish, and by extension cuisine styles. 采 as phonetic also echoes picking.
菜.history: Everyday food vocabulary from early imperial texts onward. Same form traditionally and simplified.

萌.origin: Means a plant beginning to sprout. Modern internet slang borrowed Japanese moe senses for 'cute,' layered on the budding metaphor.
萌.history: Classical botanical/inceptive uses; slang sense is 21st-century. Graph unchanged by simplification.

parts.note: often linked to enclosing frames / simplified from 營

营.origin: Traditional 營 depicts orderly enclosures—military camps—and extends to managing, operating, or seeking a living (营业, 营生). Simplified 营 preserves a compact form of that idea.
营.history: From military camp to business operations is a natural extension. PRC form 营 replaces complex 營.

萧.origin: Evokes sparse, withered plants—desolation and autumn chill (萧条). Also a common surname. Related 箫 is the vertical bamboo flute.
萧.history: Traditional 蕭 uses 肅; simplified 萧 uses 肃. Literary staple for bleak scenery.

落.origin: Grass radical with phonetic 洛 captures leaves falling and, generally, dropping or settling. Extended to whereabouts, declining fortune, and completing a task (落地).
落.history: Very frequent verb with multiple readings in dialects; standard luò. Form same traditionally and simplified.

著.meaning: write; notable; wear (zháo/zhe variants via 着)
著.origin: Means to make evident or to author a work (著名, 著作). The particle/verb 'wear / catch / continuous aspect' split into simplified 着 while traditional texts often keep 著 for both.
著.history: Simplification assigned many grammatical uses to 着, leaving 著 mainly for 'write / notable' in PRC orthography.

葬.origin: Shows death placed amid grass—burial under vegetation or earth. The character straightforwardly means to bury the dead and appears in funeral vocabulary (葬礼).
葬.history: Seal analyses emphasize grass above and below the dead. Form essentially the same in modern scripts.

蒙.origin: Suggests being covered by vegetation—hence to cover, to be kept in the dark, or to receive (承蒙). Also abbreviates Mongolia (蒙古) with reading měng.
蒙.history: Multiple tones/readings in Mandarin (méng / mēng / měng). Traditional and simplified forms match.

蓝.origin: Named the indigo plant used for blue dye, then the color blue itself. Grass radical plus phonetic 监 (from traditional 監).
蓝.history: Traditional 藍 → simplified 蓝. Basic color term in modern Mandarin.

蔓.origin: Describes trailing vines and the way they spread (蔓延). Phonetic 曼 also suggests extension.
蔓.history: Botanical and figurative 'spread' senses are both classical. Same form in simplified Chinese.

蔽.origin: Foliage that screens something from sight—to cover, obscure, or shield. Common in 隐蔽 and 蔽塞.
蔽.history: Phono-semantic structure long established. Unchanged under simplification.

蕾.origin: Specifically a flower bud before it opens (花蕾). Grass radical with phonetic 雷.
蕾.history: More literary/botanical than basic 芽. Form identical traditionally and simplified.

藏.meaning: to hide; to conceal; store; Tibet (zàng)
藏.origin: To hide or store away, as if under plants. Reading cáng is the verb 'hide / store'; zàng appears in 西藏 (Tibet) and 宝藏 (treasure store).
藏.history: Both readings are ancient. Form the same in simplified Chinese; context chooses cáng vs. zàng.

藤.origin: Names woody climbing vines and rattan used for furniture (藤椅). Grass radical plus phonetic 滕.
藤.history: Material-culture uses are longstanding. Traditional and simplified forms match.

parts.note: tiger with open jaws and striped body
tiger radical derived from the same pictograph

虎.origin: A vivid pictograph of a tiger—jaws, legs, and stripes stylized over time. Symbol of fierceness and courage; the radical 虍 builds many 'tiger-related' characters.
虎.history: Oracle-bone tigers are unmistakable; seal script yields the modern 虎. Same in simplified Chinese; cultural icon across eras.

parts.note: in traditional 慮 the phonetic-semantic mix includes 思

虑.origin: Traditional 慮 combines tiger and thought/heart elements for careful consideration and anxiety. Simplified 虑 keeps tiger over heart. Core senses: think over, be concerned.
虑.history: PRC simplification shortens 慮 to 虑. Common in 考虑, 忧虑.

parts.note: tiger radical as classifier in Shuowen tradition
simplified lower phonetic from traditional 虛

虚.origin: Means empty, hollow, or unreal—also modest (谦虚) and physically weak. Tiger radical sits over a phonetic that once suggested a hill or empty mound.
虚.history: Traditional 虛 → simplified 虚. Philosophical and medical texts use it heavily (虚实).

parts.note: insect radical in the modern simplified form

虽.origin: Classical concessive 'although.' Traditional 雖 is analyzed with a phonetic and mouth/insect-related components; simplified 虽 is much shorter but keeps the same grammatical role.
虽.history: High-frequency classical particle surviving in modern written Chinese (虽然). Simplification yields 虽.

蚁.origin: Insect radical plus phonetic names the ant. Usually appears in 蚂蚁 as the everyday word.
蚁.history: Traditional 蟻 uses 義; simplified 蚁 uses 义. Stable insect-radical pattern.

蚂.origin: Bound form used in 蚂蚁 (ant) and some other insect names. Insect radical with phonetic 马.
蚂.history: Traditional 螞 → simplified 蚂. Rarely used alone in modern Mandarin.

蛋.origin: Originally related to reptile or insect eggs; generalized to bird eggs and any egg-shaped object. Also appears in insults and slang.
蛋.history: Became the dominant colloquial word for egg (vs. literary 卵). Form same in simplified Chinese.

蜓.origin: Almost always bound in 蜻蜓 (dragonfly). Insect radical with phonetic 廷.
蜓.history: Specialized insect name; structure stable across scripts.

蜜.origin: Points to honey produced by bees: insect radical plus phonetic 宓. Extended to sweetness and intimate affection (甜蜜, 蜜月).
蜜.history: Classical word for honey still used daily. Traditional and simplified forms are identical.

蜻.origin: 蜻 is a phono-semantic compound: the insect radical 虫 marks bugs, while 青 (qīng) supplies the pronunciation. It almost always appears in the binome 蜻蜓 for “dragonfly.” The green/blue sense of 青 also resonates with the insect’s typical coloring.
蜻.history: Formed as insect vocabulary expanded in the classical period; remains specialized as part of 蜻蜓 in modern Chinese.

融.origin: 融 originally concerns melting or stewing associated with a cooking cauldron (鬲). Heat causes substances to fuse, giving the core sense “melt; dissolve.” From physical melting the meaning broadened to blending peoples, ideas, or funds, and to harmonious fusion.
融.history: Oracle and bronze forms emphasize the vessel; modern senses include finance (融资) and cultural fusion (融合).

蠢.origin: 蠢 shows insects (䖵) with 春, suggesting creatures stirring and wriggling in spring. The concrete sense “wriggle; stir” later developed the figurative “clumsy; stupid.” Many dictionaries treat 春 as phonetic and the insect component as semantic.
蠢.history: Classical texts use 蠢 for insect movement and dull-wittedness; the pejorative sense dominates everyday Mandarin.

composition.formula: pictograph of blood in a vessel

血.origin: Blood is a pictograph of a sacrificial vessel (皿) with a drop of blood above or in it. The image records ritual blood rather than abstract anatomy. From that concrete picture came the general noun “blood” and many compounds about kinship and violence.
血.history: Early forms show the vessel-plus-drop motif. Mandarin has xuè and colloquial xiě; the given hint prefers xiě.

行.origin: 行 depicts a crossroads or a path for walking, hence “to go; to walk; to travel.” The same graph later developed the reading háng for “row; line; trade.” Motion and orderly movement along a road underlie both senses.
行.history: Extremely common from oracle bones onward. Modern Mandarin keeps both xíng and háng readings.

街.origin: 街 places the road component 行 around the phonetic 圭, naming a public thoroughfare. The semantic half makes the “street” meaning transparent: a place where people go. It contrasts with narrower alleys in traditional urban vocabulary.
街.history: Used for city streets since early imperial Chinese; standard in modern 街道 and 逛街.

parts.note: upper garment with collar and sleeves

衣.origin: 衣 is a pictograph of an upper garment, showing collar, sleeves, and the hanging body of a robe. It is the basic word for clothes and the source of the clothing radical 衤. From concrete garments it extends to “to wear” and figurative covering.
衣.history: Oracle-bone forms already look like a jacket. As a radical it classifies hundreds of clothing-related characters.

表.meaning: surface; exterior; to show; watch; form

parts.note: fur on the outside of a garment

表.origin: 表 originally meant the outer fur side of clothing—毛 (fur) over 衣 (garment)—hence “surface; exterior.” From showing the outside it came to mean “to express,” and later “table; form” and “wristwatch.” The idea of an outer display unifies these senses.
表.history: Classical Chinese uses 表 for outer garments and memorials; modern Chinese adds forms and timepieces (手表).

parts.note: skin or leather; also suggests a covering

被.origin: The character "被" combines the radical "衤" (yī, meaning "clothing") on the left, which indicates its relation to covering or wrapping, and "皮" (pí, meaning "skin" or "leather") on the right, suggesting a covering. Its earliest concrete sense is a quilt or cloak that covers the body. From "to cover; to suffer" it grammaticalized into the passive marker "by."
被.history: Early texts use 被 for bedding and undergoing an action; the passive construction remains central in modern Mandarin.

裁.meaning: to cut (cloth); to judge; to decide
裁.origin: 裁 shows cutting cloth for garments: the clothing element marks fabric, while the weapon-like phonetic suggests cutting or deciding. From tailor’s cutting it extended to “trim; reduce” and to judicial or managerial “decision; judgment.”
裁.history: Common for both sewing (裁缝) and judgment (裁判, 总裁).

裂.origin: 裂 pairs the clothing radical with 列 to depict fabric tearing apart. The image of a garment ripping gave the general verb “split; crack.” Figurative uses include social rupture and geological fissures.
裂.history: Attested for torn clothing in classical Chinese; modern compounds include 分裂 and 裂纹.

装.meaning: to dress; to pack; outfit; to pretend
装.origin: 装 is clothing plus a phonetic related to 壮/壯, originally “to put on clothes; to outfit.” Packing goods and installing equipment extend from putting things into place. The sense “to pretend” comes from putting on an appearance.
装.history: Traditional form 裝; simplified 装. Productive in 服装, 包装, 安装, 假装.

裡.origin: 裡 shows the clothing radical with 里, originally the lining of a garment—the inner layer. From the lining of clothes it generalized to “inside; within.” It is the traditional form used when ‘inside’ is written with the clothing radical.
裡.history: Traditional script keeps 裡 for ‘inside’ while 里 also means village; simplified Chinese often merges both as 里.

parts.note: bird on nest at sunset, or a basket

西.origin: 西 names the western direction. A common traditional explanation sees a bird settling into its nest as the sun sets in the west; another links early forms to a basket later borrowed for the direction. Either way, the graph became the standard cardinal “west.”
西.history: Fixed early as one of the four directions and later as shorthand for Western countries and culture.

parts.note: upper part of early waist form

composition.formula: pictograph of hands at the waist

要.origin: 要 originally depicted hands akimbo at the waist (later written 腰 for ‘waist’). From the body’s midsection—the critical joint—came “essential; important,” then modal “must; will” and “to want.” The waist image thus underlies both necessity and desire.
要.history: Classical Chinese keeps many ‘important’ uses; vernacular Mandarin made yào a core modal and volitional verb.

見.origin: 見 shows an eye (目) over a person, vividly meaning “to see.” Meeting someone face-to-face is a natural extension of seeing them. The graph is the traditional form of simplified 见 and the root of many vision and opinion words.
見.history: Present from oracle bones; traditional 見 remains standard in Taiwan/HK, while mainland China uses 见.

见.origin: 见 is the simplified form of 見: an eye above a person, meaning “to see.” It also means “to meet” and, as a noun element, “view; opinion” (意见). The composition keeps the eye-and-person idea in fewer strokes.
见.history: Adopted in mainland simplified script; same core meanings and compounds as traditional 見.

parts.note: simplified remnant of traditional 雚 elements

观.origin: 观 is the simplified form of 觀, which combined a phonetic (related to 雚) with 見 “see,” meaning careful observation. From watching came “viewpoint” and, via religious lookout halls, “Taoist temple.”
观.history: Traditional 觀; simplified 观. Core modern uses include 观察, 观点, and 乐观.

规.origin: 规 (traditional 規) relates to the drawing compass and to standards one “sees” and follows. The tool that draws a perfect circle became a metaphor for rules and regulations. Advising and planning (规定, 规划) grow from that normative sense.
规.history: Classical texts already use 規 for compass and ritual norms; simplified 规 is standard in mainland Chinese.

视.meaning: to look at; to regard; vision
视.origin: 视 (traditional 視) centers on looking: 見/见 supplies the seeing sense, while the other component historically relates to ritual attention. It means to look at, inspect, or regard something as. Abstract “vision” and “viewpoint” senses follow from directed looking.
视.history: Common in classical Chinese for inspecting; modern compounds include 电视, 重视, and 视角.

觉.meaning: to feel; to become aware; (jiào) sleep

parts.note: simplified top from phonetic in 覺

觉.origin: 觉 simplifies 覺, which joins a phonetic element with 見 to mean perception or awakening. As jué it is “to feel; to realize”; as jiào it is a period of sleep (睡觉). Sensory and cognitive awareness remain central.
觉.history: Dual readings are long-standing; simplified 觉 is used for both in mainland Mandarin.

角.origin: 角 is a pictograph of an animal’s horn. From the pointed horn came “corner” and geometric “angle.” A separate reading jué developed for theatrical roles and competition, still tied to the graph’s sharp, distinctive outline.
角.history: Oracle forms clearly show a horn. Modern Chinese keeps jiǎo for corners and jué for roles (角色).

解.meaning: to untie; to explain; to solve
解.origin: 解 famously depicts separating an ox with a knife at the horn—dismembering or untying a carcass. From physically taking apart came “untie; release,” then “explain” and “solve” by taking a problem apart. The graphic story makes the abstract senses vivid.
解.history: Classic compound ideograph in traditional pedagogy; readings also include xiè in names and jiè in some compounds.

触.meaning: to touch; to contact; to offend

parts.note: simplified from 蜀 in 觸

触.origin: 触 simplifies 觸, where a horn (角) contacts something—an ox butting—while 蜀 was phonetic. Touch, contact, and triggering emotions or taboos all grow from that physical collision.
触.history: Traditional 觸; simplified 触. Modern uses include 接触, 触摸, and 触电.

composition.formula: pictograph of speech from the mouth

言.origin: 言 depicts speech issuing from the mouth (often analyzed as 口 with a mark for tongue or sound). It means words, speech, or to say. As a radical (讠/言) it heads a huge family of language-related characters.
言.history: Core graph from the earliest scripts; simplified speech radical 讠 derives from it in mainland Chinese.

警.origin: 警 combines 敬 as phonetic with speech (言) to mean a verbal warning. Alertness and caution follow, and in modern Chinese the police (警察) are those who warn and keep watch.
警.history: Classical ‘warn; admonish’ senses precede the modern institutional ‘police’ compounds.

譬.origin: 譬 is speech (言) with phonetic 辟, meaning to explain by comparison—an analogy or parable. Using words to set one case beside another is the core idea. It appears in literary Chinese for illustrations and examples.
譬.history: Retained mainly in written registers (譬如); less common as a spoken everyday verb than 比如.

计.origin: 计 (traditional 計) joins speech with 十, evoking counting and deliberating a plan in words. Calculation, strategy, and measuring devices (温度计) all stem from tallying and planning.
计.history: Used for accounts and schemes since classical times; highly productive in modern vocabulary.

parts.note: phonetic dīng/dìng; hint links sense to 定 settle

订.origin: The character "订" consists of the radical "讠" (yán, meaning "speech") and "定" (dìng, meaning "to settle"), symbolizing agreeing to a set order or subscription through communication. In the written form the right side appears as "丁," which supplies the dìng sound alongside that settling sense. Core uses include concluding an agreement, revising a text, and booking or ordering something.
订.history: Traditional 訂. Modern Chinese uses it for reservations and subscriptions (订票, 订阅) as well as editing (修订).

认.meaning: to recognize; to admit; to accept
认.origin: 认 simplifies 認: speech plus a phonetic (人/忍-related) meaning to acknowledge with words. Recognizing a person or fact and admitting responsibility are both acts of verbally affirming what one knows.
认.history: Traditional 認; simplified 认. Central to 认识, 认真, and 承认.

讨.origin: The character "讨" combines the radical "讠" (yán, meaning "speech" or "words") on the left, indicating its relation to communication, with "寸" (cùn, meaning "inch" or "small") on the right, suggesting the act of speaking or asking. Historically, it implied a small or humble request. Later senses broadened to discuss, demand, denounce, and even punitive campaigns framed in words.
讨.history: Traditional 討. Classical military ‘punitive campaign’ sense coexists with everyday 讨论 and 讨厌.

parts.note: simplified phonetic remnant of 襄 in 讓

让.origin: 让 is the simplified form of 讓, speech with phonetic 襄, meaning to yield or cede through words and gesture. From yielding came “to let; to allow” and causative “to make someone do.” Politeness and transfer of rights are both rooted in stepping aside.
让.history: Traditional 讓 remains in some regions; mainland 让 is a high-frequency causative and permissive verb.

训.origin: 训 (traditional 訓) is speech guided by phonetic 川, meaning instruction or explanatory teaching. Training troops or students and interpreting classical texts (训诂) both involve authoritative speech that sets a standard.
训.history: Long used for moral and scholastic instruction; modern 训练 and 教训 keep teaching and admonishing senses.

讯.origin: 讯 (traditional 訊) joins speech with phonetic 卂, meaning a spoken message or inquiry. News that travels quickly and formal interrogation both involve eliciting or transmitting words. Modern ‘message; information’ senses stay close to that core.
讯.history: Used for messenger reports and court questioning; today common in 通讯, 喜讯, and 讯息.

记.origin: 记 (traditional 記) is speech/writing with phonetic 己, meaning to note down or keep in mind. Recording events in words and remembering them are two sides of the same act. Marks, journalists, and diaries all extend from that notation sense.
记.history: Fundamental literacy character; simplified 记 is ubiquitous in 记忆, 日记, and 记者.

讲.origin: 讲 simplifies 講: the speech radical with a phonetic historically related to 冓, meaning to talk through or explain. Speaking to an audience and emphasizing a principle (讲理) both highlight structured verbal exposition.
讲.history: Traditional 講; simplified 讲 is the everyday verb for speak/explain in Mandarin (讲话, 讲课).

许.meaning: to allow; to promise; perhaps; surname
许.origin: 许 (traditional 許) pairs speech with phonetic 午, originally to approve or promise in words. Permission, betrothal promises, and the adverbial “perhaps; about” (也许, 许多) developed from that assent. It is also a common surname.
许.history: Classical approval and promise senses remain; modern Mandarin uses it constantly in 许多 and 允许.

设.meaning: to set up; to establish; if
设.origin: 设 (traditional 設) is speech with phonetic 殳, meaning to arrange or establish—laying something out in words and in fact. Facilities, design, and the hypothetical “suppose; if” (假设) all come from setting up a situation.
设.history: Used for ritual arrangements and institutions in classical Chinese; modern 设计 and 设备 are core vocabulary.

访.meaning: to visit; to interview; to inquire
访.origin: 访 (traditional 訪) combines speech with phonetic 方, meaning to go and ask—visiting to inquire. Social visits, official investigations, and journalistic interviews all keep the sense of seeking someone out for talk.
访.history: Classical ‘inquire; visit’ uses continue in 访问, 采访, and 拜访.

证.origin: 证 simplifies aspects of 證/証: speech with 正, pointing to testimony that sets things straight. Proof, evidence, and certificates are verbal or written confirmations of truth. Medical and legal compounds preserve this verifying sense.
证.history: Traditional forms 證/証; simplified 证 is standard for 证明, 证件, and 保证.

评.origin: 评 (traditional 評) is speech with 平, suggesting a leveled, balanced judgment in words. Commenting, reviewing, and scoring all involve stating an appraisal. Fairness associations of 平 reinforce critical evaluation.
评.history: Common in literary criticism and modern media (评论, 评价, 批评).

诅.meaning: to curse; to swear an oath
诅.origin: 诅 (traditional 詛) joins speech with phonetic 且 to mean a spoken curse or solemn imprecation. Oaths that call harm on someone if broken sit beside everyday cursing. The power is in the uttered words.
诅.history: Mainly literary or fixed expressions (诅咒) in modern Mandarin; less common as a free verb than 骂.

parts.note: simplified phonetic from 戠 in 識

识.origin: 识 simplifies 識: speech with a phonetic, meaning to know or recognize and to have learning. The zhì reading relates to remembering and insignia. Knowledge is framed as something one can name and mark in words.
识.history: Traditional 識; simplified 识. Extremely common in 认识, 知识, and 意识.

诈.origin: 诈 (traditional 詐) is speech plus 乍, meaning deceitful talk or fraud. Cheating, bluffing, and false pretenses all rely on misleading language. The character brands dishonesty as a verbal act.
诈.history: Used for deception in classical legal and military contexts; modern 诈骗 and 欺诈 are standard legal terms.

parts.note: simplified phonetic remnant of 睪 in 譯

译.origin: 译 simplifies 譯: the speech radical with phonetic 睪, meaning to render speech from one language into another. Translation and interpretation are transferring meaning through words.
译.history: Traditional 譯; simplified 译. Core of 翻译, 意译, and 口译.

试.origin: The character "试" combines the radical "讠" (yán, meaning "speech") on the left, indicating its relation to verbal or written tests, and "式" (shì, meaning "style" or "formula") on the right, which contributes to the phonetic component and suggests the idea of a standardized method or procedure. Trying something out and examining by a set pattern both fit this structure.
试.history: Traditional 試. Classical ‘test; attempt’ senses continue in 考试, 试验, and everyday 试一试.

诚.origin: 诚 (traditional 誠) is speech with 成, meaning words that are complete and genuine—sincerity. Honesty in speech becomes a moral virtue, and the adverb “indeed” follows from affirming truth. Confucian ethics made 诚 a key term.
诚.history: Central in classical philosophy; modern uses include 诚实, 诚恳, and 真诚.

parts.note: tongue; also strongly semantic for talk

话.origin: 话 (traditional 話) pairs the speech radical with 舌 “tongue,” a vivid cue for spoken language. It means words, talk, or a story told aloud. Everyday conversation vocabulary builds directly on this graphic.
话.history: Became the ordinary word for speech in vernacular Chinese (说话, 电话, 笑话).

诡.origin: 诡 (traditional 詭) joins speech with 危, suggesting dangerous or slippery talk—sophistry and deceit. Crafty arguments and oddly twisted situations both fall under this character.
诡.history: Literary word for cunning and paradox (诡计, 诡异); less colloquial than 骗.

询.origin: 询 (traditional 詢) is speech with phonetic 旬, meaning to ask for information or advice. Consulting experts and making inquiries are polite, purposeful questioning.
询.history: Common in formal and modern written Chinese (询问, 咨询).

parts.note: speech; what is said to be due

该.origin: 该 (traditional 該) combines speech with phonetic 亥. Early senses include “comprehensive” and “that (aforementioned),” then “to owe; to be someone’s turn,” and the modal “ought to.” Obligation is framed as what words assign as due.
该.history: Modal gāi is extremely frequent in modern Mandarin; older ‘aforementioned’ uses survive in written style.

详.origin: 详 (traditional 詳) is speech with 羊 as phonetic, meaning a full, careful account in words. Detail and thoroughness are the focus—saying enough to make things clear.
详.history: Standard in 详细, 详情, and classical commentary language.

诩.origin: 诩 (traditional 詡) pairs speech with 羽, meaning to talk oneself up—boastful praise. Spreading one’s reputation with fine words is the image. It remains a literary verb for bragging.
诩.history: Mostly written/literary today (自诩); not a high-frequency spoken word.

语.origin: 语 (traditional 語) is speech with phonetic 吾, meaning spoken language or to tell someone something. As a noun it covers language and utterances; as a verb it means to speak to. It is the standard morpheme in 语言 and 汉语.
语.history: Core literacy character from classical through modern Chinese; simplified 语 is ubiquitous.

误.meaning: mistake; to miss; to harm by error
误.origin: 误 (traditional 誤) joins speech with phonetic 吴/吳, meaning erroneous words or a mistaken action. Errors, delays caused by mistakes, and misleading someone all fall under this graph. Wrong speech stands for wrong outcomes.
误.history: Common in 错误, 误会, and 耽误 across classical and modern usage.

说.origin: 说 (traditional 說) is speech with 兑, originally linked to explaining and persuading as well as uttering words. The primary modern sense is “to say; to speak”; readings shuì (persuade) and yuè (pleased, later 悦) reflect older branches of the same graph.
说.history: One of the highest-frequency verbs in Mandarin; traditional 說 still used in some regions.

请.origin: 请 (traditional 請) combines speech with phonetic 青, meaning to ask politely or invite. Courtesy formulas (“please”) and requests for leave or advice grow from that deferential asking. Hospitality vocabulary (请客) is central.
请.history: Essential politeness verb from classical petitions to modern everyday 请问 and 邀请.

parts.note: simplified phonetic from 賣 in 讀

读.origin: 读 simplifies 讀: speech/words with a phonetic, originally reading texts aloud. Studying and silent reading developed from oral recitation of classics. The character ties literacy to voiced language.
读.history: Traditional 讀; simplified 读. Core of 读书, 阅读, and school vocabulary.

课.origin: 课 (traditional 課) is speech with 果, meaning an assigned portion of study or a taxed allotment. Lessons and classes are measured amounts of teaching; levying taxes is assigning a due amount.
课.history: School sense dominates modern Mandarin (上课, 功课); fiscal ‘levy’ survives in formal language.

谁.origin: 谁 (traditional 誰) pairs speech with phonetic 隹 to form the interrogative “who/whom.” It asks for the person involved in a statement. Mandarin readings shéi and shuí both occur; the hint prefers shéi and the gloss “whom.”
谁.history: Standard interrogative pronoun from classical into modern Chinese; simplified 谁 is universal in PRC texts.

调.origin: 调 (traditional 調) is speech with 周, covering harmonious arrangement of tones and words. As tiáo it means to adjust or mediate; as diào it means a tune, key, or transfer/assignment. Balancing sounds and assignments share a sense of regulated change.
调.history: Dual readings are ancient and still productive (调整, 调查, 声调, 调动).

谈.origin: 谈 (traditional 談) joins speech with 炎, meaning conversation or discussion. Talking things over—often at length—is the core. Informal chat and formal talks (会谈) both use this graph.
谈.history: Everyday verb in modern Chinese (谈话, 谈判); traditional 談 in other orthographies.

谋.origin: 谋 (traditional 謀) is speech with 某, meaning to plan or counsel in words. Strategy, plotting, and seeking a livelihood (谋生) all involve deliberate verbal planning.
谋.history: Key military and political term historically; still common in 谋划 and 阴谋.

谎.origin: 谎 (traditional 謊) pairs speech with 荒, meaning empty or wild talk—a lie. False statements are speech that has gone barren of truth. The noun “lie” and verb “to lie” both use this character.
谎.history: Standard modern word in 说谎 and 谎言; clearly pejorative.

谓.origin: 谓 (traditional 謂) is speech with phonetic 胃, meaning to call something by a name or to remark. “What is meant by…” (所谓) and classical narrative “X said” uses are central. It frames predication—saying what something is.
谓.history: More formal/literary than 说 in modern Mandarin, but fixed in 所谓, 称谓, and classical quotation patterns.

谢.meaning: to thank; to decline; to wither
谢.origin: 谢 (traditional 謝) combines speech with phonetic 射. Core senses include declining with words and expressing thanks; flowers “decline” as they wither (凋谢). Gratitude and polite refusal are both verbal responses.
谢.history: Surname and everyday 谢谢 dominate modern use; literary withering sense persists.

象.origin: 象 is a pictograph of an elephant, trunk and body visible in early forms. Because the elephant’s form is striking, the character also came to mean “image; appearance” and “to resemble.” Abstraction from the animal to representation is a classic semantic shift.
象.history: Oracle forms are clearly elephants; compounds like 想象 and 现象 keep the ‘image’ sense central today.

豪.origin: 豪 historically links a tall/phonetic element with 豕 (boar), suggesting a bristling, powerful creature and by extension a bold person. Grand style, heroism, and wealthy local strongmen (豪强) grow from that vigor. Luxury (豪华) is a later extension of impressiveness.
豪.history: Classical ‘outstanding person’ sense continues in 英雄豪杰 and modern 豪华, 自豪.

貌.origin: 貌 centers on the face and outward appearance (皃), often with a beast radical in the standard form. It means looks, facial expression, or seeming aspect. Judging by appearances and polite demeanor both use this character.
貌.history: Stable classical and modern word for appearance (面貌, 相貌).

責.origin: 責 (traditional form of simplified 责) shows cowrie shells (貝) with a phonetic, originally demanding payment of a debt. From financial claim came moral responsibility and verbal reproach. Being answerable for something is the unifying idea.
責.history: Remains the traditional orthography; mainland simplified 责 shares the same meanings (责任, 责备).

负.meaning: to bear; to carry; negative; to lose
负.origin: 负 (traditional 負) depicts a person bearing a load of valuables (貝), hence “to carry; to bear.” Bearing responsibility, owing (deficit), mathematical negative, and suffering defeat are extensions of carrying a burden or debt.
负.history: Ancient graph for carrying; modern senses include 负责, 负数, and 胜负.

财.origin: 财 (traditional 財) places the money radical 贝 with phonetic 才, naming wealth and property. Cowrie shells as currency make the semantic half transparent. Talent (才) as phonetic also invites folk links between ability and riches.
财.history: Core economic morpheme in 财富, 财产, and 财政 from classical times to today.

败.origin: 败 (traditional 敗) shows cowries being struck (攵), the ruin of wealth—or a beating that brings defeat. Losing a battle, spoiling food, and moral failure all extend from destruction.
败.history: Standard for military and competitive loss (失败, 打败) throughout Chinese history.

质.origin: 质 simplifies 質: an axe-related element with cowries, linked to pawning valuables and to the substance of a thing. Intrinsic nature, material quality, and posing tough questions (质疑) share the idea of getting at what something really is or is worth.
质.history: Philosophical and commercial senses both ancient; modern 质量 and 本质 are central.

贫.origin: 贫 (traditional 貧) combines 分 “divide” with 贝 “money”: wealth split until little remains—poverty. The ideographic reading is unusually clear. Scarcity of resources is the literal and figurative core.
贫.history: Long used for economic poverty and sparse style (贫乏); still basic in modern Chinese.

parts.note: simplified phonetic from 冓 in 購

购.origin: 购 simplifies 購: the money radical with a phonetic, meaning to buy with payment. Acquisition through commerce is the sole core sense. Modern consumer vocabulary builds heavily on this character.
购.history: Traditional 購; simplified 购 is standard in 购买, 购物, and 采购.

贱.origin: 贱 (traditional 賤) puts money with a ‘small’ phonetic, meaning low in price or low in social worth. Cheap goods and contempt for people share the idea of being valued little.
贱.history: Price and status senses both classical; modern Mandarin still uses 贱 for cheap/despise (贵贱, 贱卖).

贴.meaning: to stick; to paste; to subsidize; close to
贴.origin: 贴 (traditional 貼) is cowrie/money with phonetic 占. Early senses include attaching a note or covering a shortfall with money; sticking paper to a surface and standing close (“next to”) are related attachments. Subsidies (补贴) keep the monetary link.
贴.history: Everyday verb for pasting and being close (贴近); financial ‘cover a cost’ sense remains common.

费.origin: 费 (traditional 費) places phonetic 弗 above the money radical, meaning expenditure of wealth. Fees, costs, and wasting resources all involve money going out. Effort can be “spent” metaphorically the same way.
费.history: Standard for expenses from classical accounts to modern 费用, 浪费, and 学费.

parts.note: weapon/army; contributes to the sense of armed plunder

贼.origin: 贼 is a phono-semantic compound linking 贝 (cowrie shell, a classical symbol of money and valuables) with 戎 (weapons/military). The combination evokes taking wealth by force — a robber or bandit. Early forms emphasize plunder rather than petty theft.
贼.history: In classical Chinese 贼 covered bandits, rebels, and traitors; modern usage centers on thieves and villains. The simplified form keeps the 贝 radical of the traditional 賊.

资.origin: 资 pairs the money radical 贝 with phonetic 次. It originally pointed to goods, provisions, and means of support, then broadened to capital, qualifications, and resources. The idea is wealth that enables action.
资.history: Traditional 資 appears widely in texts on taxation and logistics. Simplified 资 remains central in words like 资本 and 资格.

parts.note: simplified from 賣; marks the sound and sense of exchange

赎.origin: 赎 shows payment (贝) used to buy someone or something back from captivity, debt, or penalty. The right side relates to selling/exchange in the traditional form 贖, reinforcing a commercial recovery of what was lost.
赎.history: Classical law and ritual used 赎 for monetary atonement. Modern Chinese keeps the sense of redeeming hostages, mortgaged goods, or sins metaphorically.

parts.note: provides sound shǎng and a sense of esteem

赏.origin: 赏 combines 贝 with phonetic 尚. Bestowing valuables for merit is the core image; from rewarding heroes it extended to admiring scenery, art, or performance (欣赏).
赏.history: Traditional 賞 is common in imperial edicts and poetry. Simplified 赏 preserves the same structure and senses.

赚.origin: The character 赚 consists of the radical 贝 (shell, representing money or wealth) on the left, and 兼 (double or simultaneous) on the right as the phonetic. Together they convey gaining or earning wealth, often with a sense of profit stacked on outlay.
赚.history: 赚 is comparatively modern in everyday speech for making money; classical texts more often used 利 or 赢 for gain. The traditional form is 賺.

parts.note: compressed form related to 塞; marks sound sài

赛.origin: 赛 originally involved presenting offerings (贝) in competitive or festival settings, then generalized to contests and races. The upper components historically relate to blocking/filling (塞), supplying the sound.
赛.history: Traditional 賽 appears in temple-fair and athletic contexts. Modern 赛 covers sports matches, competitions, and figurative rivalries.

parts.note: two 先-like figures approaching with gifts

赞.origin: 赞 depicts people presenting valuables (贝), conveying commendation and assistance. Praise and endorsement grow from the act of formally supporting someone with gifts or words.
赞.history: Traditional 贊 and variant 讚 (with 言) split senses of assist versus eulogize; simplified 赞 covers both praise and support (赞成).

parts.note: complex phonetic (often analyzed via 羸-related forms)

赢.origin: 赢 is a complex character whose core semantic cue is 贝 (gain, profit). It means to win a contest or make a surplus. Folk analysis often breaks it into many parts, but historically it is a phono-semantic graph for profit and victory.
赢.history: Traditional 贏 is frequent in commerce and games. Simplified 赢 keeps the many-stroke silhouette while remaining a high-frequency word for winning.

parts.note: person with reddish/flushed body or fire-like color

赤.origin: 赤 began as a depiction associated with a reddish human figure or the color of bare flesh and firelight. It denotes the color red and, by extension, nakedness (赤裸) and unadorned sincerity (赤诚).
赤.history: In early texts 赤 is a basic color term beside 朱 and 丹. It remains productive in compounds for bare, loyal, and scarlet.

走.origin: 走 shows a person in motion above a foot (止), originally meaning to run, later softening to walk or go. It also developed senses of leaving and following a course.
走.history: Oracle and bronze forms emphasize haste; modern Mandarin 走 is the everyday verb for walking and going, while 跑 took over fast running.

赶.meaning: to catch up; to drive; to rush
赶.origin: 赶 places phonetic 干 beside 走, capturing chasing after someone, herding animals, or rushing to meet a deadline. Motion toward a target is the shared thread.
赶.history: Traditional 趕 uses 旱 as phonetic. Simplified 赶 with 干 is standard in mainland usage for catching up and driving off.

起.origin: 起 combines walking/motion 走 with phonetic 己. It images rising to one’s feet and beginning an action, hence start, arise, and the preposition “from” (从…起).
起.history: The character is ancient and extremely productive: 起来, 开始, 起飞. Form has stayed close to the seal-script layout.

超.origin: 超 uses 走 for overtaking on foot and 召 for sound. Literally to leap or run beyond others, it extended to surpassing standards and the prefix “super-.”
超.history: Classical Chinese used 超 for outstanding ability. Modern compounds like 超过 and 超级 keep the sense of going beyond.

越.meaning: to exceed; to cross; the more…
越.origin: 越 consists of radical 走 (to walk) and 戉 (an ancient weapon) as phonetic on the right. It suggests moving beyond or surpassing a limit — crossing borders, overstepping, or intensifying (越来越).
越.history: Historically 越 also named peoples and states south of the Yangtze. The motion sense remains primary in modern Mandarin.

趣.origin: 趣 pairs 走 with phonetic 取. Originally it meant to hasten toward a goal; later it shifted to inclination, interest, and delight — what one runs after.
趣.history: In literature 趣 marks lively charm (趣味). Modern speech uses it heavily for fun and hobbies.

足.origin: 足 depicts the leg and foot. From the bodily part it extended metaphorically to sufficiency — having “enough footing” — yielding the common adjective sense “enough.”
足.history: As a radical (⻊), 足 heads many characters about stepping and roads. Both anatomical and “enough” readings are ancient.

跑.origin: 跑 is a straightforward foot-radical compound with phonetic 包. It denotes running, fleeing, or functioning (机器跑), all grounded in rapid leg motion.
跑.history: Compared with older 走 (once “run”), 跑 became the unmarked Mandarin verb for running in modern vernacular.

距.origin: 距 links foot/step with phonetic 巨. It first referred to a cock’s spur, then to measured separation — the distance one must span.
距.history: Technical and everyday Chinese use 距离 for spatial or temporal gaps; classical texts also use 距 as a verb “to be distant from.”

parts.note: foot — heel and following on foot

跟.origin: 跟 combines radical 足 (foot) with 艮 on the right. It points to the heel and to following or staying with someone — walking at another’s heels — which yields the colloquial preposition “with.”
跟.history: Anatomical 脚跟 and verb 跟随 are older; the preposition 跟 is especially common in northern Mandarin speech.

跤.origin: 跤 uses 足 plus phonetic 交. It means a fall or stumble, most familiar in 摔跤 (wrestling) — bodies and feet interlocking until someone goes down.
跤.history: The character is specialized; outside 摔跤 and dialectal “fall,” learners meet it mainly in sports vocabulary.

跨.origin: 跨 shows a foot taking a wide step over an obstacle, with 夸 supplying the sound. It covers straddling, spanning regions, and cross-border activities.
跨.history: From physical straddling it extended to figurative spans (跨国, 跨学科). Form is stable from seal script onward.

parts.note: each; phonetic related to path traversed

路.origin: 路 combines radical 足 with 各 on the right, suggesting a route traversed on foot. It is the basic word for road and extends to methods and career paths.
路.history: Extremely frequent since antiquity in place names and metaphors (道路, 思路). Traditional and simplified forms match.

跳.origin: 跳 is foot plus phonetic 兆. It means leaping upward or forward, and by extension skipping items or a pulse jumping (心跳).
跳.history: Used from early vernacular into modern sports and daily speech; the graph itself is a classic phono-semantic design.

踏.meaning: to step on; to tread; on-the-spot
踏.origin: 踏 pairs 足 with phonetic 沓. Stamping or treading underfoot is the core sense, later yielding survey verbs (踏访) and set phrases (脚踏实地).
踏.history: Literary and modern Chinese both use 踏 for firm footing and exploratory visits (踏青).

踪.origin: 踪 marks footprints and trails with 足 and phonetic 宗. Following someone’s 踪迹 means tracking where their feet have been.
踪.history: Traditional 蹤 is common in detective and travel writing; simplified 踪 is standard in mainland texts.

踮.origin: 踮 uses 足 with phonetic 店 for rising onto the balls of the feet, often to see farther or reach higher. It is a vivid everyday motion verb.
踮.history: Mostly modern vernacular; appears in descriptions of peeking, dancing, or careful stepping.

蹈.meaning: to tread; to dance; to follow
蹈.origin: 蹈 is foot plus phonetic 舀. It means treading, dancing (舞蹈), or acting out a path — including following a bad example (重蹈覆辙).
蹈.history: Classical prose and poetry use 蹈 for ritual stepping; modern compounds keep both dance and figurative following.

蹦.meaning: to leap; to bounce; to hop
蹦.origin: 蹦 combines 足 with phonetic 崩. It depicts sudden hopping or bouncing, often lively or abrupt compared with 跳.
蹦.history: Colloquial Mandarin favors 蹦 in onomatopoeic and playful contexts (蹦蹦跳跳, 蹦迪).

parts.note: foot — fidgeting, unable to stay still

躁.origin: 躁 links restless feet with phonetic 喿 (clamor). Physical fidgeting becomes emotional impatience and irritability (急躁, 烦躁).
躁.history: Medical and moral texts long warned against 躁 as agitation; the character remains common in psychological descriptions.

parts.note: side view of a human torso, often pregnant

身.origin: 身 is a side-view pictograph of the human body, sometimes emphasizing a pregnant belly. It means the physical body, one’s person, and by extension social status or lifetime.
身.history: As both independent word and radical, 身 underlies verbs of posture and concealment. Forms are recognizable from oracle bone script.

躲.origin: 躲 includes radical 身 (body) and phonetic 朵. It suggests moving the body into a hidden position, symbolizing hiding or dodging something.
躲.history: Common in spoken Mandarin for taking cover or avoiding people (躲避, 躲开). The graph is a clear modern phono-semantic compound.

parts.note: carriage viewed from above: axle and two wheels

车.origin: 车 (traditional 車) pictures a chariot or cart from above, with a central axle and wheels. It is the basic graph for wheeled vehicles and a major radical.
车.history: From war chariots to trains and cars, 车 stayed productive. Simplification reduced 車 to 车 while keeping the iconic axle idea.

parts.note: nine; phonetic jiǔ/guǐ in this series

轨.origin: 轨 uses 车 with phonetic 九 for the ruts left by wheels. From wheel grooves it extended to rails, orbits, and abstract norms such as 正轨. The shared idea is a fixed path that guides motion.
轨.history: Traditional 軌 appears in political metaphors about staying on track; railways made the concrete sense newly vivid.

转.meaning: to turn; to transfer; to revolve
转.origin: 转 depicts wheeled turning. Readings split: zhuǎn for changing direction or transferring, zhuàn for revolving. Both grow from rotary motion of carts.
转.history: Traditional 轉 is ubiquitous in classical prose. Simplified 转 keeps both tonal readings in modern Mandarin.

轮.origin: 轮 is the wheel of a cart (车) with phonetic 仑. From the rotating rim came taking turns (轮流) and steamships (轮船).
轮.history: Traditional 輪 is standard in mechanical and literary Chinese; simplification replaces 侖 with 仑.

parts.note: yawn/lack; phonetic related to softness series

软.origin: 软 pairs 车 with 欠. Early senses include pliant carriage parts; the dominant meaning became soft, gentle, or weak (软弱).
软.history: Traditional 軟 is common in medicine and materials talk. Soft vs. hard (硬) remains a basic contrast in modern Chinese.

parts.note: vehicle — light carriage vs. heavy load

轻.origin: 轻 contrasts light carts with heavy ones. From low weight it extended to mild actions, youthful inexperience, and treating matters lightly.
轻.history: Traditional 輕 is frequent in philosophy (轻重). Simplified 轻 is a core adjective in everyday Mandarin.

载.meaning: to carry; to load; year; to record

parts.note: phonetic/zi component shared with 裁/栽

载.origin: 载 shows a cart bearing a load. Readings: zài for carrying or filling, zǎi for recording and “year.” Writing things down is imagined as loading them onto the record.
载.history: Traditional 載 appears in chronicles (记载) and transport texts. Both tones remain active in modern Chinese.

较.origin: 较 originally concerned comparing chariot parts or racing side by side. It generalized to comparison and the adverb “relatively.”
较.history: Traditional 較 is standard in argumentation (比较). Simplified 较 is among the most common grammaticalized verbs.

parts.note: carts in a file — ordered ranks

辈.origin: 辈 images carts lined up, suggesting ranks of people. It means a generation or cohort (前辈, 同辈) and can mark lifetime experience.
辈.history: Traditional 輩 is common in kinship and social hierarchy talk; simplified 辈 keeps the same uses.

辑.origin: 辑 uses 车 with phonetic 咠 for gathering and arranging. It originally suggested coordinating chariot gear or assembled parts. Later the meaning centered on compiling and editing texts into volumes (编辑).
辑.history: Publishing vocabulary (专辑, 辑录) made 辑 especially familiar in modern Chinese. Traditional form is 輯.

输.meaning: to transport; to lose; to donate
输.origin: 输 begins as conveying goods by cart. From sending things out came losing in a game (outputting the stake) and contributing funds.
输.history: Traditional 輸 spans logistics and gambling losses. Simplified 输 is core vocabulary for transport and defeat.

辣.origin: 辣 builds on 辛 (pungency) with a phonetic related to 剌. It names the burning spice taste and, figuratively, a sharp or ruthless manner.
辣.history: Chili peppers popularized culinary 辣 in later centuries; the graph also colors personality descriptions (泼辣).

parts.note: two 辛 — arguing sides to be sorted

辨.origin: 辨 shows disputed pungent/legal sides (辛) cut apart with a knife, meaning to distinguish true from false. It is closely related to 辩 (argue) and 辫 (braid).
辨.history: Classical philosophy prizes 辨 for careful discrimination. The form is essentially the same in traditional and simplified scripts.

辱.origin: 辱 is traditionally explained as soiling or mishandling something associated with 辰, yielding disgrace. The modern sense is humiliation and insult (侮辱, 耻辱).
辱.history: Moral and diplomatic texts treat 辱 as a grave social wound. The character’s form has been stable for millennia.

parts.note: simplified remnant of fuller phonetic 臱

边.origin: 边 (traditional 邊) marks edges and frontiers, with the walk radical suggesting traversing a rim. It covers sides of objects, borders of states, and simultaneous “while” (一边…一边…).
边.history: Simplification drastically reduced 邊 to 边. Both spatial and grammatical uses are extremely frequent.

达.origin: 达 means walking until one arrives. From physical arrival it extended to attaining goals and to persons of high standing (达人).
达.history: Traditional 達 used 羍 as phonetic; simplified 达 with 大 is ubiquitous in 到达 and 表达.

迅.origin: 迅 pairs the motion radical with phonetic 卂 to mean very fast movement. It colors weather, response time, and military advance.
迅.history: Literary Chinese favors 迅 for sudden speed (迅速, 迅雷). Form is unchanged by simplification.

过.meaning: to pass; to cross; to spend (time); too
过.origin: 过 is walking past a point. It covers crossing, spending time, faults (过错), and the aspectual particle for completed experience. Hints stress passing and spending time.
过.history: Traditional 過 remains in Taiwan/HK print. Simplified 过 is among Mandarin’s highest-frequency verbs and grammatical words.

迎.meaning: to welcome; to meet; to face
